item.title: [Business of Sport] The Rise of Sinner | Why Britain’s £300M Tennis System Isn’t Producing Champions
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、辛纳的"教练组合投资学"：把自己当作一支基金来运营

大多数人看到辛纳频繁更换教练团队，第一反应是"这人不好相处"或"太苛刻"。但仔细看他的换人逻辑，会发现一个惊人的模式——他不是在换人，他是在做资产配置。

Piatti 带他从少年到职业球员，功能兑现后被替换成 Cahill（因为 Cahill 有培养大满贯冠军的经验）。体能教练因为在多巴胺失利事件中"未尽把关之责"被开除，但服完禁赛期后又被请回——因为他技术层面依然是最优解。这是纯粹的功能主义人力资本管理，情感权重接近于零。

跨领域看，这更像顶级对冲基金经理管理团队的方式，而不像传统体育明星和团队的关系。有意思的对比是拉杜卡努——她换教练是"混乱型换人"（emotional churn），辛纳是"结构型换人"（portfolio rebalancing）。这两种"换人"在表面统计上看起来一样，但底层完全是两种物种。

二、"大满贯东道主诅咒"：拿到 Wimbledon 反而毁了英国网球

这是整段访谈里最反直觉的洞察，但主持人只是掠过没有点破：举办大满贯的四个国家，在过去 20 年男子网坛几乎零冠军产出（澳洲男子上一个冠军在 2002 年，法国 1983 年，美国 2003 年，英国只有一个 Murray 且完全体制外培养）。

这在经济学里有个名字，叫"资源诅咒"（Resource Curse）——就像石油富国往往发展不出制造业。LTA 每年从温网拿到 4800 万英镑，这笔"外生现金流"让整个体系丧失了寻找真正稀缺资源（教练、俱乐部生态）的动机，转而把钱砸向可衡量、可宣传的东西：公园里的球场数、参与人数、职业赛事场次。

意大利没有大满贯，反而被迫用"内生方式"建设——4500 家俱乐部对英国 2000 家。俱乐部意味着教练网络，教练网络意味着人才涌现的毛细血管。这是典型的匮乏催生结构，丰饶催生虚胖。

三、Draper 和 Raducanu 的"伤病同频"：温室化育才的生物学代价

这段访谈里最容易被忽略但最重的一击是：LTA 体系培养出的两位当红球员，都在生涯早期反复受伤。主持人只是提出疑问，但没有深挖。

我认为这不是巧合，这是过度专业化+过早高强度训练的必然产物。对比辛纳——他在阿尔卑斯山滑雪长大，直到十几岁才转向单一项目。运动科学近十年有大量研究表明：多项目童年训练培养出的运动员，成年后受伤率显著更低，运动寿命更长。

这跟当代教育的一个核心悖论完全同构：你越早"集中投入"，长期回报越差。硅谷创业圈也在验证同一件事——早期就"all-in"某个方向的创始人，往往在 5-10 年后崩盘；而绕过弯路的"晚成者"反而走得更远。

LTA 的模式本质上是"工业化温室"（hothousing 这个词主持人用得很精准），而意大利的俱乐部体系是"分布式生态"。前者产出脆弱的高纯度晶体，后者产出可以承压的复合材料。

四、一个更深的隐藏结构：英国正在用"网球"的钱补贴"高尔夫"的模式

最后一个 aha：主持人无意中点破——英国高尔夫强，因为高尔夫的物理形态强迫你必须走"俱乐部+驻会职业教练"模式（你不可能在公园里开一个高尔夫球场）。

这意味着：当一项运动的基础设施必须"重资产化"时，它自动过滤掉了公共参与幻觉，只能走精英培养路径。网球是"轻资产化"运动，因此可以逃避这个约束——而 LTA 恰恰选择了"逃避"这条路。

换句话说，英国网球体系的失败，不是执行问题，是它选择了运动本身允许它偷懒的那一面。这在企业战略里叫"路径依赖的舒适陷阱"。

📝 转录文本

Opening: A New Sinner Fan

I think you may um be a fan of someone after some pretty exciting tennis at the weekend.

Yeah. Well, so I sort of feel guilty in retrospect when we spent part of the first part of this year sort of looking a bit sort of worried about tennis and saying, you know, if SA becomes the next big thing, he's not the most charismatic guy around. Um and and you know maybe there's an element of truth to that, but I watched the Wimbledon final um from start to finish — and that was Yanick SA against um Alexander or Sasha Zerev um the recent French Open champion — and first of all I really enjoyed the match, and secondly I became a bit of a Yanick SA fan. I think that he's the real deal, isn't he?

He's the real deal.

He's the real deal. The guy has got a lot going on, right? He is a brilliant athlete. You're watching literally an — it's an on camera transformation here.

Yeah, he's a brilliant athlete. He's an amazing competitor, and he has that star quality of being able to pull out his very very best just when it's really really needed. And I just thought it was absolutely compelling watching — it was a champion, true champion's performance, cuz ZV brought his A-game.

Sinner's Background: Skiing, German, and Piatti

And it inspired me, Charlie, um my new found fandom for Yanick S — um it inspired me to look a little bit more deeply into the guy, um because he does seem like a sort of mild-mannered kind of guy, you know, good guy, nice guy, etc. Um, but uh it it's not quite as simple as that. And this is a guy who is phenomenally driven.

The extent of my lack of curiosity about Yanik extends even to the fact that, ve at various times in the last two years since he became prominent, I thought bit odd for an Italian to have a German name. So I mentioned this [laughter] I mentioned this to my wife who is German, and she and she sort of clouted me around the head affectionately and said, "You do know that there's part of Italy which is on the Austrian border where they where they speak German." And I looked it up and found that Yanik in his first language is German, and he comes from a ski village um on the Austrian border and grew up skiing — not ski jumping, very just in case you get excited about his recruitment prospects for [laughter] for naturalizing him as a Norwegian ski jumper, Charlie. Um a downhill skier.

Um and uh but clearly a young player of immense promise on the tennis side. He was taken in by a very prominent Italian tennis coach um as a teenager um —

Ricardo Piatti —

who coached him through from that young age through to being a top tennis player.

The Systematic Firing of Coaches

But since then, and as I relate in in my memo for those Substack members this week, he's been quite a hard taskmaster. You know, he fired Piatti despite all the success he's had with him um and replaced him with Darren Cahill, who's an Australian who had been coached — coached multiple Grand Slam champions. So when um in other words, when SA decided "I need to make my next — I need to get to the next level. Thanks a lot, Patty. Nice to see you. I'm now going to switch to Darren Cahill because he coaches Grand Slam champions."

He had a physical coach — bulleted him. People may remember there was a controversy over Rafel dope test, and SA fired his his fitness coach for holding him accountable for that.

Quite a serious controversy. Yeah.

Serious controversy. He then um had two new um fitness coaches, bulleted them because one of them he thought spoke a little bit too much in in the media about things and this type of stuff. Didn't want that, etc.

So, this is a guy — he's not going through coaches like Emma Radukanu does in a sort of sort of hell sort of sort of rather odd way. In a very systematic way, he is making sure that every single person who works for him is utterly dedicated to turning him into one of the all-time greats.

Well, yeah, Charlie, I actually say the opposite because Darren Cahill was meant to leave him last year and he managed to persuade him to stay.

Yeah.

So, I think what he — what he's incredibly good at doing is understanding exactly who he needs in his box.

Yeah.

And you look up at that box and you see those people and you can — you just know that every single one — Yeah. It's — Yeah, it it's in it, right?

Um and well, interestingly, the guy that he fired, Ferrer, for the — uh who was supposedly responsible or partly responsible for his failed test — was Claustable, it's very very specifically banned by Wada, and any physio really should know that. Even though, you know, he's fired him, he he's actually brought him back because he's after — after he's obviously served his ban.

Yeah. Which didn't involve him missing any grand slams and confused a lot of — cause a lot of confusion around the tennis world. Anyway, that's a — that's a deeper hole to go into.

Italy's Tennis Miracle

Anyway, the point is: this is a guy that in weeks where we've been talking about what it is to be professional and what it is to be absolutely elite at the top of your sport, this is a guy that does it. And actually, where I've been most interested in tennis over the last few years from a national perspective, which leads us into another part of this chat, Charlie, is how good Italy have been over the last few years at developing.

They've got four top 20 men's players. Um which in a truly global sport is a really significant achievement.

You got S, Kaboli.

Yeah. Berrettini.

And there is another one. Um —

Arnaldi also, didn't he — got to the semis of French. Yeah.

And then you've got Jasmine Paolini on the women's side.

Yeah.

So this is a system which is working.

The Grand Slam Windfall — and Does It Work?

Um now we've spoken in recent weeks in the whole debate over um pay at the Grand Slams — how c- certain of the top players are wingy about how much they get paid by the grand slams and went on a rather pathetic sort of not-even-a-media-strike, a sort of media downscaling of their media commitments. Um —

It was nothing. No one noticed anything.

Yeah, forget it. Um so um well, but what one thing we have noted is is that those um associations that host the grand slams — which is Tennis Australia, the French Tennis Federation, the Lawn Tennis Association that's here in England, and the US Tennis Association — so those tennis associations that are lucky enough to host a grand slam hand a very significant amount of money — each of them — to the elite tennis development schemes of their country, right?

So, it's a real windfall for those countries who who happen to be lucky enough to host one of the Grand Slams. It means that their elite systems get vast amounts of money chucked at them basically to subsidize the development of elite players.

Does it work?

Well, based on the data, you'd have to say it doesn't work.

It doesn't work. Charlie, when we — we were just chatting about this, I was thinking, hang on a second —

Because just to be clear, I've been —

Well, we we've certainly had our views on on that pay discussion, and and one of it being, you know, very much so — if you take more money for the players, then you're taking it away from the places that actually go and develop players. But then actually, like if you put the counter to that, you go, well, yeah, develop players in a very specific region, actually in the richest countries because they're getting all of this revenue anyway. So, what I would naturally want to see from that is: if that money is going somewhere, I want to see some results.

The Grim Statistics

Yeah. And if you look across — we we did a, you know, we had a look at those uh four countries and what — how many real champions they produced. The the statistics are quite frightening.

So the UK, let's call it the UK, you know, has had Murray and Radicanu in the last 20 years. France — could you could you name how many Grand Slam champions France has had in the last 20 years?

Um I don't think there's been one.

Emily Mauresmo — 2000 and — that is 2006 she did it, so I'm thinking, you know —

And that was Wimbledon, so it's actually —

Australian Open — she won two.

Actually more than 20 years ago.

Australia — Sam Stosur and Ash Barty.

US — obviously both Williams, Serena and Venus Williams, Sloan Stephens, Sofia Kenin, Coco Gauff, Madison Keys.

So a couple of things stand out there: no men — no man from Australia since Hewitt in 2002, no man from France since Yannick Noah in 1983, no man from the US since Andy Roddick in 2003. So Andy Murray is the only man from the Grand Slam countries to have won in the last 20 years.

Andy Murray Was Not an LTA Product

And guess what? What do we all know about Andy Murray and his training?

He had absolutely nothing to do with the Lawn Tennis Association. His mother didn't believe in the Lawn Tennis Association, and his family, who were reasonably well-to-do, sent him to the um Sanchez-Casal um academy in Barcelona at a very young age. As also happened to Johanna Konta, who's one of the very few British women to have um reached an elite level in in in the last 20 years.

And if you look back at other great British players — Tim Henman grew up in a tennis middle-upper-middle-class tennis family and was sent to a specialist private tennis school called Reed's near Cobham. Again, not in the LTA set — that that was run by, I think, by John Lloyd or David Lloyd. Um Greg Rusedski grew up in Canada and, you know, transferred to England once he was already a professional tennis player. Then you've got um Cameron Norrie, who is one of the other players who's reached the top 10 in recent years, who grew up in New Zealand and then went to to tennis — um played tennis in in the US college system.

So — you where you get to amongst — even forgetting the Grand Slam champions, even talking about British players who have really made anything of themselves, I've come across two: Kyle Edmund and um Jack Draper, who have what you might call been LTA kids. Now Jack — um Jack Draper, it should be noted, is the son of the former chief executive of the LTA, Roger Draper. Um, so it's, you know, again, from a very upper-middle-class background, it's slightly hard to imagine that he wouldn't have had the resources to make it even if the LTA had not eased his way.

So, we're really looking at Kyle Edmund being the only example of a successful British male tennis player — all female really, I think, maybe other than Radukanu — who has been helped through the system by the LTA.

The £300 Million Question

Now, the LTA spends 15 million pounds a year on their youth program. Sorry, by their youth program, I mean their elite youth program. So that's on these kids, right? 15 million pounds a year. And then on top of that, they spend another 35 million on putting on professional events to — with with the express purpose of helping these elite players have events to play in so they can climb the ladder.

So in that time — 15 million at the elite — 20 years — criky. So 300 million, let's say, has developed —

No one. Well —

Radicanu maybe to — to win the top tournament. Because we can't — because we can't call Murray, as you said, coming — you can't say that that money has gone to him.

So suddenly you're looking at that going, wow, okay, well — where's that going?

It's — it's a quite extraordinary allocation of capital.

The Italian Comparison: Clubs, Not Courts

Now, if I — I sort of dug into it a bit looking at the Italian system, right? Because actually the Italian tennis federation, despite not having a grand slam, still generates about the same amount of money as the LTA. And I wondered why that was.

So, I looked into it and I found that we've got 2,000 tennis clubs in this country. In Britain, in Italy, they have 4½ thousand. Now, if you want to try and work out why Italy is producing so many top players — it's a country of almost identical size to the UK — and it has more than twice as many tennis clubs.

Clubs. Interesting.

Now, clubs is important. I should tell why clubs — because it's also important in golf as well. So, the LTA puts a huge amount of money into tennis courts, right? That includes many, many, many thousands of courts in public parks, right? So, this is a court where if Charlie and Charlie want to go and have a game of tennis, we can go and play tennis for either free or or for not very much money.

But that isn't going to develop an elite player. Develop an elite player, you have to have a coach. And the coach ain't going to be in the — in the course in the park. The the coach is going to be at a club. And this is, I think, where the problem lies and why — why Italy are doing so well is they've got many more coaches.

The LTA's Defense: Participation

So that will be it. Because my counter to any critique that it was leveling was going to be: the real mandate of the LTA is to get participation up in this country. Last year, I think you had 5.8 million adults playing in the UK. You had 4 million children, 3,000 park courts renovated is your point — judged on those, that was the most successful year.

So for the money that — let's say — I think participation had — I had here 26 million which is court funding versus schools competition. That's doing great.

That's doing really well.

That's doing really well.

And therefore, you know, Grand Slam Champions we all want, but the most important part of their allocation is to get people playing the game. Okay, they're doing well.

That doesn't mean they get a let-off for spending 156 — sorry, £50 million pounds a year on an elite division that doesn't throw out anything. And, you know, cumulatively over the 20 years, if I was just going through it — and that, you know, worked out that basically 2025 they got 48 million from Wimbledon which was about 90% of Wimbledon's profits, as we know previously. So, you know, just cumulatively over the years, the account comes out — let's say they've received about 700 million pounds over the 20 years.

Yeah.

From Wimbledon.

Of which they're spending about half on the professional game — the elite game.

Yeah. Right.

Yeah. A third. Yeah. Because 16 of — let's say — no. Because if they're paying £35 million on professional events and 15 million on the on the elite program.

Oh, you're saying professional events. I'm saying professional events is a — I wouldn't — I wouldn't class that as a contributor to us developing Grand Slam champions.

That's how they see it. That's how — that's why the LTA do it. So that's their excuse for spending a lot of money on elite events is to help develop elite players.

Wow.

In in this country, because that's what gives them the ability to give them wild cards and to get more British players playing. And —

That's just like — it just gives — it's just cannon fodder to some of the [laughter] other — Arthur Fery.

Yeah. [clears throat] New exception to the rule, Charlie. [laughter] No. How — how many wild cards were there across —

Yeah. Yeah. I know, indeed.

The Italian Control Study

So, so anyway, but I thought it was interesting just looking — looking at the Italians um and saying it's a really good control study where you've got a — a country which is the same size in Europe, both developed tennis economies. Um both of them would have aspirations to producing elite players. Both of the national federations are making kind of quite a bit of money, can afford to do things properly.

And the Italian system is pushing money into clubs and keeping the club system alive and kicking. And the English system is not pushing money into clubs. The English system is pushing money into, on the one hand, public participation courts; on the on the other hand, what you might call hothousing.

The Injury Question

Now there's one other bit I just want to just raise, and this may be circumstantial, but the top two current players who have come through that system are Jack Draper and Emma Raticanu. What is the thing that is regarded as holding both of their careers back?

Injuries.

Terrible injuries. I mean, like catastrophic series of injuries as quite young players. We're not talking about players in their mid-30s. We're talking about players in their early to mid-20s. Injury after injury after injury. I [clears throat] wonder if there might be some attention which the LTA needs to pay to their performance program.

Interesting point. Yeah.

Um because it seems a bit too much of a coincidence to have your two main stars that you spent so much money on unable, basically, to get themselves on the court in one piece — hobbling around on crutches and casts all the time.

Well, they should be in the best years of their career. Well, coming into the best years of their career.

Absolutely. Yeah.

The Golf Contrast

Um so look, it's an interesting thing. And if you contrast it with golf, where Britain over-indexes and does exceptionally well, what do we have many more of than other —

Golf clubs?

Golf clubs. Yeah, golf clubs with qualified PGA professionals who will pick up a young player, teach them the basics, get them up to a certain level.

Now, you're kind of limited, aren't you? Because golf, you can't have a park court in the same way that you can have a — golf course generally has to be a golf club. So, so the system, you're almost forced into that model without having to make a decision.

That that is true. So, yes, we have both many more golf courses and many more golf clubs, cuz in golf the two things are basically the same. Um but I would probably again think about — and do you remember one of the most interesting things you said about Norway and football last week was how many coaches they have.

Yeah. The sheer number of qualified coach — 300 — sorry, there was one coach every 325 people.

I'm going to come back to what the number of qualified golf coaches is in Britain. I'm going to guess that it's pretty much the highest per head of population of anywhere. I mean certainly in Scotland — I mean it's quite extreme, that — I mean Scotland I think have got some sort of golf pro factory somewhere and they roll them out wherever you go in the world. If you go and play golf at some obscure place in Thailand, it'll say um "PJ golf professional Hamish Matagot" — it's quite extraordinary, they get everywhere. So anyway, we'll come back to it a — non —

Closing: Prize Money Position Unchanged

Listeners, I'm just saying — although we're criticizing, I still am not convinced that the players deserve any more money than they've been getting at the moment. The tennis players —

Oh right, yeah, no, absolutely not.

Even — even if we're questioning whether that distribution of cash. I still think a 19 or 20% increase on your Wimbledon prize money was — was pretty good. So, yeah, we're not changing — I'm not changing my position on that. But I do think having a look at the allocation of that, and actually then holding to a standard of — um of success — is is important.

item.description:
## 一、辛纳的"教练组合投资学"：把自己当作一支基金来运营
大多数人看到辛纳频繁更换教练团队，第一反应是"这人不好相处"或"太苛刻"。但仔细看他的换人逻辑，会发现一个惊人的模式——**他不是在换人，他是在做资产配置**。
Piatti 带他从少年到职业球员，功能兑现后被替换成 Cahill（因为 Cahill 有培养大满贯冠军的经验）。体能教练因为在多巴胺失利事件中"未尽把关之责"被开除，但服完禁赛期后又被请回——因为他技术层面依然是最优解。这是纯粹的**功能主义人力资本管理**，情感权重接近于零。
跨领域看，这更像顶级对冲基金经理管理团队的方式，而不像传统体育明星和团队的关系。有意思的对比是拉杜卡努——她换教练是"混乱型换人"（emotional churn），辛纳是"结构型换人"（portfolio rebalancing）。这两种"换人"在表面统计上看起来一样，但底层完全是两种物种。
## 二、"大满贯东道主诅咒"：拿到 Wimbledon 反而毁了英国网球
这是整段访谈里最反直觉的洞察，但主持人只是掠过没有点破：**举办大满贯的四个国家，在过去 20 年男子网坛几乎

item.title: [All-In Podcast] Can the AI Industry Regulate Itself? Stripe Wants PayPal, China Catches Up, NY Bans Datacenters
item.dc:creator: All-In Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个被忽略的"元模式"：AI 治理正在复刻互联网的中国化路径——只是方向反了

大多数人把 Demis 的 SRO 提案、Anthropic 的州级"棘轮战略"、纽约的数据中心禁令看成孤立事件。但把它们叠在一张时间轴上，你会发现一个反常的镜像：中国用中央集权制造了 AI 的"确定性护城河"，而美国正在用"分权 + 恐惧"制造同样的护城河，只是收益者从国家变成了两三家私营实验室。

Sachs 说 Anthropic 想要"FAA for AI"，Chamath 说要防止"少数玩家用资产负债表把梯子拉上去"——他们其实在描述同一件事的两面：监管资本主义 (regulatory capitalism) 的自我组装。真正让我意外的观察是：Anthropic 的策略并不是"游说政府"，而是把政府当作分销渠道。它不需要说服华盛顿，只需要在 50 个州同时点火，让州级立法成为它的 API——一个把"安全焦虑"转换成"竞争对手合规成本"的 API。这是一种前所未见的商业模式：把外部性货币化的反向 SaaS。

跨领域连接：PayPal 收购案、Bending Spoons、以及"数字文艺复兴"的共同物理定律

表面上，Stripe 出价 PayPal、Bending Spoons 收购 Evernote/WeTransfer/AOL、Ryan Cohen 盯上 eBay，是三个不相关的故事。但把它们放进一个热力学框架里看——这些"僵尸互联网资产"就是被困在低能态里的高熵系统。它们积累了巨大的用户网络（4.39 亿 PayPal 账户），却因为组织熵（咨询公司文化、创始人流失、产品停滞）无法释放能量。

AI 不是在"改造"它们——AI 是在扮演麦克斯韦妖：一个能以极低成本对内部混乱进行分类、重排、再激活的信息机器。这解释了一个反直觉的现象：为什么 AI 时代第一波真正的巨额价值捕获，不会发生在纯 AI 公司，而会发生在"AI 原生运营者收购老互联网资产"这类交叉地带。Bending Spoons 从米兰远程重启十几家死掉的 Web 2.0 公司，本质上和 SpaceX 收购发动机公司让 Colossus 绕过清洁空气许可，是同一个操作——都在用"新一代物理"来套利"上一代监管/组织摩擦"造成的价值真空。

最出人意料的连接：Russia Today、反 GMO、反数据中心、以及一个正在成型的"认知供应链攻击"

Freeberg 提到 Russia Today 与反 GMO 情绪的时间相关性时，几乎在无意间揭示了本期播客里最深的系统性洞察——认知层是新的供应链，而供应链是可以被污染的。GMO 情绪曲线在 RT 进入美国后翻倍上升，在被驱逐后回落。同样的曲线正在数据中心议题上重演。

但真正让我意外的是这个连接：Anthropic 用"AI 灾难叙事"驱动州级立法，与外国影响运营用"数据中心叙事"减缓美国基建，在信息流形态上是同构的。它们都利用了同一个心理学漏洞——Sachs 说的"道德恐慌 (moral panic)"是免疫系统外的一种寄生模式：先制造焦虑，再提供"解决方案"（无论是新监管、新政策，还是让对手停下来）。

这里有一个极不舒服但也许更真实的假设：在信息生态里，"内部监管资本主义"和"外部认知战"不是对立的两股力量，而是共生的——它们互相为对方提供合法性和素材。Anthropic 的恐惧叙事让外国影响操作显得"有道理"；外国操作又让 Anthropic 的监管游说有了"民意基础"。这就是为什么 Chamath 说"必须尽快确立规则以避免这个 off-ramp"——他真正担心的不是任何一方，而是两者共振时产生的驻波：一个能让美国自我阉割 AI 竞争力的稳定态。

一个被所有人错过的连线：Token 价格差 100x = 主权代价

播客里所有话题——SRO、纽约禁令、开源之争、Apple 起诉 OpenAI、SpaceX 的数据泄漏——最终都指向同一个数字：每百万 token，Grok 是 1.5 美元，Fable 是 56 美元，中国模型是 50 美分。这不是价格差，这是一个国家愿意为"AI 主权幻觉"支付的税率。

当 Ramp 的 CEO 说 token 支出一年 21 倍增长、CFO 们即将迎来"错过财报的一天"时，他其实在描述一个宏观现象：美国企业正在集体为一个尚未证实的风险模型 (existential risk) 支付一个 100 倍的溢价。这个溢价的最终受益者不是纳税人，不是安全，而是那两三家品牌自己是"安全 AI"的公司。Sachs 说的"品牌自己安全，禁掉不安全，赚钱"这个三步战略——其实是对整个西方 AI 生态系统正在支付的隐性 GDP 税的精准描述。

Freeberg 详解 SRO 概念

I think it's worth putting a little definition around this proposal, which is to form an SRO, self-regulatory organization, because they're not purely independent. SRO's like FINRA and the National Futures Association, they exist in the financial markets and they were created to allow the financial institutions to set their regulatory rules, how they check each other, how they make sure that everyone is being safe because they're obviously all trading risk with one another. So the industry doesn't want to have exposure and they certainly don't want to have things get slowed down because that would make the markets inefficient.

So the analogy with AI is pretty appropriate here which is that there are many players in the industry. They are all trying to progress AI technology and no one wants to have a single regulatory body that comes in from the government or outside that says here are the tests you guys have to pass with your models in order for them to be appropriate. As we saw in California when California tried to pass AI legislation, I think it was about a year a year and a half ago, none of what they wrote even made sense at the time. But fast forward a year, none of those kind of rules and requirements actually map to the technology of the day.

So the purpose of an SRO like FINR run NFA is they can adjust how tests are being run, who is actually running the test and make sure the right experts are involved in doing this, independent experts that is to do the testing with federal government oversight but not control. So in the case of FINRA NFA, they report up ultimately to Senate committee and House committee that gives those committees oversight of those governing bodies that are supposed to be doing the work to make sure that they're doing their frigin jobs.

So the SRO concept would be that experts could be brought in from industry that know how to assess models for things like cyber risk, for things like biorisk, for things like weapons risk, social manipulation, etc., etc. that independent body can get voted on, can get changed over time, and because they actually have expertise in running software valves and running tests like this, they can operate at a faster pace than setting up a new government agency.

So, it's it's kind of a very elegant solution. And I think it's why everyone, to your point, Jal, and I I'll say this is right, the industry recognizes that there needs to be some degree of oversight and checkpoints here. And I think that this could actually solve that problem. So that's why I think everyone's kind of climbing on board with it because it doesn't actually hand stuff over to the government. It says, "Hey, we're going to get the right people to to take a look at these things and the government is going to have oversight ultimately."

Did Anthropic and OpenAI have a point of view? They both signed up to it. I don't think Daario directly, but Dario's president gave his thumbs up and then I think Sam gave his thumbs up which means they're on board.

He said you know there is a lot of common funding because Dina Powell asked this question on stage and he said there's a lot of common funding patterns of these people that are protesting um the data centers and he said you could actually trace it back to the same people that in a different era were protesting fracking. And so he was saying like it's these are all just hobby horses that they use to raise money, have a job. They're like professionally paid protesters. They kind of just show up out of nowhere. I didn't realize that there was such a commonality, but they're the same people.

The thing that I just can't understand for the life of me is why Anthropic is still funding these groups that want to put the kibos on new data center construction. There's one called public first where Daario just gave his first seven figure contribution and then a bunch of other employees at anthropic gave it and you know all these groups are trying to slow down AI development with new regulations and making it harder to build new data centers and at a certain point you just have to wonder I mean is this regulatory capture or they just kind of lost the plot because the number one thing slowing down the growth of anthropics revenue it's not demand I think it's the availability of compute in data centers. And so you're just kind of wondering like what is the point of all of this?

It's so true. I was talking to someone in politics about this and the theory that they had is well the Democrats aren't going to pause the data centers forever. They're going to pause them until they feel like they're in enough control that they can dictate all the rules. And so in other words, they're calling this a moratorum. And I think it does mean that the data centers are going to stop but eventually they're going to be in a position to say okay here are our terms if you want to turn these things back on right you want to lift the moratorum and then that's when we get this you know big government democrat defined AI regime and you know that it's going to consist of a new regulatory agency and new speech controls the whole trust and safety agenda from social media will be ported over that's this was this

One person I was talking to, this is what he was speculating is the real agenda is that eventually once Trump is no longer president or in some future Democratic administration, they will eventually lift the moratorum but on their terms. Now I think that's a really dangerous thing to do because you know Trump is president for another two years and then no one knows what's going to happen after that. And even if you lift the moratorum in say 2 and 1/2 or 3 years, it's going to take a couple of years for those projects to even ramp back up. So when you start talking about a moratorium on data centers, it's not like a a few month pause. It's probably a good 5 years at least before you know you can get another data center switched on in the state of New York.

Chamath 论数据中心资产定价与全球布局

Just so you know how bad it's gotten, there's a curve that you can use to price data center assets. And I think you guys know this, but I have this portfolio of these assets that myself and my partner Nita have accumulated. And what's so interesting is when we talk to all of the hyperscalers about giving us a price because we're trying to figure out whether we should keep it or build it or just sell it, the most incredible thing is how extreme the price is at the front end of the curve. When you have verifiable energizable power today. And the reason is exactly everything that you're saying, Saxs, which is that when you look out into the future, you know, we've said this before, but it's about 40% of all these projects are getting mothballled and stopped. And so it's creating this massive deficit of available energy to actually drive the use of AI. So to the extent that you actually want, you know, drug discovery or you want cancer diagnosis or you want better healthcare or better legal advice, we may actually not be able to service it based on all of the demand that exists because the power isn't there. The energy isn't there. And the reason why that's not there is because folks are just kind of reflexively protesting something that they don't completely understand clearly. So I think it's a really it's a really big problem.

I mean, we're going to have GPUs chasing energy. like where's their energy and just drive the GPUs there is what's going to happen right let me add one layer to it which is they're not only trying to stop data centers from being built in the US they're trying to stop data centers from being built internationally in our friends allies and partner countries and the way they're doing that is the same political forces that are stopping data centers are also behind all these new export controls on chips so they want to make it harder and harder to export chips to more and more countries including our friends and allies and so there's not going to data centers here. There's not going to be data centers in our allies. I mean, where are we going to put these things? I mean, those allies have unlimited energy, Middle East. Like, if you want some data centers, well, what's funny, Jason, is, you know, we did a bunch of Middle East data center stuff and then it's kind of stopped. Meaning, like there wasn't this growth that I thought would happen because it's a very conveniently placed geography. It's the Middle East for a reason. And so, you know, you can serve 4 billion people very quickly in under 200 milliseconds from there.

Instead, what happened was there was this explosion in Asia and specifically in Australia, which kind of surprised me because I would have thought that those folks are a little bit even further out on the DSA, you know, far left. I thought these things would not have happened, but they they were able to get big deals done. So, in this weird way, you have all of these other countries kind of running to try to embrace this stuff quickly. They've done a decent job. They're doing stuff to sort of like displace some of the energy that that is needed in the US. But the problem is we need to have enough surplus here because this is where most of the commerce is going to get created. That really that I think should

these are these are luxury regulations. Like you can afford if you're New York State or California to be like, you know what, we don't need this. This is a luxury for us to have an extra data center. If you're Australia, you might really need the money. If you're Texas, you might really want the money. So this is what's so crazy like virtue signaling only goes so far until your debt to GDP is high enough and or your productivity is low enough andor your foreign direct investment is low enough where you're like all right you know what screw all that we're just going to build a data center

but the the other thing is if you saw what happened this week the UAE now is able to import the best-in-class leading chips and so to your point Jason I think it restarts the cycle where you have to look very carefully at the Middle East because it's a very attractive place to build these things

and by the way you saw the you even if you just if you think about fiber and the milliseconds as you're talking about yes you can get to the four billion people but I don't know if you saw the the giant Starlink versions now they make like a really big version I think you got like one of the enterprise versions but there's like an even bigger enterprise version and they can bundle them together and you're starting to get to like 10 gig 20 gig setups so that means you could start putting these things almost anywhere which gets also like adds another

Freeberg：Russia Today、反 GMO 与"认知污染"

can we see your clip. The thing that you were mentioning before, this is not to your point as prevalent in the Middle East where you have monarchies and governments that aren't ruled by democracy, but in democracies, we see this anti-data center movement taking hold.

This chart is something that for me always kind of played a role in my understanding of where the incredible anti-GMO sentiment came about in the United States,

which is great.

Russia Today, this Russian media outlet launched in the US in 2010. They were kicked out of the US by Biden in 2022. And you can see that prior to Russia today existing in the US, there was no anti-GMO sentiment. GMOs were around since 1996. That's when they first had their big commercial launch in the US and were pretty prevalent for, you know, 14 plus years before everyone started to think GMOs are bad. We got to get rid of GMOs.

And you could ask people a hundred different ways very pointedly and specifically about the facts on the matter and the science of GMOs and all this sort of stuff, but everyone always had a reason why they didn't want them, similar to what we're hearing now with AI and data centers.

And it turns out that if you track back all of the media that had all this anti-GMO sentiment that ultimately got picked up by the mom bloggers that ultimately got put into social media feeds that ultimately everyone just accepted as truth. A lot of it originated in this Russian media push that happened around this era. And you can actually see this on the Google trend data that shows GMO and it's kind of write up. And then as Russia Today started to get cut by different media outlets and people stopped retweeting them and stopped reflecting them and stopped writing articles that followed Russia Today the anti-GMO sentiment declined in the US and I think you can see this going back decades.

You know there's this effort that the KGB kind of designed during the cold war called directed measures which was really meant to try and create an influence campaign through affecting media. So putting this kind of propaganda out through foreign media, particularly targeted western democracies. And you know, you could argue that maybe you could trace back what happened in Germany with nuclear energy as being kind of similarly originated. But there have been a series of these pushes that seem nonsensical if you're fairly rational and can have an actually objective debate about the scientific merit, the economic merit, the benefits of these technologies. But for some reason, what we call the activist community become heightened to them. Say that we've got to get rid of them. And everyone's got these different unfounded, scientifically unfounded reasons why they want to get rid of them. And you're like, wait a second, how did we end up in this place that we're literally handicapping ourselves.

And I think we're seeing something similar happening with data centers in the US today. The funding of the NOS's as they're being called, the media that's supporting this, the retweeting of the media. And then you asked people, there was a poll that came out today, something north of 50% of Americans believe that data centers increase the cost of water and electricity. Even if the data center is fully recycling the water and they're producing their own electricity, there's still this kind of repugnant reaction to the data center.

And so there has been this like deeply sewn psychological shift that's happened in the United States. and and and you know, people have these, well, I hate the rich, I hate tech, I hate AI, I don't want any of this stuff, I don't want any of this stuff,

but where does it all come from? I do worry that there's some degree of kind of call it foreign, you know, influence influence. I I don't love the word influence because everyone kind of everyone captures it up, but there is some degree of like I would say there's there's foreign interest. Let's let's call it that. No, I think it's more than that. So just one month ago, just one month ago, OpenAI published a blog post called PRC linked influence operations are targeting AI debates in the US and Politico covered this and a lot of other sites covered this. Basically what they are saying and in fact many people are saying is that China is behind a lot of these influence campaigns to shape US attitudes on AI data centers.

It makes sense. It makes a lot of sense. and there's going to be a congressional investigation of this. It does make sense cuz it is in their interest, right? If they can stop us from building this necessary infrastructure, then that's a way for China to win the AI race. If they can the market, if they can incentivize anthropic to, you know, pull the ladder up, if they can kill open source in the United States and constrain demand or the optionality and choice of lower, cheaper models, think about that for a second. at $56 per million input tokens. I mean, versus 50 cents for the rest of the world. All of a sudden, it doesn't take a company that's much much worse than you to beat you when your cost is 50 to 100x more. Right? That's just the math. The math ain't mapping.

You know, it's Sasha made the point that these enterprises are not just paying for AI with money. They're paying again by feeding those frontier models their proprietary knowledge, right? And all their their alpha. So, it's like a double whammy. It's like it's more expensive and you're potentially mortgaging your future. Look, let's be honest. It is obvious where foreign governments have an enormous incentive to try to manipulate and influence the comingings and goings in America. I think we should just acknowledge that. The idea that that doesn't happen is very naive. Now the question is we have to be able to call it out and put our finger on it because otherwise what is clearly happening is that there's a lot of Americans that will just fall for this and they will not think from first principles.

Sachs：道德恐慌与我们即将扼杀的皇冠明珠

We have a huge we have a huge moral panic going on with respect to AI. Look when you talk about catastrophes that could result from AI. What are we talking about? We're talking about things that might happen in the future. Nothing resembling this has happened yet. you know, even the cyber risk that everyone's been talking about, job loss or job loss, it's like none of it's turned out to be true. We haven't seen any of it so far.

But we're on the threshold, I think, of destroying the crown jewel of our economy, which is the system of free market innovation that we have, this culture of rapid iteration of anyone with a good idea can go raise risk capital and start their idea, start their company. And we're on the verge, you know, now we're talking I think about how far the Overton window has moved where we're actually saying that creating a FINRA for our industry might be better than all the alternatives. FINRA is a bunch of stock brokers writing rules. And when's the last time there was ever any innovation in that sector? I mean, I guess Robin Hood made trading free. That was it.

That was a big one. Yeah. Flow. Okay. But that's not real innovation. Okay. That's like an innovation with respect to a pricing model.

And we're actually saying that that might be the least bad alternative is having the equivalent of a bunch of stock brokers creating new rules that all these AI companies are not going to have to abide by. It's crazy. We are going to we are going to throw away the lead that we have in this. And by the way, Kimmy K3 just came out and people are saying it's it's now right up there. It's very very close to the frontier. We may have months on China if that. and we're going to create all these crazy rules and new regulatory bodies for risks that have not manifested yet.

It's worth monitoring the situation, but it's not worth panicking. Like, you should monitor the situation with self-driving cars and job loss. China is certainly doing that. They just stopped giving out permits for self-driving cars as an example because it's going so well and they're losing jobs and and there are people who are getting there's a little civil unrest. So they just said we're going to make self-driving cars um licensed and so they're not giving out any more license moratorium on license for now. It's worth watching Mythos and if it could hack your system. Palo Alto Network's checking it out, other people checking out. It's all worth monitoring but yes it there's no disaster here today because of AI. nothing's jumping out of your chat GPT window. You know, the worst case scenario is you blow out some tokens, you know, okay, great. That that's that's the biggest

There's only a handful of companies that are even at the frontier and they all have safety testing and red teaming and all the rest of doing a good job. Yeah, I'm saying stop that. I'm just questioning whether we need some vast regulatory apparatus now to start doing all certainly premature and we did this because of science fiction and Daario saying all jobs are going away. I mean that that that was the most ridiculous thing when he said like he's panicked that it's going to be 80 or 90% jobs in 2026. What was his claim? I Nick get the exact claim. I think he said 50% of jobs. He said he said with it he said 50% of entry- level knowledge worker jobs are going away within 1 to 5 years. That was one year ago. So it's a little ridiculous. Yeah. I mean it's but he's not even know how to use the tools yet. He's been in a state of panic since GPT2. Yes. Yes. I mean remember they wanted they wanted to have uh regulatory approval for models that use 10 to the 25th flops. Right. And every single AI model is like well past that threshold now. Yeah. And we haven't seen any of the the harm. Look, they thought that 10 to the 25th flops would be enough compute to create, you know, the Terminator, you know, to create Skynet.

讥讽 Daario 的三步战略

No offense, Freeberg, but one guy's panic attacks, one guy's anxiety condition might have shaped the whole course of history here like Dar does Daario have like I'm not making light of it, but does he have an anxiety issue where he's like overly concerned about this stuff or is it just delusions of grandeur? Come on the pod, Dario. Invite's open. Come hang out. I'm sure he'd love to come on the pod after you just accused him of having a panic attack but I mean, he seems like he's in a perpetual one. No, let me tell you, listen, I it could be psychological, but I actually think that there's a strategy that makes a lot of sense, and it's a very simple, straightforward strategy. Number one, brand yourself as a safe AI company. Number two, ban unsafe AI. Three, profit. Yeah, there you go. That's the strategy. Kind of brilliant.

科学角：Calico + Revel Pharma 的抗衰老突破

All right, everybody. Go to allin.com/events and sign up for the Allin Summit in September. Scholarships are open. Let's do a quick amazing deep robust science corner with our boy David Friedber.

Before we get into the science corner, I'm going to give a shout out to Ronnie Dog for adoption. I love family dog rescue in Sonoma. Check out his Instagram link. God, here he goes

in the description. This dog needs a home. He was fostered and he lost the foster home. Someone come and grab him. He's awesome. All right, let's get into this. This is what we're doing. He's trying to he's trying to get more Q points. That dog looks delicious. Gross. You don't live in Sri Lanka anymore. Chimal. Yeah, seriously. Sri Lanka taking a spray. How do you marinate that dog in Sri Lanka? Do what you got to do in Sri Lanka. Salt and pepper free or do you like something else? You know, just a little salt and pepper. 12 hour marinade. Do you use bolet? Oh, do do you like do you like a little yogurt and garam masala? Maybe do a little spicy. Do you guys want to talk about reversing aging? Yes. So, I want to talk about but I got to drop.

All right, guys. I got to go to the EIFFEL TOWER. PAUL,

I'LL COVER IT. THE audience will stick around. Science corner. All right. So, Jamal, you can drop two if you want. I'll cover science corner solo.

细胞外基质、CML 与 AlphaFold 设计的酶

So in the past we've talked about Yamanaka factors which are these proteins that can go into cells and reverse the aging of the cell and the cell starts to act young again. Pretty amazing. And uh there's a lot of advancement happening on that front.

But this paper that came out just this week that everyone's kind of going crazy about was put out uh jointly by Calico which is Google's kind of you know age reversal startup that's super secretive that they're not allowed to talk about in partnership with a group called Revel Pharma. And what they focused on was what's called the extracellular matrix. The parts outside of the cell that age.

And and what does aging actually look like outside of the cell? Well, over time, sugars and fats bind to proteins in the area between our cells and they accumulate. They don't get cleaned off. And as they accumulate and they don't get cleaned off, they make it harder for your body to clean out that area, to maintain that area. It causes stickiness. It causes binding. And that reduces mobility. and ultimately leads to things like wrinkles in our skin, makes it harder for our joints to move.

Is that what visceral fat is? No, it's called glycation. And so it's the binding of sugar and fat to the proteins that sit in that extra cellular in between the cells. Exactly.

And so it's that whole gunky area in between the cells that when you're young works well, everything's smooth. The proteins get replaced if they break down. And as you get older, sugars and fats kind of stick to these proteins, block them up. And as they get blocked up, your body can't repair them. It can't clean them. And more importantly, it changes the structure and the shape of those proteins. So things like collagen that are far apart stick together. And that causes things like wrinkles and that causes immobility. And it also causes inflammation because then those proteins kind of look different than they're supposed to. And your body starts to attack them. And that activates inflammation. And that's why we get one of the reasons why we get more and more inflammation as we get older.

And so one of the key what are called advanced glycation end products that's the term for these things is called CML. CML is kind of the predominant molecule that that gets formed in this extracellular matrix that's driving aging and nothing breaks it down.

So these scientists set out to try and create an enzyme. An enzyme is a protein that breaks something down um that can break down CML. And remember, a protein is just a series of amino acids. And those amino acids are programmed by DNA. So you can put three letters of DNA to make an amino acid. So you can literally just print DNA and then put it in a bacteria to print proteins and then test those proteins to see what they do. That's the the modern kind of era of kind of protein synthesis and protein testing.

And so these guys kind of went out and they took the target which is CML and tried to figure out okay how do we actually degrade CML clear that extracellular matrix and reverse aging. And they started with AlphaFold and they used Alphafold to find a protein that could bind to CML and activate an enzyatic or process that would break it down. And then they took that protein from AlphaFold that comes out of a bacteria. They produced it. They started to test it and then they started to find some of the binders or the parts of that protein that they could make better and they used you know DNA programming to change it and they made hundreds and then thousands of variants of it to measure activity which is how good is it at breaking down the CML and they did this recursively five different cycles

and then eventually they tested it once they' kind of gotten it breaking down the CML really well in a test tube they started to test it on the proteins that we would find in our body, cassine, collagen, retinal proteins which are in your eye, hemoglobin, and they were able to get rid of 52 to 97% of the CML, just degraded away.

And then they um they found several sites where they were able to degrade over 90%. And then they took actual human skin from elderly patients that had donated their skin and they put this enzyme onto that skin and they were able to eliminate 55% of the CML on the skin which basically reversed the skin's age down to the age of a 31y old. This is from greater than 70 year old patients just by putting this enzyme on the skin.

And so it's kind of a groundbreaking demonstration of combination of alphafold what's called directed evolution where you change the order of the DNA that changes the structure of the protein to test different proteins do high throughput screening and ultimately make a novel protein that doesn't exist in nature today that can do something pretty profound for human health.

And now the next set of questions is okay well great this enzyme is awesome. How are we going to get it into our bodies? How are we going to get it into that extracellular matrix? Is it going to be a cream? Is it going to be a shot, a supplement? Could we eventually take an RNA shot that makes the protein inside of our body and starts to do the degradation from within? A lot of questions kind of still to be answered, but it really, I think, lights a great path forward.

Amazing for these novel therapies that we're developing. It's [ __ ] awesome. I mean dude likeing you know, all my I got all these joint pains in my hip and my shoulder now. Like everything you can feel yourself getting older. Well, that's I I will tell you this right now. That will not be the first market. The first market will be cosmetic and cosmetic skin. Yeah. It will be a trillion dollar market. If if you can create a cream, dude, if you could put this enzyme literally on your skin and have it absorb a cream. Yeah. Game over. It's it's a that that alone is $2 trillion. But I mean, dude, AI, let's just talk about applications of AI, why it's actually awesome that everyone should be able to agree on and you can't be convinced by some foreign scop. This is [ __ ] awesome. I mean, this was alpha full used to discover this thing and evolve it and drive this outcome. Everyone can benefit from it. It's just so profound that we have this tool at our disposal in this day and age. I think it's pretty awesome. Anyway, thanks for sticking around for Science Corner. Guys, I love you. All right, bro. Love you, too.

收尾

We'll let your winners ride. We open sourced it to the fans and they've just gone crazy with it. Queen of besties are my dog taking your driveways. Oh man, my habitasher will meet. We should all just get a room and just have one big huge orgy cuz they're all just useless. It's like this like sexual tension that they just need to release. Your feet. We need to get merch. I'm going all in. I'm going all in.

item.description:
## 一个被忽略的"元模式"：AI 治理正在复刻互联网的中国化路径——只是方向反了
大多数人把 Demis 的 SRO 提案、Anthropic 的州级"棘轮战略"、纽约的数据中心禁令看成孤立事件。但把它们叠在一张时间轴上，你会发现一个反常的镜像：**中国用中央集权制造了 AI 的"确定性护城河"，而美国正在用"分权 + 恐惧"制造同样的护城河，只是收益者从国家变成了两三家私营实验室**。
Sachs 说 Anthropic 想要"FAA for AI"，Chamath 说要防止"少数玩家用资产负债表把梯子拉上去"——他们其实在描述同一件事的两面：**监管资本主义 (regulatory capitalism) 的自我组装**。真正让我意外的观察是：Anthropic 的策略并不是"游说政府"，而是**把政府当作分销渠道**。它不需要说服华盛顿，只需要在 50 个州同时点火，让州级立法成为它的 API——一个把"安全焦虑"转换成"竞争对手合规成本"的 API。这是一种前所未见的商业模式：**把外部性货币化的反向 SaaS**。
## 跨领域连接：PayPal 收购案、Bendin

item.title: [Bits + Bips] Is This Crypto Rally Real? GSR's Andy Baehr Maps the Signals to Watch
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、"Fed 至日" —— 一个被忽略的季节性隐喻

Andy 在整场对话中最富诗意也最容易被忽略的一句话是 "Fed solstice"（Fed 至日）。他把市场对加息峰值的集体感知，比作我们对夏至冬至的天文认知——你不需要看到春天，你只需要知道太阳已经过了那个"翻转点"，市场情绪就会瞬间反转。

这里有一个反直觉的洞见：牛市的启动点不是"利率见顶"，而是"我们知道利率会见顶"的那一刻。这两者之间可能相差数月，但资产价格几乎是同一天开始定价的。这也解释了为什么这轮反弹总是"单级火箭"——市场还没看到那个至日，只是感受到了偶然的暖意。

二、DeFi 利率 = 加密世界的"心电图"

大多数人盯着 ETF 资金流、盯着 Bitcoin 价格，但 Andy 悄悄透露了一个近乎"内幕"的信号：Aave 的借贷利率与美国国债的一年期利率几乎重合。这意味着——加密世界的"信用利差"为零。

跨领域连接来了：这与 2007 年 TED spread（LIBOR 与短期美债之差）压缩至历史低点前后的状况在结构上惊人相似——流动性泛滥、投机意愿枯竭、市场进入"低能量休眠"。传统金融用信用利差判断风险偏好；加密世界现在有了自己的等价物。Andy 没有明说，但这暗示着：当 DeFi 利率突然拉升到 20%+ 时（正如特朗普当选后那个月），那不是危机信号，而是杠杆需求苏醒的第一次心跳。

三、DAT（Digital Asset Treasuries）的"中段结构性买盘"悖论

一个让我意外的观察：Andy 把 DAT 定位在"动量中段"而非启动端。这颠覆了很多人的直觉——大家以为 MicroStrategy、MetaPlanet 这类公司是"永动机式"结构性买家。但他指出一个残酷现实：当 mNAV 跌破 1，ATM 发行反而稀释股东价值，DAT 就"熄火"了。

这里的跨领域连接是反身性理论（Soros）在 21 世纪的复刻版本：DAT 的买入能力依赖股价溢价，股价溢价依赖买入能力形成的叙事——这是一个自我强化也自我毁灭的闭环。所以 DAT 不是牛市的"发动机"，它是牛市的"涡轮增压器"——只有当主引擎（散户 + Perp 杠杆 + ETF 流入）已经点火，DAT 才能放大动力；主引擎熄火时，它比谁都跑得快。

四、隐藏的元洞见：这场对话本身就是"低能量市场"的镜像

最出人意料的观察在对话的结构本身：Andy 使用的每一个比喻——"火箭助推器熄火"、"ambivalence（矛盾摇摆）"、"洋葱皮层层参与"、"至日翻转"——都是关于"缺乏东西"的比喻，而不是关于"存在什么"的比喻。

这与 2021 年那种"什么都在涨"、比喻全是"月亮、狗币、超级周期"的语言形成刺眼对比。语言学上，市场周期可以通过分析师的隐喻库来预测——当行业顶级交易台在描述市场时开始大量使用"缺席、等待、耐心、意外"这些词，往往对应着大底部前的最后一段沉默期。这不是玄学，是集体心理向语言的漏出。

一个可操作的启发：下次听访谈时，数一数"惊喜（surprise）"这个词出现的次数。Andy 全程用了 4 次，且每次都指向"上行意外"。他没说破，但这可能是他真正的仓位。

开场：为什么"意外"是最强的价格燃料

I don't think it's priced in right now, and so surprise is one of the most powerful, I guess, emotions that can help fuel either up or down moves in prices. So passage would be a surprise, and I think it would be hard to imagine a market that doesn't experience some some lift.

嘉宾介绍：GSR 的 Andy Baehr

All right, welcome back. So I am very pleased to bring in my guest today, a repeat guest, a friend of mine, Andy Baehr. He is the Managing Director of Asset Management at GSR. So welcome, Andy, great to see you again.

Andy: Steve, yeah, good to have you here.

CPI 数据与市场情绪

Steve: And you're the perfect guest to have this week because, as I said in my opening, crypto has had its best week in months. People are feeling bullish, Bitcoin's been pushing up against $65,000, ETH is starting to set its sights on $2,000 again. And it all really, I think, comes down to one number: 3.5%. Specifically, the June CPI. And it's down from the year-on-year rate of 4.2%, and core has been flat month over month for the first time in five-plus years as well.

So 3.5% is not any number to write home about, and it's far off the target of 2%, but it's gone down. And there's been a bit of a relief rally. That said, I think the question that I want to pose to you first — and I'm sure everyone has been kind of weighing themselves — is what to make of this. Because, again, it seems like the reasons why CPI went down seem to be one-offs. I don't necessarily expect that to happen again when we get another reading next month.

And although it appears that the proclivity for a rate hike during the next FOMC meeting next week could be off the table because of this, Kevin Warsh also said in congressional testimony — I think his exact phrasing was "inflation was a choice" — meaning that he's still committed to being hawkish if he needs to be.

So what do you make of this? Is it just as simple as a relief rally, or are there bigger trends underlying that are worth pointing out here?

Andy：矛盾摇摆 vs. 信念

Andy: Yeah, it's a great question. There's a lot to unpack there. It feels, especially given all of the ambivalence — and that's the word that we've been using to kind of tag the market conditions through most of the second quarter, even at the end of the first quarter — ambivalence, not meaning the market doesn't care what it's doing, but that it will have these pulses of what appear to be well-fueled rallies. You even start to see a little bit of energy returning into the perpetuals market. And then suddenly that rally kind of dissipates very quickly, and then there's a liquidation, and we're back to where we started.

And that happened with Bitcoin in the high 70s getting through into the low 80s around the time of Consensus in the springtime. And then we had that kind of shunt down to the low 60s, down to sort of the production level of Bitcoin, which actually did bring some energy back to the market. But we're still in this kind of ambivalent phase, right, where I guess the opposite of ambivalence is conviction, where you can reliably depend on these rallies to continue and to really create a different momentum cycle.

So to phrase your question in another way: is this another ambivalent, single-booster-rocket rally where there's no rocket to follow once the booster rocket is expended? Or is this where we begin to get legs?

"Fed 至日"：峰值鹰派何时到来

I think, zooming out, the best thing that feels comfortable to say is that we're in an environment where we don't know where peak hawkishness is in terms of market sentiment. It hasn't been really since like 2022, pre-FTX, when the Fed hiked very aggressively to sort of burn off the fiscal stimulus after COVID. And of course, crypto assets were very challenged, as were equities, in that environment. Why? Because we didn't know what peak hawkishness was going to be.

So think of it as seasonality: we don't know when the Fed's solstice is going to be — that moment at which we collectively feel comfortable that we know when the hikes are going to end. And it doesn't help, of course, that we have a new Fed chair, one who we're not that familiar with yet, but certainly one who doesn't seem very comfortable or very eager to sort of soothe us.

So I think until you collectively, globally reach this idea of what I'm thinking of as the Fed solstice of peak hawkishness, and you can sort of see over the crest of the hill to what might unfold — beyond that, it's very difficult to feel like any kind of rally is going to be reliably sustainable.

如何评价 Kevin Warsh 这位新 Fed 主席

Steve: Yeah. And there's a lot to dive into on that answer. So let's try to break it down piece by piece. One quick question to follow up, because I don't think you and I have really had a chance to speak about Kevin Warsh since he was confirmed as Fed chair.

He doesn't want to give out forward guidance in the same spirit as Ben Bernanke or Jerome Powell, et cetera. And he's not issuing his own dot plot. He really wants the Fed to be — and I guess this is praiseworthy — reactive to the data. And I have some sympathy for the Fed giving this forward guidance because I think in some ways it was then stepping into a void that was abdicated by Congress. But that's a whole other story.

What do you make of him as a Fed chair in a moment like this? Where, on the one hand, it could be somewhat soothing to markets that he is sort of striking his independence — the whole "inflation is a choice" phrase is one that I think history books 10 to 50, 100 years from now will be written about. But at the same point, he has a president that wants lower rates, and this inflation print, even if the reasons for the decline are one-off, could lead to some competing priorities. So what do you make of him?

Andy: I'm far from the greatest or the most skilled Fed watcher or Fed whisperer, but it's pretty clear that you don't have a sycophantic Fed chair. You have one who has declared, in bold print, that he's going to be independent and not necessarily aim to soothe the market nor over-inform the market, even if he were.

It's a new relationship with the world and the Fed chair, and that's always a special time. He also has a not-the-simplest set of circumstances to work with, when you've seen the gyration of things like energy prices, which now have subdued but could reinflate very quickly given the geopolitical situation.

So I don't know. And I think few people feel very comfortable except that they have a sense — and it's priced into interest rate futures — that there's very likely hikes coming up, whether they're sooner or later, whether we can delay them, or how many there will be, we don't know. But we know that we're in this uncertain, heightening cycle and we don't know where it ends. And that's where I come back to: when we collectively get to this comfort point of "okay, we've reached peak hawkishness," the sentiment can shift very quickly.

Bitcoin 真正定价的两样东西

Let's remember, this all comes down to two things with respect to Bitcoin in particular. Bitcoin is looking at inflation expectations and it's looking at nominal rate expectations. And just like in 2022, when the nominal rates accelerated so quickly that they just blasted past inflation expectations, that was very difficult for Bitcoin — because that expected real rate was going higher.

When we get to the point where expected real rates can be understood better, that forms, I think, a better macroeconomic support for Bitcoin, but also more practically, it will form a better sense of what financing is going to be like in fiat terms to provide more leverage into the crypto system, which it desperately needs to regain some of the volatility and energy — volume that has declined since last October.

So it all really comes down to rates. And I have a lot more ideas about rates that I hope we can cover today. But in the zoom-out phase — and looking at crypto in anything more condensed than quarters for me is very, very difficult — I do think that all of that adds up to learning and collectively getting to that point of max hawkish sentiment and seeing the other side of the hill.

股市轮动：Mag Seven 与 Russell 2000

Steve: Yeah. And I guess within the spirit of that max hawkishness and trying to read some of the tea leaves in the equities markets, I'd love to get your thoughts on some of the rotations that I'm seeing, and I'm sure you are too. The Mag Seven stocks continue to struggle, but the AI ones are going gangbusters. And we're seeing a real rotation into smaller businesses, more cyclical stocks — the Russell 2000 is doing extremely well. What do you make of that? And how does that impact your view of risk sentiment right now?

Andy: I do think it's quite curious. And by professional choice, I'll spend more time looking at crypto sectors and crypto market cap sizes than equities. But you can't help notice this Mag Seven volatility versus other sectors which seem to catch a lot of energy. And you just wonder whether that's just — I think even the last time we were speaking on this program, this topic was coming up about unanticipated equity rotation.

加密世界中的类似现象：小盘表现超预期

It actually makes me think about how crypto performed in Q2, where as bad as it was, you had actual outperformance of small-cap crypto relative to Bitcoin, Ether, Solana, XRP — which is startling.

In my old job, we built this index called the CoinDesk 80, which is kind of a mid- and small-cap index that starts at sort of token number 21 and goes down to 100. In any kind of healthy or even stable neutral market condition, you can rely on large-cap cryptos to sort of pull away from small-cap crypto. It just means that there's a collective attention on those larger liquid names. And over time, that's a pretty reliable indicator of a normal market.

So to see, in such a sudden uptrend of small caps gaining a lot of energy and briefly outpacing lower-volatility Bitcoin, Ether, Solana — normally over time, you're going to have those mega-caps sort of pull away. So in Q2, to see that CoinDesk 80 index was down but it was down less than Ether, is really kind of stunning. And it just showed how much evacuation there was from those majors — in ETFs, in perps markets, in spot markets, in digital asset treasuries like your own.

Is that a capitulation level towards the end of Q2? Perhaps. But it's certainly a sign that there was an evacuation that happened over that longer period of time.

Now, coming back — anecdotally, I'll see a down S&P 500 and I'll see Apple, Amazon, Microsoft up, and vice versa. What to make of that? I would guess that traders are going where they see the action is going to be, which has been, I guess, the biggest rationale for why crypto has less energy than some of these other sectors or subsectors right now.

从"矛盾摇摆"到"信念"：加密市场的买盘从哪里来？

Steve: Yeah. And I think that leads into the discussion that I want to have next with you, which really kind of touches on one of your first points: conviction. Where do you see conviction in the marketplace? Clearly, we were able to see conviction the last couple of years in the Mag Seven. We are seeing conviction right now when it comes to AI stocks and anything adjacent to the AI economy.

I mean, one doesn't need to look any further than — aside from just the market performance of crypto stocks — money flowing out of crypto ETFs so they can have more dry powder to get in on the SpaceX IPO. And then the Anthropic one and OpenAI ones that are probably coming later this year, and buying into every stock in that market under the sun. And to your point about some of the rotations into the cyclicals, and the idea of traders just focused more on volatility but not conviction from retail, from large buy-and-hold institutions — that certainly rings true to me.

And I think that's a good segue into the crypto discussion because, again, terrific week. I mean, ETH is up big, Bitcoin's up big on the week, on the month. Finally, we're seeing some green shoots. But each time this has happened, especially during this crypto winter, it's fallen back. There was a brief relief rally, ironically, during the Iran conflict when it was in its heyday a few months ago. But that had a little more to do to perhaps gold reversing back to the mean and maybe some crypto picking up a little bit of that slack.

And I am unsure if we're going to really see a real sustained uptrend, like we have to be able to identify: where's the buying pressure coming from? Is it sustained? Are there real almost non-macro factors that are unique to crypto that are going to help keep this going? Some evidence to me is promising — ETF flows in Bitcoin and ETH both ended eight weeks of outflows with positive inflows, but they've been whipsawing this week.

But in my opinion, too, it's also hard to have this discussion without thinking about Strategy, where they sold almost $500 million — they topped their ATM to $500 million — and they didn't buy one Bitcoin with it. They're just stocking up their cash to make sure they have enough dividend payments to handle their preferreds for the next couple of years. MetaPlanet is the same way. So that's one huge structural buyer that is sitting on the sidelines, at least for now.

So I know that was a long preamble to me asking you a question, but I'd love for you to kind of react to what I just said. And what are you seeing, especially as you kind of monitor trading desks? How is the smart money positioning here?

数字资产金库（DAT）：完整市场的补丁

Andy: Yeah, all good points. At GSR and within my vertical in Asset Management, just to comment on DATs first: we're pro-DAT, in that we definitely see how they can help complete the market. Or the combination of having a treasury that's focused on one digital asset plus native skill in trying to put that asset to work — I think that takes a pretty unique skill set.

Your firm and other digital asset treasuries who do this well provide to U.S. equity investors, we think, or have the potential to provide, an interesting way to experience a digital asset allocation that has these kind of extra features. Strategy famously does this mostly through financial engineering. There are other ways to do it, some of which are more crypto-native. And if they're done skillfully, they can be done in a way which meets investor objectives. And if they're not, then they'll be rooted out and investors will vote with their feet from then on.

去年反弹的教科书式路径

In terms of what takes one of these thin booster-rocket rallies and actually lets the vehicle continue to achieve altitude and velocity — the rally that we had last year, I think, was your picture-perfect model of a great rally. And Ether kind of led that rally, in my view.

Stage 1: It started with a very concentrated long-Bitcoin, short-Ether hedge fund or active trader position that, when it began to unwind, Ether kind of came out with a short squeeze.

Stage 2: Crypto-native people saw this happening, saw a trend starting to form, and piled in — both in terms of spot but also in terms of perps. Let's remember, all of the crypto market is somewhere between two-thirds and three-quarters (depending on when you measure) derivatives, and a quarter to a third spot. So derivatives are fantastically important in determining where prices move.

Stage 3: Once the spot-perp rally continued, you started to see this probably around May, June of 2024 — ETF inflows, even to the point where Ether inflows were higher than Bitcoin ETF inflows, which was quite remarkable, especially since they were newer and the relative size of the assets. And then, of course, you had this punctuation of the GENIUS Act being passed, and so many stablecoins rely on the Ethereum network to exist. So you had this fundamental extra boost.

I think you'll be able to trust these rallies more when you see the new entrants — layers of onion skin sort of start to participate. It has to start with a catalyst. For Ether, this year's catalyst may be all of the fundamentals linked to the formation of all these foundation spinoffs. It actually might be a narrative and fundamental basis why Ether is outperforming in Q3 other majors, pretty solidly.

You do need to have a little bit more volatility and energy and leverage in the market for that perp layer to really create some lift.

那么，钱从哪里来？

Steve: What we've been missing, I think. And I didn't mean to interrupt, but where do you think that money comes from? Who are those participants? Is it normal trading desks that perhaps have been sitting a little on the sidelines that get back in because they have a conviction that they want to express?

We're talking right now — the stablecoin supply has dropped about $10 billion since May, which is not a massive number in a $300 billion-plus market, but it's not nothing, and it's the largest drop since the Terra/Luna collapse, which is something that we all kind of remember too. So that's dry powder that is going away.

So this all goes back to my one question: who are going to be those structural buyers? Is it the DATs? For all the reasons that you and I just discussed — right now it's difficult, with the vast majority of them trading at an mNAV below one, it's dilutive for them to tap ATMs and issue additional common shares. Doesn't mean they won't always, they won't necessarily do it, but it does complicate things a little bit more.

We're not seeing any sort of government strategic reserve, which I know had been discussed for a while. And it still seems that a lot of attention from perhaps the ETF buyers that would go into crypto is still going into AI, looking for some of those soft spots — maybe not the direct AI plays, but now the adjacent ones that perhaps have a little bit more room to catch up.

So, all this setup — and we're going to talk about clarity, I know you mentioned a few of the offshoots from the AI that have come about and those are crypto-specific — but if there are one or two people watching, listening, one or two signs to suggest that this is going to get some momentum, what are you looking for? Or is there anything interesting that you see come across your desk that makes you sort of change your forecasting?

DAT 在动量结构中的真正位置

Andy: We look at it constantly. And you brought up an interesting point that the DATs, especially the DATs outside of Bitcoin — I didn't mention them in that narrative about that very textbook rally of last year, where you had constituent after constituent coming back in — because DATs at that point were still a little bit new.

You would think about traders, then you think about spot and perp buyers on centralized exchanges, then you think about the ETF flows starting to reverse, and so on. But DATs would not only slide in there as buyers who I think would participate sort of in the middle of momentum, right, because the shareholders would get excited and that would help create the policy momentum for more token purchases to happen — but there are also holders that would be structural and more permanent.

Because ETF holders now, famously, that's not permanent capital — that's not, not even a little bit.

Steve: Not even a little bit. And just look at the last eight weeks, it's very impermanent.

Andy: Correct. And I think that brings out another point. When we talk about ETFs and multi-token ETFs — there was another one that came to market today — and those being investment products, we're still in this market where you're dealing with traders.

让市场脱离"矛盾摇摆"的信号

And to your question of who is it going to be who really gets the altitude of a rally going and creates more reliable momentum — traders will pick up on things pretty quickly. But momentum signals now have been frustrated for so long that a momentum signal — and this is exactly what we're measuring with our ambivalence and conviction gauges, which we've used externally a few times — you really need to see that a momentum cycle is in place once, I think, people feel that it's trustworthy and that there won't be enough Fed surprises or geopolitical surprises or other regular political surprises to ruin things. I think it'll be game on. I think we can exit the ambivalence phase as meaningfully and assertively as we've entered into it.

But maybe that kind of Consensus-event-type rally was the strongest of them, that felt like it was going to be strong enough, but it wasn't. I think for the people listening who are just waiting to get in — I would almost rather join a rally that you feel really has legs and be patient and be more comfortable not getting a bottom price, but getting a more healthy momentum rally.

So our quantitative signals that we use both for our ETF product and for institutional absolute return products — they're certainly not solely momentum-driven. There's a whole library of signals that we use, but a lot of those signals, or at least some of the sections of signals, are geared around how sustainable is this energy that's flowing into the market.

一切归于"能量"

So to sum it up in one word: it's all about energy. If there's enough energy — and this is where the booster rocket metaphor feels very comfortable — if there's enough energy to create that altitude to get you into space, you'll be fine. But if there's not enough energy, it won't.

And I like to measure energy in terms of volumes of spot and derivatives. I like to watch funding rates. More recently, I've really started to like to watch DeFi rates, because DeFi rates are an excellent leading signal of who needs leverage — like, who is borrowing money to go and buy stuff.

DeFi 利率作为杠杆需求的心电图

Steve: And what are they telling you? Have you seen anything in changes in put/call ratios, DeFi rates creeping up?

Andy: I think it's simpler than that. In my old seat, I spent a lot of time looking at Aave rates. We published this SOFA rate for Aave, which basically compounded Aave rates into a daily rate and published that. These rates went over 20% the month after the president was elected. And now they're right around where the risk-free rate is — where the yield curve is out from SOFR to one year, so they're all around whatever, three-and-three-quarters to 4.1%.

Steve: So there's no term, there was no credit spread basically for DeFi over money markets, and it doesn't seem like people are anxious to lever up, is what you're saying.

Andy: Precisely right. And the most fascinating thing about that is — imagine a situation where Warsh gets up and he has a particularly strong cup of coffee, he's feeling great, and says, "You know what, I'm going to surprise, I'm going to do my first surprise, and I'm going to lower rates." Right, not going to happen, but let's pretend. The market would love it, asset prices would love it, Bitcoin price would most likely love it.

And what would happen: people would go on Aave and start borrowing. And since those are supply-demand pools, you could see this situation where fiat rates go down and DeFi rates — not only Aave, but every vault on Morpho, every Steakhouse vault, every Gauntlet vault, everything on Veda and Concrete and all these platforms — would shoot up, because there would be this acute rush to lever.

And leverage, whether you do it by perps or by borrowing to loop your spot holdings, is really what's going to shoot prices up pretty high to the level where maybe, okay, that triggers some ETF inflows, maybe that triggers some DAT purchases, maybe that actually triggers some longer-term holders from getting involved, and then you have a good market.

But in the meantime, if you're looking at DeFi rates and they're hovering around where SOFR to one-year Treasuries is, it's hard to see that. That just means that supply is just pounding into these platforms and saying, "give me any yield I can get." Those interest rate models are just supply-demand, linear stepwise linear functions. They're very, very simple. The more supply, the lower the yield. The less demand, the lower the yield. So if you see these rates hovering around risk-free, that's low energy.

Steve: Okay, a very easy thing for people to monitor.

Andy: And given all the new and exciting fixed-income products in the DeFi world, there's a very rich way to examine how these things are moving and changing.
