item.description:
## 一、麦卡伦下课背后的"责任逃逸"结构学
大多数评论都在讨论麦卡伦的战绩，但真正值得注意的是访谈中两个人的语言结构对比：麦卡伦用"四年周期"来定义自己的失败，而 Richard Gould 用"过去六到九个月的几个结果"来描述同一件事。**时间尺度的选择本身就是一种权力表达**——运动员被训练在长周期里承担责任，而管理者习惯于把时间窗口切得越短越好，因为窗口越短，可归因的责任就越小。这不是英格兰板球独有的病，而是所有"运动员出身 vs. 行政出身"的组织里反复出现的裂缝。
一个出人意料的观察：**麦卡伦之所以能坦然承认失败，恰恰是因为他曾经是顶级球员**。视频里那句"能爬到运动巅峰的人往往对自己诚实，否则爬不上去"是全场最深的一句话。也就是说，Gould 的推卸并非人格缺陷，而是**他所处的选拔机制从未奖励过诚实**——行政官僚是被"避免出错"筛选出来的，而运动员是被"直面失败"筛选出来的。两种物种，无法通婚。
## 二、FA 与 FIFA：把"投票权数学"错当成"影响力物理"
节目里最反常识的一段，是 Stubbings 指出：**如果你根本拿不到男足世界杯、女足世界杯又

item.title: [投资TALK君] 芯片存储大屠杀，跑路还是抄底？财报大杂烩
item.dc:creator: 投资TALK君
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、把「割肉美光」放进整段独白里，你会发现一个被忽略的元交易模式

投资TALK君今天最真诚的一段，不是关于芯片周期，而是他反复回到同一个动作："我把ISRG清了，我把AMD换了Intel，我把Oracle换Microsoft的仓要考虑，我把美光加了但知道错了不加了"。

如果把这些换仓动作抽出来做时间序列，你会发现一个惊人的、他自己都没明说的规律：他不是在"买卖股票"，而是在不断替自己的"认知敞口"做再平衡。基本面变差→清仓ISRG（不是止损，是"取消订阅"这家公司）；对Oracle判断更清晰→用Microsoft换Oracle（不是加仓，是"迁移信念"）。

这个模式的反常识之处在于——大多数散户把仓位当作对股票的承诺，而他把仓位当作对自己判断的租约。租约到期（判断变了）就退租，不管有没有亏损。这解释了为什么他能反复强调"割肉不重要，认知才重要"——因为在他的操作系统里，割肉根本不是损失事件，而是信息更新事件。

二、他讲「因子失效」时暴露了一个跨领域的深层规律：任何被命名的东西都会失效

他随口提到的那家量化基金研究——"当越来越多人使用某个因子，因子本身就失去了预测力，退化为纯粹的风险衡量工具"——这句话如果你只把它当量化知识听，你就错过了它最锋利的地方。

这是Goodhart定律在金融市场的完整形态："当一个指标成为目标，它就不再是好的指标。" 但更惊人的跨领域连接是——这条规律在语言学里叫"符号的能指化"，在传播学里叫"meme的稀释"，在人类学里叫"仪式的空洞化"。

举一个你绝对没想到的连接：为什么"AI Agent"这个词最近突然让所有人反感？因为它正在经历和"动量因子"一模一样的死亡过程——从一个有预测力的概念（能识别真正的技术突破），退化为一个人人都能贴的标签（失去了区分能力）。Coding会被商品化，"AI Agent"这个词本身也会被商品化。TALK君今天反复讲coding会commoditize，但他没意识到自己讲的这套框架同样适用于所有热词，包括他自己正在使用的所有KOL话术。

三、最出人意料的观察：他今天讲得最好的是Uber，但他把Uber放在了错的框架里

他讲Uber用AI提升operating leverage，讲Working Pod，讲Forward Deployment Engineer——这些他都零散讲了，但他自己都没意识到这三件事拼在一起是什么。

它们是同一个东西的三个面：AI落地不是"技术问题"，是"组织形态问题"。 Uber的Working Pod、软件公司的FDE、Anthropic的Sonnet被Codex追赶——这三件事共同指向一个被市场严重误读的真相：AI这一轮真正的护城河不在模型，也不在算力，而在"AI-native的组织结构"。

这里有一个极其反常识的推论：如果这个假设成立，那么未来最值钱的不是模型公司，也不是Hyperscaler，而是那些能把AI"嵌入进别人组织"的中间层公司——Palantir的FDE模式、Accenture的咨询壳、甚至Uber自己内部孵化的"AI转型方法论"，都可能成为下一轮的稀缺资产。

跨领域连接：这和19世纪末电气化的历史惊人一致——真正赚钱的不是发电机制造商（西门子、通用电气最终利润率有限），而是教工厂如何用电重新设计流水线的咨询公司和电气工程师群体（Frederick Taylor的科学管理运动的经济基础就是这个）。TALK君今天嘴里的"FDE"，本质上就是AI时代的Frederick Taylor。

所以今天这场直播里最被低估的一句话不是"美光是不是周期股"，而是他一句带过的"Uber可能是第一家真正看到operating leverage的公司"——这句话如果是真的，那它对整个二级市场估值框架的冲击，比任何一次芯片下跌都要深远。因为它意味着"AI增效"从叙事变成了财报里可量化的margin expansion，而这才是市场真正能定价的东西。

Of course, if you know more about this company, you should know what I'm talking about. It's because it has a prior year development.

What does that mean? You can understand it as an insurance company, including banks, they all have a prepaid fund. Prepaid funds are for future losses. You can understand that last quarter, he was too prepared. In other words, the loss he expected last quarter was not that much. So, he released the loss he expected last quarter. This is called prior. It's about the previous correction. The previous part of the loss, the part of the loss expected to be lost, after correction, it all becomes profit.

It's the same as the bank releasing reserves. The reserve and the prepayment is the same in the bank releasing bad debt. Because your prepayment and your reserve is for the future bad debt and loss. The bank is the same. For the future compensation, because every Because every piece of the bank will have a future loss rate or a calculation price. Calculation loss is there. It means that the calculation loss was originally wrong. But actually, the calculation loss, the calculated loss, the calculated expected loss, has a very big factor of human nature in it. It can be fixed by human nature. Human nature can be judged. I'm judging. Of course, the model gives the answer. After giving the answer, it always has a factor of human nature.

So I don't want to say that they are manipulating this financial report, but I want to say that the insurance company wants to adjust the financial report for one or two seasons, and it is completely capable of adjusting it. It only needs to adjust this prior year development. What I mentioned, If you know this industry, or if you have experience in insurance companies, you will know that there is a lever in insurance companies to pull. In a marketplace, you find the right match, and you find the right restaurant, so the match You ask your agent to send a paper to the server, and then the server goes to the restaurant to get the food, and then they deliver it. But Uber not only connects to this service, but also connects to these three main bodies, and provides a lot of services and insurance. These things are likely to become a part of these platforms, including the contract. There are many contractual part-time jobs behind Airbnb. These things, it's impossible to complete it with just one agent. So it's not very clear, but I've seen some risks. But recently, the landfall of AI on Uber seems to be pretty good in terms of efficiency. This is the only thing I've been talking about with Uber lately. This is my recent understanding.

关于苹果、DRAM 减仓的方法论

How long will it take to clean up the Korean green card? I don't know. I really don't know.

Apple recommends continuing to eat oil. The problem with Apple's recommendation to continue to eat oil is actually the same as the previous one, whether to keep it or not. I don't think this is a good problem. It doesn't exist. Why is there no option to reduce the number of people who eat oil? Right? You either clean the warehouse or continue to eat oil. Apple is now stubbornly breaking a new high. I know it's stubborn now. I went to see it in particular. Apple's stubbornness is a high of 2020. It's very high now. 20.6 is the high position. As Jia Guiwen said, Many people ask if I should cut out the beauty and DRAM. I know some of the newer viewers may not understand my whole thinking framework and just want to get the answer from me. I may not have the answer. If you ask me if I should cut out DRAM, I really don't have the answer. But someone asked me yesterday, I think it's the US light. In the video yesterday, he asked if US light should be cut. My answer is that if your warehouse is big, you should cut it. You should cut it at this position. Whether you cut it at a low price, I think you should cut it. No matter where you buy it, I think cutting it at this position is a very reasonable choice. If your warehouse is very big. Because if you make a wrong judgment, and you are worried about it, it means your psychological tolerance is no longer enough to tolerate the warehouse. In this position, of course, you can judge from other technical aspects whether it is in the low position or not. You can also start from this perspective. But from my perspective, I think that once there is a problem with your mentality, your position will definitely be in trouble. At this time, what you should do immediately is to take some positions first. You don't have to consider how much loss you are losing now. You can take this position because you want to take this loss as your future experience. It is an experience. You can leave the field if you lose. That's what I think.

下半年 10% 回报的预期与 CoreWeave

This question is about whether I still think there will be 10% return in the future. If so, then it's possible to have a 78% vacancy in August. I share my point of view and my opinion, but you will find that most people still do other things with my point of view. And I think the fall... I set the stage first. If I think it will fall, I actually plan to... Because I know that the market will fall. No matter what happens, in the second half of the year, because there are two backslides in the market in a year, It's too normal. This is something that will happen in normal markets. So this is what happens within the seven generations. My expectation is that if there are some rebound in big technologies, if the valuation is relatively high, I can cut some big technologies. Because the core of the U.S. stock market, if you want to make money in the long term, you stay in the market. I said it 10,000 times. Stay in the market is the main reason why you can make money in the long term in the U.S. stock market. Then use index as your core, and then each stock as a satellite. CBRS is interesting. After listening to the talk, I think it's interesting. I actually had a lot of conversations with AI that day. I talked about my views and the market research he did for me. But we haven't reached that point yet. In the future, the demand for speed in the application, the demand for speed in inference, will be very high. Of course, it depends on what I do. But I think the demand for speed in agent will be very high. I think there is room for speed there. As for whether the big model can run, because many big models now use MOE. That's why Zerobus has used the OpenAI model to run it last time. I think there is room for it there. I'm just waiting for them to solve it and wait for a more reasonable solution. to see if we can get a more reasonable price. Last time, I said that the price was at 35 billion. I think it's a reasonable position. Then it rebounded to more than 200. Then someone said, "Look at this rebound." I said, "It will definitely fall anyway." Because there is still a problem with the settlement. I think if we can start to slowly set up below 30 billion, this company is absolutely worth it. Because you are If you start with 30 branches and start building 20-odd branches at a average price, I think it will have a lot of potential. Because as time goes by, I think agents will definitely fall. That's a matter of time. After agents fall, and now in the reasoning part, there are already some Because once the IQ reaches a certain level, the problem that the model can solve, there is already enough demand for speed. It's a speed requirement when you give me a reasoning. So I think Syribus has this application. Again, for Grok, Invida's acquisition is the best for Sylvester. It's a matter of price. I've been paying attention to this company. I'll see if there's a good opportunity after it's done. I'll share the points I bought with you before.

Many people say that the CPO and storage are out, but will there be a positive rebound if it falls like this? When it rises, storage is the future, light is the future. Standing under storage and light, we can definitely go to the other side. How do I say it? I don't know. I'll talk about it later. Now the price has fallen, storage is out, CPO is out. The price decides the story line.

But there is a bit of a... It takes a lot. because the leverage can reach that position. It's not a one-day thing. So after I finished the leverage, there might be an event that has a flush out, and then it needs to keep overlapping the basic surface, and then it will slowly come out. So don't expect many trademarks to appear in a V-shaped reversal in a month. I think a V-shaped reversal is a very small-value event, but it doesn't mean that these trademarks are totally unworthy of investment. So don't expect it to bounce back immediately. I don't think that's reasonable.

What are the advantages of the US stock? I think it's a good thing for big tech and these hyper-scalers, these technology companies that are doing infrastructure. Because they sell the basic algorithm of the bottom layer. They sell services on this basis. There's an added service. Of course, it's not necessarily a good thing for two big model companies. Think about it from a long-term perspective. If the token price is used on the scale of AI agent, is it a good or bad thing for these data-based companies? It's definitely a good thing because they sell the underlying algorithm and sell an ad service on the underlying algorithm. Then the margin can reach 30% to 40%. Without the support of these algorithms, you won't have an agent, you won't have an AI smart body on this scale. This is the most direct result.

So I think, including the previous talk, about whether the US dollar can go out of a non-weekly segment, I think it doesn't make sense. You talk about whether the US dollar can go out of a week, but you don't bring money to big tech. If big tech doesn't have ROI, how can the US dollar go out of the week? Who gives you money to go out of the week? Are big tech fools? Without ROI, they will give you so much money. So it's impossible for the market to do a lot of big technology and then do a lot of US light. Even think that US light is a North American-style technology. This is unreasonable. I think the market will face this problem. I'm not a brainless AI. So I think the whole market needs to keep updating the basics. Where will it go? What kind of colorless will appear in the future? I think the next problem will be the landing. And the landing should be big enough.

下一个"够大"的落地场景：软件市场与 FDE

The reason why so many companies are going to FOMO Coding is because... I mean large companies are going to FOMO Coding to take over the market. It's because the Coding market is huge. Coding is the symbol of the operating cost of the US tech company of $20 billion. So the market is huge. And now, even if you look at the ARR of Anthropic, it's only $50 billion. I gave an example yesterday. I said, even if you can take over a quarter of the market of $20 billion, it's only $5 billion. The market of US$5 billion. 1/10, right? And Zuby has just taken 1/10 of the market. So the market will have a bigger share.

That's the next landing. The next landing needs to be big enough. And the next landing, your buyers, the buyers who buy your API, they need to have money. They need to be willing to spend. Which market is that big? Which market? I actually have thought about it. You can think about it. Which market do you think is that big? Actually, I think this is also what I think in a situation where the price of tokens is so high, I think the software market, just like what Huang talked about, the application market, is actually very big in the software market. And the software market itself has a very large market share. Through these software markets, through the price reduction of tokens, the AI services added to the software market become attractive.

I have mentioned this example on the second live broadcast. I said, I was thinking about a question myself. One of my expectations is that The next is software. In fact, we haven't seen a very obvious increase in software and AI-related AR in the first quarter of this year. The volume is still very small, but it will grow. As the volume grows, I expect that... Because the volume of the software market itself is very large, It's bigger than the coding market, the entire American software market. It's bigger than the coding market. There's so much behind it. All companies need software. All companies have an IT budget in software. In the case of IT budget, they can spend 10% of their budget. Are they willing to? They are willing to. As long as you can provide the utility of the additional function of AI, you can provide value there. And they have the current PyPath.

So, I'm looking forward to As the model gets better, and as these software companies add AI to their current products, as long as the product is well-made, I believe that it will be similar to the current version of Anthropo, where the coding can be loved by so many big tech companies. I actually expect software companies to make AI the same as the token of the technology company FOMO coding. so that ordinary companies can also feel the effect of AI on their workflow.

That's why many software companies have now sent a new work group called Forward Deployment Engineer. It's similar to FDE, Forward Deployment Engineer. The company itself wants to send send an engineer to the company and tell them what AI functions we have added to our software, and then understand the operating program of the company itself. It's similar to what Appalachian Palant does. It helps them to combine AI better into their workflow. It's very necessary. How would a normal company know? I don't know your functions, so I don't know how to combine them.

So I'm actually looking forward to this. But the problem is with B2B, There are direct channels of consumption, but it takes time. B2B has always been slower to accept new technologies. Unlike tech companies, they are very willing to embrace new technologies. But ordinary companies don't. But once I see these FTE engineers, they can make like sales or implementation, I expect that for ordinary companies, the AI functions that are added to existing software will also be similar to the technology companies' FOMO and CI for coding.

What will happen is that the technology companies, as we go down the whole stage, token maxing, how many tokens you can burn, how many tokens you can burn on the coding, how many tokens you can burn, until the token budget. At the beginning, I found that I burned too much after burning for three months. In the end, I found that I could burn more tokens after I came in with these open-end models, but I could keep my spending unchanged. In the same way, I could burn more tokens and solve more problems under the same spending. How to model optimization? This is the stage that software companies are currently experiencing. This is the stage that technology companies are currently experiencing. I expect that ordinary companies will also experience such a stage. From the beginning, they felt that AI was so powerful and could help them solve so many problems. They could do a lot of improvement in their real-life work. They were willing to spend money, but they thought that you can only help me do some lift-up and then just get some OPEX. Some departments may be able to get rid of it, and use AI functions to replace it. But I found that there was no way to directly help me increase my revenue. So after spending this stage, I started to have control over CODs and tokens. Only after controlling it, will we realize that there are other options. Open source models may also help us do this.

I think Clam may go on the same path as the same technology company. I'm looking forward to it because the market is very big. Once the market is up, for these companies behind it, whether it's Hyperscaler or Neocloud, it's a good thing for these companies that do the calculation. Including including for the model companies that are closed and open source. Because they have a very large application market. So I think it's very unreasonable for Anthropic to do this on its own. Because it can't do this in the software market. Because it doesn't have this generation, it can't form this platform in the software market. the company will take the initiative to seal the model. It won't. Because there is no clear path. Right? There is no clear path or a clear replacement. A general company won't take the initiative to think about an experience, to think about something and say, "I'm going to try it now. I'm going to use Antwerp and look at Cloud, and see if Fable can replace a certain workflow, and then spend a lot of money on research." It won't do that. It won't take the initiative to do this. Normal companies won't do that. So I think it's not reasonable for Anthropi to do this. The most reasonable thing is to take the performance of the model and then other companies integrate your model into their products. It's the most reasonable way to do it. And it's the fastest way.

左侧分批建仓的具体问答

Okay, because of time, I'll leave the message to the end. I have a question. Usually, yes. Usually, yes. Because if you have a target range, usually, yes. If the price rises, - I think it's possible. Unless you have a lot of stock in your first stock. But I don't recommend doing that. Instead of buying it first and then considering it. If it falls, I'll make up for it. If it doesn't fall, I won't. I don't think... I don't usually do that.

Robinhood. Isn't Robinhood quite strong recently? Robinhood, from... Last time, I said in the app that Robinhood was a strong position before 94 yuan. After breaking the 94 yuan mark, if there's a chance of a comeback, it'll be a better one. It will be a relatively good trend. And during that time, Bitcoin was actually very weak and was always breaking through. Robinhood has actually come out of a low position and started to rebound. Because although Bitcoin's currency circle is relatively weak, its other services should not be too bad.

Robinhood has a huge advantage in the future. You know Trump account, right? There will be more than 10 million accounts in the future. And the 10 million accounts can't be put forward. They are for the children. Robinhood is in charge of opening accounts. Next generation. Next generation wealth.

Do you know why banks... Do you know why banks give you money when you open a bank? Banks give you a lot of perks. Sometimes they give you an iPad, sometimes they give you a gift card worth hundreds of dollars. Do you know why they do that? Because they know that once you open a bank there, you are their lifetime customer. They will definitely earn more money than the amount they give you now, 300 or 400 dollars. That's why for these brokerage accounts, That's why I often talk to people like Futo Momo, who open their accounts and give you these subsidies. It's the same thing. If they give you 100 yuan or 200 yuan today, it's not a big deal. In the future, you will have more money to trade on their platform.

He has the money for the baby, and he can't trade it. He can't trade it until the 18-year-old can. That's a very valuable sum of money. Because the long term of the Belp is going up. This is a great advantage for Robinhood in the future. We're not talking about five or ten years later. This is a long-term advantage. He has the younger generation and the baby generation. But he has the next two generations. The current generation and the next generation. This is a very long-term advantage. You can't completely set the price now, but you will find that the AUM of Robinhood will continue to grow. For these exchanges, the AUM's continued growth is very important. Because AUM multiplied by its rate, you basically know how much it is.

Big technology is changing the upstream and downstream oil that has fallen a lot. If there are no upstream and downstream oil companies, and now big technology is changing some upstream and downstream oil, I think it's okay. Even though the treasure of IBM is not the software, how do you see the entire software block? Is it better to look at hardware or software? You have to really look at the software block. I don't think the software block is a good measure. It's too general. For example, the cybersecurity in the software block is better than hardware. It doesn't even have a downfall. After IBM's treasure came out, the software block was down. The IGV was down, but the cybersecurity was up. Is cybersecurity a software block? It's a kind of a stream of software blocks. So I think software blocks should be looked at separately.

I... I still have a few opinions. I insist on soft-script dual-script. If I can pick some of my better components in software blocks and then hardware, I still think I can continue to use it. From the perspective of the hardware block, I think it's a low-end. If you look at the hardware block, it's in the center.

Robinhood's stock. I'll update it. Tomorrow's video, Friday, Friday, Friday. If I have time, I'll record a video for you tomorrow. I'll update the stock tomorrow. If I don't have time, I'll update it on Sunday.

Oracle vs ServiceNow 的选择、Figma 的观察

Oracle and Now. I'm considering building a warehouse. I'm considering Oracle and ServiceNow. There are too many. I'm thinking about another one. When I add too many labels, my shares will probably reach 18. I think there are too many. One, it affects the management. Two, it's not very meaningful if it's too scattered. At one point, I might do consolidation. But now, it's a bit scattered. I won't consider ServiceNow because I have Adobe. I won't consider ServiceNow. Although it's not in a certain industry, I still picked one out of the software version. I also have Microsoft. I thought about it. I might have to leave Microsoft in the software version industry. Oracle might be the one I want to use. So I said I would use Microsoft to replace Oracle. Because Oracle and Adobe are about 13 or 14 nodes in total. It's not small.

Figma may become a breakthrough in software applications. Jason from All In Podcast recently started to prepare Figma. Even the CEO of Figma thinks they won't be destroyed. I haven't heard much about them. I only hear about them from the news. I haven't heard much about their reports. If they have time, I can go and listen to their reports.

Forward PE 数据来源与工具推荐

Okay. Someone asked me if there are any good websites that can replace the forward PE table that shares the middle of the spectrum. I really didn't find it. I didn't search it on the website specifically, but if you have it, you can leave it in the comments. We can share it together. I have asked... I have an app that will update soon. It's my own app. I'm going to change the version soon. There are a lot of problems now. For example, it's lagging. It's slower to open. I've been wanting to get them to make a report. I said, "You should change this." The app will immediately update. After that, it will be fully updated. After that, it will add some functions that are not very useful. Another factor that will be added is something that everyone is always concerned about. I want to add Belk500, NDX, and SOX. I want them to all be added. After adding them, I want to reconcile them. I want to see how much difference there is between them and what I can see from the spreadsheet.

I always think that no matter which website you look at, the result may be different. Because forward P is a result calculated by human beings. It's the result that the institution expects. On the top of the scale, they put all these institutions' results together and made a middle position. So if you go to any website, if it doesn't have all the data behind it, it won't have a result like Pengbo. But I don't think Pengbo's result is the truth. No, it's not. But no matter what you look at, the more institutions you adopt, the better. Because if you adopt only one institution, your result will be very biased. But if there are some institutions behind it, and they support the forward PE you calculated, there are certain reference points. More importantly, it's the horizontal comparison.

I think that's the most important thing. For example, the SOX I see today, this graph shows that it's 21.9 times, 21.94 times. The highest point is 30x. If you see other platforms at 28x, which is now at 18x, then you don't compare 18x with 29x. There's no point. You compare 18x with 28x on that platform. What is 18x and 28x? It's a relative position. So I think there are references on that platform. As long as you can find the forward P on other websites. But I don't know if there are forward P on other stocks. I believe you can find it on other websites. You can check out Fingertips. You can try it out. Everyone can try it out.

What is this? Coifing. I don't know. I haven't tried it. It's also recommended by everyone. Coifing. It seems like many people use it for free. You can check it out. I'll check it out. If it's useful, I can recommend it to everyone in the video or chat with everyone. I know many people have some problems with this.

收尾：核心-卫星组合与开超市 vs 集中持仓

Finally, let's answer a question. I think this question is good. He said, I think it's better to open a supermarket or focus on a few stocks. Open a supermarket. You said open a supermarket. I thought you were really going to open a supermarket. You said you bought a lot of things at the supermarket, right? My question. I think it's better to open a supermarket and buy a lot of trademarks.

I think from a personal perspective, the core of satellite storage is index. Index can be divided into SPY and QQQ. I think QQQ must be a pair of indexes, not just around SPY. Because I think in the market, we make money from beta for a long time. And we should make money from beta. If we can't make money from beta, I don't think so. The so-called beta is when the market is rising, you can try to be more than the market. I say the market is when the BOP is rising, you can try to be more than the BOP. Where does the beta come from? From QQQ. From the technology sector.

In this case, I think SPY and QQQ can be a core warehouse. Then on this basis, if you don't have the time, you're not like me, right? At least in this industry, I work every day. Many times, making videos becomes a daily task. I have to prepare for the task I have to do. I have to contact many companies every day, read many companies' financial reports, and do a lot of market research. Without my experience and time, I personally think it's better to invest in this sector than 7 or more. If you reach the top, it's better than 10. Because you don't have time to follow up. You can't follow up with 10 headlines.

The so-called root of 10 headlines is that you need to know The simplest way is, for example, if I ask you today, how much profit margin does the company you buy have? How much is its revenue? If you don't know how much its profit margin is, how much is its profit margin, and how much is its PE, then you don't know. So it means that you don't know about this company at all. It's just because other places may think that you can buy this company. These are the most basic knowledge, including what the management said in the last quarter's financial report. Basically, I have all the companies, and I must see every quarter's financial report. I know what they talked about last season and what they will do next season. You must know these things. If you don't know, it means you don't have the ability to manage so many topics. just add 5 or 6 tags.

Once you have the core tags, these 5 or 6 tags won't take up too much space. For example, the core tag takes up 70%. You have 5 tags, so each tag takes up 6%. You can add 10% to the tags you like. 10% for a tag is actually not small enough for the overall investment group. Then on this basis, if QQQ guarantees beta, I'm just giving an example. I'm not saying this group is suitable for everyone, but I'm just giving an example. 40% of QQQ, 40% of SPY, 40% of QQQ, or 30% of SPY, 40% of QQQ, 70% of the warehouse will be gone. I'm only talking about the stock warehouse. If there is a debt-based requirement, that's your personal problem. You can arrange it elsewhere. I don't care about this. I don't care about asset allocation. I only care about the stock market. 40 SPY, 30 QQQ, and the remaining 30 are distributed to 6 units. One unit is a 5% share. Right? 6, 5, 6, 30. This 5% share is actually very good. If you can pick the 5 units better, if the 5 units can win the standard, then these 5 units will help you get Alpha. Then QQQ can help you get Beta.

With such a share, I believe even if you have empty 5 units, or empty 4 units, your overall investment group, you won't run out of 500-500. Unless your 5-5 stocks are all running some concept stocks or some NIM, then I can't do anything. But even if you buy NIM, your 30% of this range will not affect your remaining 70% too much. You may mark 10% at most, you do not do well 6%, 5%, at least you won't run too bad. I think this is the most important thing Basically, if you have a higher risk profile like me, you don't need to have a core 3-way, you can be a core. But if you are a core, you need to have time to follow up. I mentioned this before, for me, I think there are too many advantages over the 10, 8, and 19. Although most cores are already mastered by me, But I think there are still a lot of them. Because I think there are a lot of them. Maybe it's a problem with the amount of work in the future, or the number of meetings. Maybe it's too scattered. That's also a problem.

I strongly recommend that you take a look at the core satellite. Because after you master this thing, you can do whatever you want with the remaining 30% of what you have. Even if you mess it up, you won't have any anxiety like you did recently.

Okay, QQQ. The SOX fell 70 days ago. It's now falling again after the talk. QQQ just fell over 700. Okay, okay. That's it for today. If necessary, I'll make a video tomorrow. If the market falls more tomorrow, that's it for today. See you tomorrow. Bye.

## 一、把「割肉美光」放进整段独白里，你会发现一个被忽略的**元交易模式**
投资TALK君今天最真诚的一段，不是关于芯片周期，而是他反复回到同一个动作：**"我把ISRG清了，我把AMD换了Intel，我把Oracle换Microsoft的仓要考虑，我把美光加了但知道错了不加了"**。
如果把这些换仓动作抽出来做时间序列，你会发现一个**惊人的、他自己都没明说的规律**：他不是在"买卖股票"，而是在**不断替自己的"认知敞口"做再平衡**。基本面变差→清仓ISRG（不是止损，是"取消订阅"这家公司）；对Oracle判断更清晰→用Microsoft换Oracle（不是加仓，是"迁移信念"）。
这个模式的反常识之处在于——**大多数散户把仓位当作对股票的承诺，而他把仓位当作对自己判断的租约**。租约到期（判断变了）就退租，不管有没有亏损。这解释了为什么他能反复强调"割肉不重要，认知才重要"——因为在他的操作系统里，割肉根本不是损失事件，而是**信息更新事件**。
## 二、他讲「因子失效」时暴露了一个跨领域的深层规律：**任何被命名的东西都会失效**
他随口提到的那家量化基金

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] Putin's Playbook – Sarah Paine
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、地缘政治的"负外部性引擎"：为什么强者身边总是废墟

表面上看，Paine 描述的是俄罗斯的"顺序吞并"战略——一次消化一个邻居，制造缓冲区，等待时机。但真正令人震撼的洞察藏在她随口提及的一句话里："环顾俄罗斯和中国，它们被地球上一些最功能失调的地方所包围。是运气不好，还是它们本身就是共谋？"

这里揭示了一个反直觉的系统性真相：大陆型霸权不是"恰好"生活在废墟中，废墟是它生产出来的副产品。这就像生态学里的"顶级掠食者悖论"——为了维持自身的生态位，它必须持续制造周边的"生态贫瘠"。健康的邻居是威胁，只有失败国家才是安全的缓冲。

这带来一个跨领域的意外类比：这与平台经济中的"焦土策略"几乎同构——某些超级平台通过持续压低生态位内其他玩家的生存能力（而非直接消灭）来维持不可替代性。俄罗斯对乌克兰做的事、腾讯早年对独立开发者做的事、Meta 对新兴社交产品做的事——在结构上是同一种"生态位维稳"逻辑。

二、陀思妥耶夫斯基的"东方主义"：文学作为帝国潜意识的泄露

Paine 引用陀思妥耶夫斯基那段"在欧洲我们是奴才，在亚洲我们是主人"的日记，是整段演讲最耐人寻味的一击。

这里有一个大多数俄罗斯文学爱好者从未察觉的黑暗结构：《罪与罚》、《卡拉马佐夫兄弟》里那种对救赎、屈辱、被拯救的痴迷，其实是一种"帝国自卑—帝国补偿"的精神机制。当一个文明在西方面前感到二等，它就必须在东方创造出属于自己的"更低等的他者"来维持心理平衡。

跨领域连接来了：这与心理学中的"踢猫效应"（displaced aggression）在民族尺度上的呈现完全一致。陀思妥耶夫斯基不是在写游记，他是在为整个俄罗斯民族的心理位移提供哲学许可证。而更令人不安的是——Klyuchevsky 那句"俄罗斯是一个正在殖民自身的国家"，说明这种位移最终会转向内部：当外部扩张受阻，同样的暴力逻辑会向国内的社会阶层实施。布尔什维克对整个贵族阶级的清洗，本质上是对外扩张受挫后的"殖民能量"的内收。

三、一个惊人的观察：连续型帝国没有"止损函数"

Paine 提出了一个几乎无人注意的关键机制缺陷——"没有辞职委员会"（no council on when to quit）。这句轻描淡写的话是整个演讲的隐藏核弹。

海洋型帝国（英国、荷兰、美国）有天然的止损机制：股东、议会、财政周期、商船公司的资产负债表。赔钱的殖民地会被"卖掉"。但大陆型帝国的扩张成本是隐藏的、递延的、由沉默的农民承担的，因此它从未收到"停止扩张"的价格信号，直到系统性崩溃。

这里有一个让人后背发凉的跨领域映射：这与现代 AI 系统中的"奖励黑客"（reward hacking）在数学上同构。一个没有对齐机制的智能体，会不断优化其单一指标（领土/参数量/用户数），直到系统因为忽略了未被计价的外部成本而突然崩溃。普京不是一个失败的战略家，他是一个"对齐失败"的智能体——他的效用函数里没有"何时停止"这一项。

而更深的洞察是：中国和俄罗斯历史上反复的王朝崩溃循环，可能不是"腐败"或"外敌"造成的，而是这个缺失的止损函数在起作用。它们不是被打败的，它们是被自己缺失的反馈回路噎死的。这也解释了为什么 Paine 说 "there are no enduring alliances in this world"——因为任何理性的邻居最终都会意识到：与一个没有停止函数的实体结盟，等于把自己的存活概率外包给对方的内部崩溃时刻。

📝 转录文本

大陆型安全范式的游戏规则

In this world, there are certain rules for playing this game.

Rule number one: no two-front wars. I've shown you all the neighbors. If they gang up on you, it will be game over — in a very bad way — for you.

Secondly: no great power neighbors. Why? Because today's friend can be tomorrow's foe. And that spells trouble.

So, what do you do? You take on your neighbors sequentially, like one at a time. You set them up to fail, you destabilize the rising, you adjust the failing, and you set up buffer zones in between, and you await the opportune moment to pounce and absorb.

This is Vladimir Putin's game.

"豺狼国家"策略

Better yet, you get the neighbors to do the work for you. How? You sow their mutual resentments, you get them to fight each other, you deluge them with fake news — so that Russia can play the role of the jackal state: once the neighbors have sufficiently weakened themselves and weakened each other, Russia can move on in and steal a kill made by others.

这一范式的内在缺陷

This theory of security has certain problems.

One, you're surrounding yourself by failing states. You can look at Russia and China, and they're surrounded by some of the most dysfunctional places on the planet, and you can go, "Gee whiz, are they unlucky? Are they complicit?"

And moreover, there are no enduring alliances in this world, because the neighbors figure it out. The hegemonic power is just a long-term problem.

And there's also no council on when to quit. Like, how much territory do you take before you choke on it? And it turns out that there's been a tendency over the course of China's and Russia's long histories to overextend. And this helps account for the implosions of various empires and dynasties.

但请勿低估其历史有效性

Now, before you dismiss this security paradigm, understand: it was very effective. Certainly prior to the Industrial Revolution, no one cared in this world about collateral damage or killing a bunch of innocent people. This paradigm is all about killing people, breaking things, to take territory.

And you've seen it operating in real time in your own lives — in Syria, Ukraine, and other places. This is why those ancient ruins, the ones that still remain, are ruinous — because this sort of warfare is ruinous.

三段俄国精英的自白

All right. Here is one of the preeminent historians of the late Tsarist period, Vasily Klyuchevsky, who's describing his country. He said:

"The history of Russia is a history of a country in process of colonizing itself. Her area of colonization grows in tandem with her national territory — at times shrinking, at times expanding — age-old movement. Therefore, the periods in our history are the stages which our people have gone through in the occupation and development of the land acquired by them."

There is absolutely no mention of the people who actually live on this land. It's like irrelevant from his point of view. But this is the way Russians have long looked at things: that if you have small neighbors, someone big may take them over. And even worse, if the weak become strong in their own right, it's dangerous. So you want to keep moving outwards and expanding.

And here, a generation earlier, you have Fyodor Dostoevsky — the novelist, the author of Crime and Punishment and other light reads. Here he is confiding in his diary:

"In Europe we are hangers-on and slaves, whereas we shall go to Asia as masters. In Europe we are Asiatics, whereas in Asia we too are Europeans. Our civilizing mission in Asia will bribe our spirit and drive us thither."

So this idea of empire is very deeply rooted in Russian thinking.

And here is Count Sergei Witte. He's probably the most distinguished public servant in the late Tsarist period. He was a finance minister. And he's looking at the world in 1903 and he goes:

"Look, the problem for Russia is to obtain as large a share as possible of the outlived oriental states."

And he's thinking of the Ottoman Empire, but particularly of China. And he goes:

"Look, Russia historically, geographically has the undisputed right to the lion's share of the expected prey. And this elemental movement of Russia began a long time ago with Ivan the Terrible, aptly named. And the absorption by Russia of a considerable portion of the Chinese Empire is only a question of time — unless the Chinese get their act together."

So here you have a statesman at the highest levels of the Russian government who is looking at Russian national security — it's all about territorial expansion.

普京，以及大陆型战略的终极风险

Well, Putin also looks at the world this way, apparently.

The problem for the likes of Putin is: if a continental power botches strategy, its known world can vanish forever. And this is what happened to Imperial China, Imperial Russia, and many venerable civilizations.

I love this photograph of an aristocrat's whimsical palace that's long gone — as a result of one of the most horrific genocides of the 20th century, when the communists made good their promise to get rid of entire social classes. And I mean, get rid of — like, gone forever. With the Bolshevik Revolution and the ensuing Russian Civil War, followed by collectivization, and then the Great Purges that finished the job.

This is the continental world. It is a world without insurance policies.

Okay, now for the maritime world.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [YC Root Access] Building the Agentic AI Platform for Hospitals
item.dc:creator: YC Root Access
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、真正的产品不是 AI，而是"让说'试试看'的成本趋近于零"

大多数人以为 Bunker Hill 卖的是医疗 AI，其实他们卖的是组织决策的边际成本。传统医院里，一次"要不要用某个 AI 工具"的决策，绑定着两年的采购、IT、合规、变革管理——所以每个"是"都必须是慎重的、经过任务小组反复审议的、押上职业信誉的"是"。这种高成本迫使决策者不得不用"重要性排序"来筛选想法，而重要性排序本身就是创新的杀手，因为真正颠覆性的想法在早期看起来总是不够重要。

Bunker Hill 真正做的事情，是把"说 Yes 的成本"从两年降到两周。一旦这个成本足够低，医院的决策模式就会从"审议模式"切换到"实验模式"。这不是一个 AI 故事，而是一个制度经济学故事——就像 AWS 之于软件创业，把"启动一个想法"从需要买服务器、租机房，压缩到刷信用卡的十分钟。医疗 AI 的"AWS 时刻"就是他们真正在下的赌注。

二、意外的观察：LLM 让"不酷的问题"变成了"酷问题"的通行证

这是整个访谈里最反直觉的一段。研究者过去只做"酷"的临床问题——心脏病预测、癌症筛查——因为每个模型都要从零训练，你必须选值得投入几年的问题。结果是：医院金字塔顶端的"启蒙问题"被过度研究，底层的行政、账单、转诊排队等"无聊问题"完全没人碰。

而 Mish 揭示了一个几乎所有 AI 创业者都没看透的悖论：恰恰是解决了那些"不酷"的底层问题（账单、转诊、Medicaid 资格审核），"酷"的问题才有可能被采纳。因为医院的采购、IT、变革管理带宽是共享的稀缺资源。你只有帮医院把底层的水管铺好，他们才有余力去关心顶层的诊断创新。

这里有一个跨领域连接可能会让你意外：这个模式和日本战后制造业的"改善（Kaizen）"哲学几乎完全同构。丰田不是靠一次革命性发明超越美国车企，而是靠把无数微小的、不酷的流程改进堆积起来。硅谷 AI 圈今天迷恋"基础模型的下一次飞跃"，但 Bunker Hill 正在证明：医疗 AI 的胜负手在"复利式的微小自动化"，不在"英雄式的诊断突破"。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：他们的组织结构就是他们的产品哲学的复印件

留意一个几乎被访谈者一带而过的细节——21 个人的公司，工程师只做平台层，前端部署团队是"聪明但非技术的"人。这不是效率选择，这是哲学一致性。

Bunker Hill 相信"知识—推理—行动"三个 primitives 可以吸收整个医疗行业的复杂性。如果他们真的相信这一点，那么他们自己的公司架构就必须证明这一点：如果连他们自己都需要工程师针对每个客户写代码，就等于承认自己的抽象是漏的。

这里藏着一个更深的洞察，跨到语言学里去：Chomsky 的生成语法认为，人类有限的语法规则可以生成无限的句子。Bunker Hill 在做的，本质上是为医疗流程发明一套"生成语法"——21 个人的团队之所以能服务数十家医院、几百个用例，就是因为他们相信自己找到了医疗操作的"深层结构"。工程师维护语法本身，前端部署团队负责用这套语法"造句"。

这也解释了为什么他们敢押注在"团队规模保持极小"这件事上——一个真正找到正确抽象的公司，扩张的应该是 token 消耗，而不是人头。这句话（Ankur 半开玩笑说出来的）其实是本次访谈里被严重低估的一句预言：它可能是下一波 AI 原生公司的组织学新范式——员工数与营收脱钩，token 消耗与营收挂钩。传统 SaaS 的"每美元 ARR 需要多少员工"这个基准，可能在 AI 原生公司里要被"每美元 ARR 需要多少 token"取代。

四、一个额外的隐藏结构：创始人的"火鸡骨"就是他自己的父亲

访谈里 Mish 用"火鸡骨病人"讲了 Bunker Hill 的完整价值链——病人为一个无关的原因来到急诊，CT 扫描顺便发现冠状动脉阻塞，AI 触发一整套后续照护。而故事的另一端，是 Mish 自己的父亲——他花了几年时间试图部署一个能预防心脏病的模型，几乎放弃，然后父亲在 Abu Dhabi 心脏病发作，医生做 CT 时正好发现了他模型能识别的那种斑块。

这不是巧合，这是创始人-市场契合度（founder-market fit）的极端形式：他要解决的问题在他放弃之前，先在他自己家里发作了。硅谷谈论"founder-market fit"通常是软性的经验相关性，但 Mish 的案例接近于存在意义上的绑定——他做这件事的意义结构里，藏着他不做这件事的代价。这种绑定极难在 pitch deck 上看出来，但它往往是长期抗周期的最深护城河。

📝 转录文本

开场与融资公布

[music]

Today we're really excited to have Mish Khandwala from Bunker Hill Health. Thanks so much for joining us. Thanks for having me, Ankur. Mish, you have a exciting announcement to share today. Why don't we kick things off with that? Yes, absolutely. Uh we are very, very excited to announce our Series B today. Uh we have raised $55 million thus far. Uh this round was led by Vinod Khosla at Khosla Ventures. And joining this round is obviously Y Combinator. Uh also Alfred Lin from Sequoia. Uh Octum Ventures, Felicis, and this all will go into building the Agentic AI platform for health systems. Um we think that this is the way how AI gets adopted in the future and I'm excited to share more about what we're up to.

Bunker Hill Health 在做什么：降低医疗行业的"迭代成本"

So, tell us a little bit about Bunker Hill Health. Um so, maybe to begin, what does Bunker Hill Health do today? Like, give us the full span of kind of what the company does.

Absolutely. We are really obsessed about lowering the cost of iteration in healthcare. I think healthcare has this reputation of it's hard to move the needle in or it takes a long time to get something done. We are really obsessed about changing that. How can we make the speed of iteration faster? How can we make the cost of iterations less?

So, what do I mean by that? Um I'll give you two sides. Let's take the side of an executive at a health system, at a hospital, where this executive is responsible for all AI initiatives. And from their point of view, what's going on? In their shoes, they're thinking, "Wow, there's so much potential of AI. I think we're well past that, like, oh, is there potential of AI or not?" Like, it is so obvious that there is so much potential to make better patient care happen, drive additional revenue, save cost, drive business objectives. There's so much low-hanging fruit even. But, it doesn't seem from their standpoint that there's a viable pathway to actually capturing that potential.

So, like they're presumably, you know, they're already using ChatGPT in their personal lives or whatever. Like they're aware that AI is amazing, but they don't necessarily see how to incorporate it in the hospital setting.

Yeah. So, again, if you're let's let's say I'm the that physician um or that executive at the health system responsible for AI initiatives, I have people coming to me left and right across the organization. I have the chief of vascular surgery coming to me saying, "I want an AI tool to track aneurysms so that we can deliver better patient care." Oh, and by the way, it also drive better business. So, great. I have the chief of radiology coming to me with uh with uh solutions that they want to adopt, the chief of cardiology, I have the revenue cycle team, the quality team, you name it. They're all coming to me with saying, "Hey, we want to adopt this AI tool." And in my position as an executive, again, this hypothetical scenario where I'm the executive at the health system uh responsible for AI, I am not questioning at all whether these AI tools will deliver or have the potential to deliver value. That the value proposition makes sense. It also like you can just see the benefit.

And the teams want it. Yes, the teams want it, too.

为什么部署一个 AI 工具要花两年

However, this was a bitter lesson that we learned through many years of working in this industry that the effort that it takes to onboard a new tool far outweighs the benefits. It's like you will spend 2 years trying to onboard a new tool and only for it to deliver one specific thing. Imagine if for every app you ever used on your iPhone, you had to buy a separate phone. Like how would that feel? Like it would just feel like, "Can I live without it?" Probably.

And why does it take 2 years to onboard one of these things? Like what were those sources of pain in these places?

It's because the barriers of entry are very high. Um if the health system saying is saying yes, we are interested, you need to get through procurement, you have IT security, you have data privacy, you have all the other sort of contracting and procurement related things that need to happen. You then actually need to get IT resources to implement the solution, and IT resources are shared across different departments. Everybody is sort of fighting for the same resource. Um you get your day in court at some point where you finally get IT resources. Then you have to go through change management.

Um there's just so much to be done where on paper it doesn't seem like a lot. It's like, why can't this be done in two, three months? But when you actually roll it out, it naturally takes a year plus to get that done.

So again, from that viewpoint of that physician or that executive at the health system responsible for AI, I am like, there is no way I can incorporate all of these tools. So it feels almost like I see the potential, but I do not know of a good way to capture it.

The insight there is that if you look underneath the hood of all of these sort of different solutions, they look very similar where they are extracting data from different systems of record.

Presumably EHRs or whatever.

Exactly. And then putting that through large language models. And as these large language models have gotten better and better, the requirement for fine-tuning or doing any additional machine learning work on top of it has also reduced. And so in that position, in that hypothetical position where I'm the executive, I am thinking, why am I going to buy so many different solutions? It's not so much the economic cost. guys have built integrations to all of these things.

Exactly. And so when we get into a health system, we lead with the idea of a platform. Like we have this concept that a platform should have three pillars, knowledge, reasoning, and action.

Knowledge is how we obtain information in. So that's those are the connectors that are doing the read activity. So common sources would be the EHR, the electronic health record, um imaging archives, EKG archives, ERPs, things like that. And also the internet. You know, there are a lot of clinical guidelines on there. So that's the knowledge work.

That feeds into the reasoning brick, which is where the AI lives. And then AI gets you an answer. Which is great. But if we don't take an automated action on top of that, you just have a dashboard that allows you to admire the problem as opposed to actually [laughter] doing something around it.

And so, the re-output of the reasoning brick gets fitted fed into the action brick where we can notify patients. We have, you know, auto snail-mailed patients. Uh we have auto-texted SMS'd patients. Um we have obviously through the EHR AI voice calling to some more nascent uh level. We can message providers through the EHR through automatic fax.

Got on a CT scan of the chest where the heart was visible, where the arteries around the heart were visible. The patient felt through care gaps, literally needed a heart heart attack before they saw a cardiologist.

And so his proposal to our lab was, could you build an AI model that could comb through every scan that is being done at Stanford? Basically you screen every patient coming to Stanford Hospital and see if they're at high risk of heart disease and if so bring them to the cardiologist. And that problem got super exacerbated by LLMs. Because now many algorithms just became prompts.

Right.

now do with these much easier to program algorithms so they're just prompts, but it also I guess simplifies the deployment to some degree because well, you just need to pick some LLMs and then you can solve a huge number of problems as opposed to before you needed some like, you know, bespoke deep learning pipeline for each different use case and then for each of them figure out how they're going to deploy it through a single infrastructure and they might need very different amounts of compute and whatever. And so, if you look at like the Maslow's hierarchy, there are some problems at the base level that a health system faces at the care delivery system faces. And then there are some like at the very top that are enlightened use cases. And everyone was just going after the enlightened use case because why else where where else would researchers spend time on?

That's right.

But with LLMs, we now actually had a way to offer solutions for every problem, every type of problem within the Maslow's hierarchy, um such that a health system could say, "Hey, we want to invest in this type of platform. Yes, we will solve the hair on fire, the important and urgent problems for today, but it also gives a way for everybody else for their enlightened problems to be solved as well." offering the very bottom of the pyramid here.

Now, if there was no link back to the EHR, whoever is reviewing those results would have to basically play the game of like uh needle in a haystack, and that would have been an incredibly difficult way to see adoption happen. But in this way, because you could just link it back. So, yeah, tool use and just citations back to the EHR or to the system of record.

That example's also cool cuz it also feels like the type of thing that a human may not have caught cuz it's cuz it's like in a niche, you know, weird one that that's a huge advantage of having these systems that are like, you know, they basically are just like, "Yep, sir, I'm just going to go read every single document and just like read everything and give you the full detail."

unreasonable to expect humans to do that. Um and so yeah, you can just have it do more work than what you would expect it to do.

Which actually is a great segue into my second school of thought, which is since we were very academic students of this industry of like, let's see what the value proposition is, how do you validate models, things like that. I have been a strong believer that the performance comparison should not be 100%. It should always be against the standard of care.

Mhm.

Now, what's the difficult is that we for most things we don't even know where the standard of care is.

Right, we don't really measure accuracy or whatever person.

But that has been a big push that we are trying to make, which is always try to evaluate these things in terms of how they compare to the standard of care. So, there are some use cases for which that bar is really low. If you don't have an existing process because human labor is too expensive and too scarce, so you were just not doing something. So, now with the sheer fact of doing something in most cases the bar is so low to clear because the the alternative is doing nothing.

Right.

Obviously, if there is an existing process for something, then you need to show it against the standard of care. So, that's been a huge something that we advocate a lot for, which is let's make it a comparison against the standard of care, which we can measure based on historical. Now, again, you know, we can just do historical analysis very quickly and get you what was happening before and after.

And and how do hospitals think about that as the standard? Like now as you've built these relationships with a number of quite a few hospitals, do they have you adopted that because that's how they see the right way to measure effectiveness, or is that coming from you guys as like you bringing I guess sort of a the appropriate benchmark to set to determine what the right models to use are?

It's more so the latter. I think there's an element of this is what I really love about applied machine learning or applied AI where people from the tech side need to work so closely with the people in the industry to understand what's going on, how to measure things, things like that. And so, this is like naturally, you know, every person we talk to should and does ask like, "Oh, how does this perform?" And we're like, "Well, that's not necessarily like obviously that's a question you need to ask, but the headline question is how does this perform against the standard of care?" And it resonates. Like this is one of those things where once you say it, you never forget about it.

very obvious when you when you say it.

团队与组织：21 人服务比员工数还多的客户

Okay, why don't we change gears slightly and talk a little bit about your team? I'm I'm curious especially in this current technological moment we're in, um founders are really rethinking or in some case have been thinking about how to architect the team that can use all the incredible technology that's being built really effectively. And you already described some of this earlier. You mentioned you have a product team and sort of forward deployed folks who go into the the hospitals. Um how have I guess like changes in technology, especially around coding agents, affected how you think about what your team should look like and how lean it should be and also what everyone's job should even be in like building this product and getting it to customers?

Yeah. It's both team and product. I think we always when we generally think about how AI changes the shape of teams, we think about it in terms of efficiency. But I think there's also something to be said that the first derivative of the product should also look very different as well.

Uh with all of that said, like this comes back to our engineering team only works on the the platform level. No one has ever written I mean nobody writes that much code anyway, but nobody is even at like no engineer is working on a specific use case. We only work at the level of abstraction.

Those primitives.

It's very important, and there should be no compromise on that. And then the forward deploy team is the one where that can serve multiple different health systems over a dozen of use cases per customer, uh and really drive the product's adoption across the health system.

And so for that reason, like our engineering team will remain lean. Um and I I just me I just can't see the 10,000-person team anymore.

Yeah.

It's just like why? And and for what it's worth, I really really love working in a smaller team. Uh like we are 21 people today, and we have more customers than the number [laughter] of people actually at this size.

That's awesome.

Uh which is which is great, but um when you have such a small team, you just have fewer problems. Like none of our engineers need like a product brief of what's going on in the field. Th- we we every engineer is kind of like a product engineer to some extent. Like they understand what's important for the customer, and they kind of work backwards from that.

Um so every engineer is much more like they see what the customer is able to use the product for and see where to go from there. Um even on go-to-market side of things, whether that's marketing, obviously, you know, there's there's the applications of AI are so obvious. But even on more on the fields go-to-market side of things, it's because we can now one person can do the work of so many different people. It's just amazing to see that happen.

Um and so I want to keep our team lean. We're still growing. Like we're going to hire so we're going to go from 20 to a modest 35 by the end of the year.

[laughter]

Nothing like 20 to like 100. Those days I feel they're gone or maybe we're doing something wrong. I don't know.

Probably your token spend will go up faster than your people spend, yeah.

Yes, absolutely. But uh yeah, I we think of teams as we add um it's very systems-oriented systems thinking again. It's like every stakeholder at the hospital, whether that's the decision maker, whether that's the end users, whether that's the IT team, whether it's procurement, they all are mapped to one individual from Bunker Hill.

Oh cool.

Um and similarly again, and no engineer works on anything use case specific only at the platform level. So because of things like that, I feel like we have a very functioning small team. And I want to keep it that way. Uh it sounds very fun. It it's a very fun time to be working on something like that.

It's a It's a really fun team size to be in. That would be very fun.

Yes. Five is when like no, like this is like everything is a P0 suddenly.

Right. [laughter]

Uh 100 is when like I think things start getting unwieldy and 1,000 2,000 like I hate that that seems I I I can't fathom why anymore.

Yeah.

Yeah.

患者视角：一个"火鸡骨"病人的完整旅程

Maybe as a final question, I'm curious, you know, in thinking from the perspective of patient, you know, we've talked so far quite a lot from the perspective of hospitals and um

innovators

uh innovators in hospitals. And now I'm curious from the perspective of patient, what does your company mean for a patient interacting with the health system? Not necessarily just today, but let's say over the next decade. Like how should I expect to experience something different, if anything, from my side as a patient?

Can I walk you through a patient journey?

Yeah, sure.

This is a real story.

This is the a current patient journey.

Yes. Yeah, yeah. We had a patient last Thanksgiving who had a turkey bone stuck their throat. And so, they came to the emergency room of this partner hospital that we work with. Uh obviously for the turkey bone. And everybody at the hospital was treating the patient for that turkey bone, obviously. Um but they get a CT scan. And the Stanford model that I had described earlier, it works on that scan behind the scenes.

Um the patient gets discharged after the turkey bone, but then the patient gets an automated letter from us on behalf of the hospital saying, "Hey, you came in. You got the CT scan. We found this um blockages in your arteries. We recommend that you follow up with the cardiologist, especially because given your records, we haven't seen any indication that you're being treated for it right now."

The patient comes back to the hospital. Now then sees a primary care provider. The primary care provider says you need to see a cardiologist. And the patient gets added to the referral queue. Now this referral queue has thousands of patients. And it's first in, first out in a conventional setting. Can you imagine?

Okay. First in, first out.

Seems suboptimal.

Everybody is stat, right? Like everybody is an urgent referral. So, you just have this ginormous queue. And instead of doing first in, first out, there's an AI agent that is combing through every patient in the referral queue, applying an urgency score, and then changing the prioritization order. Which patients can be treated by virtual care, which patients need to be treated in a brick-and-mortar setting, which physicians should they be matched with because of their specialties.

So, then that patient gets a scheduling done also through an AI agent. And then finally sees a cardiologist much earlier than they would have seen in the first in, first out referral queue. They see the cardiologist. The cardiologist understands the patient's history. And turns out that this turkey bone patient was actually experiencing chest pain, but had dismissed it as heartburn. So, this patient was symptomatic. And very likely that they would have had a heart attack in the next couple of months or years.

And so then the patient undergoes further testing and as the patient is getting tested the health system needs to ask the insurance company if they're going to cover the cost. You have an AI agent helping with that as well. Then the patient finally gets uh the procedure. So then this case they got a triple vessel bypass surgery where it's an open heart surgery and you it's a very cool surgery actually. Uh pretty cookie cutter these days but I still find it like whoever thought of it amazing.

Crazy, yeah.

Uh so the patient gets the surgery then the follow-up also happens automatic. So across the journey, you know, like whether and then also for billing the health system uses an AI agent to then bill the insurance companies.

So throughout the journey whether that's diagnosis patient sort of like through the continuum of care of all the way from diagnosis to referral patterns to actually advising on the treatment to getting in front of the right physicians to um actually then making sure that the hospital gets paid for it so on and so forth. Like the for the patient you can expect a lot more of a connected experience, a lot more like as if there's somebody always looking out for you.

And hopefully as AI continues to deliver there'll be more and more applications of AI that go towards those enlightened use cases of like earlier diagnosis, better treatment selection, enrollment in clinical trials, helping matching patients to the right therapy. And this can all happen through one system of action, I believe. Information is coming in. You're running AI and you're taking automated actions.

That's a pretty inspiring present and future. Yes. Thanks so much for joining us. This is really great. me. Appreciate it. [music] [music]

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、真正的产品不是 AI，而是"让说'试试看'的成本趋近于零"
大多数人以为 Bunker Hill 卖的是医疗 AI，其实他们卖的是**组织决策的边际成本**。传统医院里，一次"要不要用某个 AI 工具"的决策，绑定着两年的采购、IT、合规、变革管理——所以每个"是"都必须是慎重的、经过任务小组反复审议的、押上职业信誉的"是"。这种高成本迫使决策者不得不用"重要性排序"来筛选想法，而重要性排序本身就是创新的杀手，因为**真正颠覆性的想法在早期看起来总是不够重要**。
Bunker Hill 真正做的事情，是把"说 Yes 的成本"从两年降到两周。一旦这个成本足够低，医院的决策模式就会从"审议模式"切换到"实验模式"。这不是一个 AI 故事，而是一个**制度经济学**故事——就像 AWS 之于软件创业，把"启动一个想法"从需要买服务器、租机房，压缩到刷信用卡的十分钟。医疗 AI 的"AWS 时刻"就是他们真正在下的赌注。
## 二、意外的观察：LLM 让"不酷的问题"变成了"酷问题"的通行证
这是整个访谈里最反直觉的一段。研究者过去只做"酷"的临床问题——心脏病预测、癌症

item.title: [Bits + Bips] BTC Back Above $65K: Green Shoots or Bull Trap?
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、标题与内容的惊人错位：这本身就是最大的洞察

这段转录最诡异之处，不在于它说了什么，而在于它"该说却没说"什么。标题承诺的是 BTC 重回 6.5 万美元的市场解读，实际开场却把观众直接抛回了 138 亿年前的宇宙大爆炸。

这不是失误，很可能是节目主持人刻意为之的修辞策略——在讨论"green shoots or bull trap"（真复苏还是假反弹）之前，先用宇宙尺度重置观众的时间感。当你刚刚思考完 138 亿年，再回头看一根 4 小时 K 线，你的心理波动率会被强行压低一个数量级。这是一种认知层面的"降杠杆"。

二、跨领域连接：引力聚合 ≈ 流动性聚合

"From darkness, gravity pulled matter together to create stars and galaxies." — 主持人（或旁白）选择的这句话，几乎是加密市场结构的一比一映射：

  • 引力 → 流动性：市场中的"暗物质"是场外静默资金，它不发光，但它决定星系（价格结构）如何形成。
  • 恒星形成 → 价格发现：物质密度突破临界点才点燃聚变——同理，成交密度突破某个阈值才点燃趋势。
  • 45 亿年前地球形成 → 减半周期后的价格结晶：需要漫长的引力吸积，才能出现一个稳定运行的天体（或牛市结构）。

出人意料的推论是："Bull trap"其实是宇宙学意义上的原恒星失败——质量不够，引力（流动性）撑不住聚变（趋势），最终坍缩为褐矮星，只发出微弱红光后归于沉寂。用这个视角看 6.5 万美元的反弹，你要问的不是"K 线形态"，而是这次聚合的"质量"够不够点燃自持反应。

三、隐藏的元信号：内容缺失也是信息

真正让我警觉的是——转录内容极短，且完全没有涉及 BTC。在系统性分析中，"数据缺席"往往比"数据存在"更有价值。这暗示两种可能：

  1. 信号截断：这可能只是节目冷开场的前 15 秒，主体内容还未展开。若如此，节目组愿意花如此长的"宇宙学铺垫"，说明他们判断当前市场情绪极度浮躁，需要用宏大叙事强行降温——这本身就是一个逆势指标。
  2. 叙事框架：将比特币放入"从大爆炸到今天"的时间轴里，是把 BTC 隐性锚定为一种类地质学资产（scarce, foundational, slow-forming），而不是一种交易标的。这是叙事战场上的一次静默进攻。

啊哈时刻：当一档投资节目开始谈论宇宙起源时，它真正在做的往往不是分析——而是为观众重新校准时间偏好。而时间偏好，才是牛熊的真正分水岭。

📝 转录文本

Opening: A Cosmic Framing

Hey, let's talk about the history of the world.

The Big Bang

Our story starts with the Big Bang, roughly 13.8 billion years ago. From darkness, gravity pulled matter together to create stars and galaxies.

The Formation of Earth

Eventually, about 4.5 billion years ago, Earth formed.

观看原视频 →

这段转录最诡异之处，不在于它说了什么，而在于它"该说却没说"什么。标题承诺的是 BTC 重回 6.5 万美元的市场解读，实际开场却把观众直接抛回了 138 亿年前的宇宙大爆炸。

## 二、跨领域连接：引力聚合 ≈ 流动性聚合
"From darkness, gravity pulled matter together to create stars and galaxies." — 主持人（或旁白）选择的这句话，几乎是加密市场结构的一比一映射：
