But on figuring out what to build, figuring out what are the right strategic directions or experiments you should run to improve outcomes for your business — that's not changing as fast as the humans knowing how to use the tool to get and to plug in all the right data to be able to take the right decisions for taking your product forward and to take your business forward.

结语

主持人：Um, listen, I love the product, but even more than I love the product and you as a founder, I love the outcome. The outcome for business is extraordinary. So, anybody who's listening, Lovable is absolutely worth your time. Don't wait. Just put it on your corporate card and start building. That's my message. Just start building with Lovable. It's an incredible product.

And uh, congratulations on being reborn six times, 'cause every 6 months you add 100 million in revenue, it seems. And then everybody says Lovable's dead because the new foundation model is so good. But you keep studying your customer, and you keep somehow surviving and thriving. So congratulations as an entrepreneur.

Anton：Thank you so much, Jason. I enjoyed that chat. I hope you enjoy the rest of your stay here in Paris.

主持人：It's pretty great. And the Palace of Versailles is so impressive, huh? Uh, someday we'll be building this with Lovable and Optimus robots.

Anton：I'm looking forward to it. I'm going all in. I'm going all in.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 洞察一：Intel 的衰落不是技术问题，而是"董事会人类学"问题
Pat Gelsinger 说 Intel 的问题是"business people 取代了 technical people"，这是表层解读。**真正的深层模式是：一个组织的"招聘 DNA"具有极强的自复制性**——他一句话点破了组织生物学的铁律："business leader 只会晋升 business leader"。这不是管理学，这是**克隆学**。
让我惊讶的观察是：Intel 在 5-6 年里向股东返还了 **1000 亿美元**，而**同期它连一座新工厂都没建**。这不是"错过风口"，这是**一个技术公司主动进行的化学阉割**——用财务工程把自己变成一个不能繁殖新技术的实体。有趣的跨领域连接：这和**华尔街投行的"分红文化"侵蚀制造业**是同一个病，也和**大学被"论文 KPI"侵蚀研究文化**是同构的。当一个组织开始用**下游指标**（股价、EPS、论文数）优化上游行为时，它就已经死了，只是尸体还在走路。
## 洞察二：Steve Jobs 那句"我已经 port 了四个版本"揭示了一个反直

item.title: [GoldRepublic Global] Michael Oliver: Gold and Silver Are About to Reignite
item.dc:creator: GoldRepublic Global
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽略的"张力反转"：黄金上涨的真正燃料不是恐惧，而是无聊

大多数人以为黄金因"战争"、"地缘冲突"上涨——Michael Oliver 却用一个反直觉的历史数据把这套叙事推翻：2022 年俄乌开战后，黄金反而下跌了 20%。真正让金价系统性上升的，不是恐慌新闻，而是一件极度乏味的事：M2 货币供应几乎每十年翻倍的抛物线曲线。

这里藏着一个令人意外的结构性洞察："黄金是新闻的敌人，是曲线的朋友"。新闻属于事件驱动的高频层，而黄金真正对应的是货币系统底层的慢变量——像地壳运动一样几乎看不见，却决定山脉走向。这也是为什么每次黄金"出人意料"地在利好中下跌、在无消息时暴涨——它不是在回应现实，而是在回应现实之下的会计恒等式。

二、跨领域连接：白银的"50 年盒子" ≈ 语言学中的"未被言说词"

Oliver 反复强调白银被压制在 1980–2024 的 5–50 美元区间整整半个世纪。这让我想起语言学里的一个现象——"缺席即信号"：一种语言中如果某个概念长期没有词，通常不是因为不存在，而是权力结构在压抑它的表达。

白银的价格盒子，本质是一种"金融语言学禁语"。它有工业需求缺口（连续 5–6 年供应赤字）、有货币属性（数千年历史）、有 M2 通胀（若匹配货币供应应在 300–500 美元）——三重定价理由同时存在，却在市场"词典"里长期沉默。当一个概念被压抑得越久，一旦破封，其表达强度会是补偿性暴力（overshoot）的——语言学如此，价格也如此。

Oliver 用"10 倍对数区间"计算目标价，其实是在做一件语言学家做的事：测量一个被压抑的词，一旦被允许说出，将激起多大的语言洪流。

三、最出人意料的观察：政府债券危机是"反向核弹"——它不摧毁一切，而是把一切重新定价

大多数分析师把美债危机描绘成"灾难"。但 Oliver 的框架里有一个隐藏的逆转：债券危机不是终结，而是资产阶级的大洗牌。当 60/40 的旧秩序死亡、当日本+美国同时进入债务危机、当 M2 无法停止扩张时——钱必须去某处。这不是毁灭性通缩，而是一场流动性的迁徙。

这里有一个更深的系统性真相：当代金融体系其实运行在"三选一悖论"上——政府不能同时（a）不违约（b）不通胀（c）不让市场崩盘。过去 40 年通过全球化和央行技巧勉强规避了这个三角。但今天，日债已经暴露，美债正在暴露——这个悖论正在结账。

而 Oliver 提到的一个几乎没人注意的细节最耐人寻味：信用卡公司（Visa、Mastercard）自去年 10 月起在标普上涨中悄悄下跌。这是"消费者层债务疲劳"的心电图——底层已经在漏血，但被 AI 和半导体的烟花掩盖。这与顶层的政府债务危机形成共振信号：债务金字塔从顶端和底端同时开裂，中间的股票估值层就成了最后被压碎的夹心层。

"啊哈时刻"：股市不是被通胀杀死的，也不是被利率杀死的——它是被"没有其他可以放钱的地方"这个假设被证伪杀死的。当 60/40 变成 60/20/20（股/债/金），那个额外的 20% 从哪里来？——从股票的估值里来。这才是即将到来的"再定价"的机制本身。

📝 转录文本

开场与核心判断

We think the stock market is topping. Still, the trigger numbers that will tell us that it is top to top are not far below the market. It doesn't take a 20% drop in the market to trigger these numbers. We're talking single-digit percent drop from current levels. And we think it's coming probably this quarter.

I don't mean you're going to crash or anything like that, but you're just going to start down. And I think the monetary metals know this — that there is a government debt crisis at hand. We've never had one. It's bigger. It's a nuclear event. It makes mortgage crisis look like nothing compared to a government bond crisis.

We're at the edge of a massive breakout that when you break out above that, you're going to have the biggest monetary metals panic you've seen ever. A public realization that miners are dirt cheap. Silver would be at $300 to $500 right now just to match the decay in the money supply. So silver is a lag beast. Now, you know, there's arguments why it is. So, I think we're at a cusp where when it happens, a lot of it's going to happen this year.

主持人开场

Welcome to the Microscopic Podcast. My name is Alex Adrenov. I'm your host today and I have the pleasure to welcome Michael Oliver. Welcome back, Michael.

Michael: Good to be back.

Alex: Today is the 14th of July and I'd like to remind my English speaking audience — which is mainly from the US, Canada, England, UK and maybe some Australia — that as you can see I'm wearing my French jersey. It's the 14th of July. This is a national celebration day of the best, and also the semi-finals of the World Cup that happens in North America. So you apologize my French jersey today.

But I would like to ask you first, because we've had an interview about a year ago in August of last year, and you said that the US equity market was hitting the largest bubble in its history. Well then gold and silver and miners were actually beginning a structural rotation. But since then silver has obviously, as we all know, delivered one of the largest moves of the whole group. Commodities overall have broken also higher, but US equities have remained pretty resilient.

Looking back — which part of your framework has been most strongly confirmed in your opinion, and which part has taken longer than you expected?

股市："艰难顶部"的形成

Michael: Well, the stock market is doing what we call a laborious top. It made one in 2000. It took a year and a quarter for it to top out and finally go down. It made a high and then made a new high and it was just struggling around — a lot of sideways to slightly up ink on the charts.

Same thing happened in 2007. There's a whole year of distribution up there. Finally made a new high in October of 2007 with a Fed rate cut, surprise rate cut, and that topped it. That topped the market. And then it wasn't really until you got into the spring/summer of 2008 that it first started to fall apart. So it was laborious — that's the point I'm making.

This one is too. Since January, February of last year, we thought we'd have a drop. We had a serious drop and that was due to Trump's tariffs — another transitory news headline that dominated everybody's mind. It's still there. We still have tariffs, but the market went up anyway. Okay, get the point.

Then we had the war event in March. The S&P had hit a high of 7,000 I think at that point prior to that. And we dropped down and took out a couple months worth of price lows due to another — what we regard as a transitory event — the Iran thing. And we argued to people then, even though we were looking for a major top to be completed, we said: uh-oh, if you sell this war news, they're going to gut you. They're going to hit you real good. Sure enough, shot to a new high. Take the war news and shove it.

16 年的廉价货币与更大的泡沫

Okay, there's bigger issues at hand here. We have a bubble. A bubble that's been in the making for 16 years — 17 now, 2009 low. During that lengthy period of time, the central banks have been in excess to prior norms in terms of printing, printing, printing — artificially pricing money way below reality. So 10 of those 15 years we had short-term rates at like zero. Free money. Oh boy, let's party, you know, let's go buy something. And they did. They bought stocks.

And even when they raise rates a bit — "oh gosh, they raise rates" — it got up to 5% or something. When you look back 75 years on a Fed funds rate chart, the stuff that the Fed controls, short end, even that 5% is silly looking. It's like in the bottom third of the history of the last seven, eight decades of rates. So even when they raise rates, rates were still low. So we've had 16 to 17 years of effectively free money — or cheaper money than we've ever had in history — in the modern history.

So naturally something went up. Why? Because there's a river flow of money and the public put it into stock market, which is a typical place for them to put it, and that helps create bubbles like the dot bubble, the real estate/mortgage bubble. This time we have a bigger bubble than ever, and this is going to affect all markets.

政府债务危机：核级事件

And I think it is already built into the monetary metals — that is the government debt crisis. T-bonds, Japanese government bonds which we know are in crisis. Now our bonds are at the point of even looking at price charts or yield charts causing pain and fear.

We've argued that the T-bonds are headed into a crisis — US bonds — and that's a problem for investors because where are you going to put your money usually when the stock market rolls over? You can put it into bonds, sort of comfortable place to be. But that alternative isn't working anymore. And even the head of trading — CIO of trading for I think it was Morgan Stanley — said some four, five, six months ago: the old 60% / 40% rule (stocks/bonds) in a portfolio is no more. It should now be 60 / 20 / 20, 20 being gold. Which is a hell of an admission on this guy's part. And he's right. Something's changed.

And I think that's something that you got to watch. It's not AI. It's not semiconductors bubble breaking or whatever. It's government bond crisis. We have never had one before. We had the mortgage crisis, private debt, back in 2007–8–9, which sunk some banks, almost sank General Motors and got bailed out. God forbid if it's government bonds. Japanese — okay, let's ignore that one. Uh-oh, now it's US. Uh-oh, we got a real problem. And this is global. It's going to impact the world.

触发点：单位数百分比的下跌就够了

So that's what we're facing right now. We think the stock market is topping. Still, the trigger numbers that will tell us that it is topped are not far below the market. And we cover those in our reports — as you know, you get the reports. It doesn't take a 20% drop in the market to trigger these numbers. We're talking single-digit percent drop from current levels, and you can start triggering our long-term momentum structures like blowing the bridge out from underneath the market.

So it's not going to take much to start that process, and we think it's coming probably this quarter — the initial breakage. I don't mean you're going to crash or anything like that, but you're just going to start down.

And I think the monetary metals know this. I think they know that there is a government debt crisis at hand. We've never had one. It's bigger. It's a nuclear event. It makes mortgage crisis look like nothing compared to a government bond crisis. And that'll have many effects in many places.

Jamie Dimon of JP Morgan some months ago said, "We're facing government debt crisis. We're going to have to deal with it now." He didn't mean 10 years from now. And our technicals argue he was dead on right. It's coming and it's here — we're at the edge. And yet it's not a chatter point on TV, financial TV — still talking about AI or bank earnings or this, that, the other, but not talking about the government debt.

资产类别的迁移：从股票到商品

Alex: How would you quantify exactly then, for those who do not receive your newsletter — which I recommend to everyone — which levels exactly are you looking at? Because for example, now the S&P is at 7500. How exactly would you go about it in terms of numbers?

Michael: Well, it looks like there's an asset class shift underway, and it's subtle and few people are commenting on it. All they're commenting on is oil rose recently.

In fact, last October, we put out a report arguing that the Bloomberg Commodity Index — a broad, fairly well-balanced index of commodity prices; it includes oil, energy, also grains, soft commodities like sugar, cocoa, base metals, and also monetary metals, but there's not an excessively heavy weighting in any of those categories, so it's not biased by energy for example — anyway, in October of last year, the Bloomberg commodity index after floundering sideways had a drop from 140 back in 2022 (high price level for Bloomberg) after having made a low below 60, by the way, in 2020. So in two years, it shot up from under 60 to 140, which is still cheap for the Bloomberg. Its high in 2008 was 237. So even getting to 140 is only halfway back to the high.

Then we dropped back down to 100 and lower on the Bloomberg and labored for a couple years quietly, like 90 to 100. Just blah, blah, blah, nothing. In October last year it was 107 on the monthly close. We said: that's it. Next major up-leg in commodities is commencing. And it surged up past the high of 2022, which is 140 — we got above that slightly, meaning positive to zigzag. And since it pulled back into the 120s.

But oil was not the main factor there. Everybody thinks, "oh, commodities went up because oil went up." No. Oil didn't even turn up by our metrics until January. Price then was $65 West Texas, and it surged on the headline news of "oh, world's gonna end, so we got to buy oil." It was a laggard in the commodity complex. The commodity complex had already been moving up — it already moved up 20, 30% or so from the low, from a breakout level last October. Oil joined in January, some months lagged to the commodity complex. So it wasn't a leader in the commodity rise.

智能资金已经在移仓

So in other words, what I'm arguing is that the asset class shift out of stocks — which I think is already underway by some asset class movement by major portfolio managers — you can tell they're buying something else. They've been buying monetary metals. They've been buying commodities, commodity-related stocks. That is the smart managers, the ones who are ahead of the game. The mass of them have not yet. Wait till that happens.

So I think we've got an asset class shift underway, which is not uncommon. The late 70s, for example — we went into a world recession, they called it stagflation. And while rates went up sharply, Fed raised rates on the short end, long-term bond yields went up, gold exploded. So did commodities. So the popular assumption that "well, if they take rates up that means gold's got to go down" is total baloney. There's plenty of other examples where that's simply not the case.

美联储已开始购债

But anyway, this time around we have a government debt crisis and the Fed knows it. And they even said last November — President Williams of New York Fed said middle of November — the Fed's going to start buying bonds now. And he gave the excuse of "to provide liquidity in the bond market." Oh, that's nice. Not to support the market, but to give it liquidity. Cool.

Anyway, since then, bond prices have gone down, meaning they've not been able to support it. All they've done is slow the decline, slow the rise in yields. And right now we're at levels that even price chart watchers or yield chart watchers realize — uh-oh, we're about to blow out all the lows of the last couple decades in price and push through the yield ceiling that we thought we had.

And the Fed can't let that happen because they can't let the house burn down, especially when it's a government debt crisis. They can't just twiddle their thumbs and say, "well, we're not responsible for that — we deal with inflation and the job market, it's not one of our mandates to protect the bond market." That's not true. They'll print, print till they're blue in the face, just like the Bank of Japan has done. In which case: monetary expansion, inflation, monetary inflation. And that is what the driver is of gold and silver historically — simple monetary inflation.

And this time around, I think it's going to help commodities as an asset category. Meaning: we're facing an asset class breakage and shift out of the stock bubbles and into a cheap asset category called commodities. It's historically cheap to itself. It's historically cheap compared to the stock market. It's just dirt cheap by any measure. And I think that's where an investor should likely be over the next couple years — in commodities and stocks related to that, not to mention the monetary metals.

日本作为全球再定价的导火索

Alex: We'll talk about commodities in more detail a bit later, but I would like to still pick your brain on the potential trigger for the US bond and equity bear market that your thesis is about. It might sit outside of the United States and you've just alluded to it — Japan. Japan spent decades exporting capital because of domestic yields. JGB yields are actually rising and the Bank of Japan is gradually reducing its role in the bond market as well. Couldn't Japan become then the catalyst that forces this global repricing of financial assets that you've also alluded to?

Michael: Yeah, we all know — though it's not talked about much on financial TV — the abandonment of US bonds and also a dumping of the dollar as a reserve asset or reserve money. The dumping of our bonds is incredible. The Japanese, the Chinese, everybody getting out of US bonds. They're smart.

There's a major change underway here. The US dollar is no longer going to be the reserve currency. Why we even need a reserve currency, I don't know. We live in an electronic world where transfers of money from one currency to another can occur like that. It's not like back in a couple hundred years ago when the British pound was the reserve currency.

But I think the dollar is on its way out as a reserve currency, and the US government bond market is one of the triggers that's probably helping spark that — that's one of the main ways of owning our assets, is through our bonds.

T-Bond 图表：三次反弹皆失败

So we're at the point now if you punched up a T-bond futures chart — for example, 30-year bond futures — you'll see that we collapsed from the year 2020 to the year 2022 from 190 on T-bonds to 117 — a 12 — that's a collapse. Yields went up big. That's October of 2022 we made that low.

If you look at that chart, monthly price chart of T-bonds — anybody can punch it up and look at it — you can draw a line sideways for three, four years now where the bonds are unable to get off the floor. They've had three rally efforts by our measurements, momentum measurements, to try to get something going on the upside, meaning get yields down. And each one of them has failed. And that's three waves — is about all you get. And I think that third wave has now ended. And that's when the Fed started buying back in November.

And now we're back on the floor again, to the absolute lower end of the floor. And we've even traded this week down to 110 and a half on T-bonds — 110 and 16/32 — where if you close a week there, you take out all the weekly closes of the last couple years. The point is you're at the point now where even those who just watch price charts, not our momentum work which forewarned of this months ago, will say, "Oh god, what's going on here?" And that's when the Fed will have to do something more aggressive behind the scenes. Not admit it — because that would admit panic — but just like the BOJ, you have to defend those bonds. You have to buy them. You have to print, print to buy them. Otherwise, what are you going to do? Twiddle your thumbs and let it burn? No.

So we know we're at the edge of that. We already know the major government of Japan has a bond crisis. Well, to have some third world country have a bond crisis, that's common. Who cares? Japan — now the US. That's an upending of the financial world. But it's not talked about. We're talking, "oh, the earnings of this company or that, oh, the AI is looking good," and then there's the panic bubble we just had in the semiconductors. It's now breaking, by the way. I think it's a bubble that's burst.

黄金作为货币锚的回归

But the real issue is the debt market, government debt market. And I think gold knows that, because it knows there's no way you can simply shuffle this away and say, "well, it's okay, it just sort of gets better." Uh-uh. No. It's a crisis we're going to have to go through. And gold knows that. And it knows there's only one alternative. They got to print the hell out of the money unit.

And so when we look at the dollar index — which a lot of people think is the value of the dollar, which it's not — the value of the dollar is best viewed by looking at an M2 chart. Upward parabolic curve, increase in the money supply. That's what money is. It's degrading year by year, decade by decade. Like, you know, when your granddad built the house, it was $4,000–$4,500. And your dad built one, it was $45,000. Now the median home price in the US is $450,000. That's because of the degradation in the money unit. But people don't factor that into their thought process.

Anyway, that's the crisis we're facing. And I think the monetary metals know — they're not waiting for the news that ongoing monetary degradation like we've seen over the last 16 years, even while gold's been rising for the last 11 years, since 2015. And if you look at an M2 chart, parabolic. In fact, gold's outpaced the M2. It's been a better place to be than the stock market. If you go back to the dot-com high in 2000, and look at the S&P and see where it is now, percent gain, it's barely matched the increase in the money supply, meaning it's not really gained value in terms of real buying power.

So that's the reality we're facing. And I think it's no longer going to be incremental. I think we'll see later this year — it's not going to be an incremental solution to this problem. It's going to be a chaotic, violent one in markets and in people's lives. Thank you, government. Anyway, that's where I think we stand, and I think there's great opportunities here if you get in the right place at the right time.

黄金重新成为货币锚？中国与金砖国家

Alex: So would you say then it's a legitimate statement to say — and it's been also covered for the last year or two — that gold de facto has replaced the US treasuries as reserve asset, and will actually bring back its role that it used to have as a monetary anchor before Nixon pegged it off, and before that the gold standards that we had in different parts of history? That we are re-anchoring gold now, or gold is being re-anchored globally? And that China — I've talked with Alasdair who's preparing for what he says, and I've spoken to him a few days ago about the fact that China is at the moment preparing for actually positioning themselves for a UN-backed gold system alongside China and other BRICS countries. Do you have anything to add to that? And how realistic?

Michael: So, as the crisis proceeds, especially in the Western world where they're not acknowledging this — they're totally fiat-happy with it. Okay, well, they're going to be unhappy with it, because even academicians who've been supporters of the Fed will begin to say (and already are) that "you're not in charge of jack. Something's wrong here." Especially when we hit the crisis, especially when the stock market breaks — because that's where the average guy feels gut-kicked.

平均家庭仅剩投资组合的微笑

Right now, the average family, the only thing they can smile about is their portfolio, their retirement account has been incrementally rising. They're not exploding, but you know, each year, "oh, another 10, another." So, it's the only thing they got to smile about. It's certainly not their credit card over the limit and maxed out and 90 days past due. It's not due to their mortgage rates. All that stuff's negative.

But when you break that stock market, you'll have wave effects also — like the job market changes with the stock market. It doesn't lead it. You go back and look through history — for example dot-com top, 2007 top. You don't get a real negative shift in the job market — the data the Fed likes to look at, "how good's the economy, the job market" — that data doesn't go south hard until the stock market has already broken pretty substantially. So looking at the job market now and saying like Powell just said today, "the economy is in good shape but the job market looks good" — he doesn't link the data to history. It never shows negative and bad until you're months off the high in the stock market. Then they start having the layoffs — and that's when the average guy, one, his portfolio goes down, that doesn't feel good if he has one; and two, his wife got laid off or he got laid off and they got three kids. That's when the stock market creates emotion, and emotion creates doubt and fear.

And that's when you get the tone of panic. No longer is it incremental. It's now "oh my god" type of action. And it wouldn't just be with the average guy on the street, because the top wealth guys are going to be hurt by this too, because they're no doubt heavily in the stock market, and they're going to get gut-kicked real hard. So it's going to be a widespread panic.

That's when this type of shift that China is doing will become more likely elsewhere. And I suspect the West will be the last to do it. Which seems kind of funny, doesn't it? You have what used to be a communist country — they still use the name Communist Party, but frankly their economic policies don't reflect anything that Mao might have said. They have private roads, etc.

But people will then look — even the West might say — "you know, that is a good idea. Maybe fiats aren't particularly stable. Maybe they create instability. Maybe we need to do something like that." But it's going to take a crisis to provoke that change. And likely that crisis — the onset of it to the panic point — will be very brief. It's not going to take a year to get it into a panic mode. Once it starts to break — stock market especially — that's when you will move into a global economic downturn and emotions associated with it. And that's when people rethink reality, and those in government power rethink reality, or somebody takes their place like in Argentina, you know, Javier Milei kicked out the two prevailing effectively socialist parties that have been there for decades, and he's cut government, cut government, cut government. So, countries like Argentina probably will survive this better than we will for obvious reasons.

Anyway, that's the thing we're facing. And I think gold is sitting on top of this powder keg and knows it, and this recent pullback does not alter our long-term trend assessment of gold and silver at all.

黄金近期回调的技术解释

Alex: So how do you explain the recent then price movement in gold? We topped above 5,000 to 200 approximately ounces — dollar per ounce — a few months ago, and then the crisis hit in Iran and people expected that with geopolitical instability gold would still do well, but actually it's kind of the opposite. While oil was rising there, different factors in play. So how do you explain this best? What impacted gold price?

Michael: This happens in bull trends. I could show you countless charts of markets that move from A to Z — massive price moves, five-fold, ten-fold moves — where somewhere in the midst of that was one hell of a sell-off. And no doubt everybody in that market at that time said, "oh, that's it, it's all over." And then when it wasn't all over and you took out that peak, that prior peak that they thought was the top, you go absolutely parabolic — because there's total uncertainty. You've already washed out all the weak longs, and suddenly everybody's going to grab for it again because they realize the reason that it's going up.

金价上涨的真正驱动：法币退化

Right now, nobody seems to know why gold is going up. Very few, anyway. And we argue that it's always underlying — yes, with up-waves and down-waves — but underlying, it's a degradation in the money units. If we didn't have fiat currencies, gold would have been stable for the last hundred years. The only reason gold's going up is the fiat unit going down in real value. And that's what "gold's up" really means. It's the degradation in the dollar — like, it almost doubles its quantity every decade. Same with the euro, the yen. They're pieces of paper. Like Ludwig von Mises said, only government can take a valuable commodity like paper and make it worthless.

So that's what we're facing. But the real crisis of that — the speedy part of it, the accelerated part — is just around the corner. And I think this stumble that gold just had — which, by the way, again via our long-term annual momentum metrics where we measure not the price action of gold versus some short-term metric, yes intermediate factors broke down, but the long-term annual momentum of gold is still positive. That break didn't break a thing in terms of the long-term momentum trend, and therefore we view it as a temporary and intermediate pullback.

In fact, right now as you and I are speaking, we're trading right below some levels that we think — not that they can see it on the price chart, but by our momentum metrics — say you get much above today's high on a weekly close, and gold and silver and the miners, you've seen your last pullback. And this time the rally won't be like the other rallies we've had in the last five months, the prior five months, that go up and then come back down. This time it'll go. And I think we're on the cusp of that right now, literally as we do this interview. Today we've probed up right near levels that you don't want to close the week out above if you're a bear.

关键价格水平回顾

Alex: So in which exact numbers would that be?

Michael: When you look at gold price and silver price, you see this — first off, the real break was a two-day phenomenon. People think, "oh, been going down for five, six months now." Yeah, but most of the break — about 80% of it — occurred in two days. Last trading day of January, first trading day of February. Most of the price drop occurred then, literally in 48 trading hours. And since then, you've gone up and down, and you've made lows that are lower than that February low, but not excessively lower.

I think gold's low back then was 4,400. Where were we trading today? We traded up to 4,100. Silver's low then I think was $64 in early February. We had a high today up near 61. So really, in terms of time duration and of the decline, ever since that two-day break, all the decline has been marginal versus that low. I think what's wearing people out is that it doesn't seem to go back up again. It goes up, but then comes back down. And so they're worn out emotionally by the time and the frustration.

三次白银买入信号

And the other reason they're worn out is they didn't get in at the right time. We had three major buy signals in the last two years:

  • March 2024 — silver was up to 25, 26. We said buy it. Major breakout. At that point, it blew through its 2020 high, which had been 30 bucks. Boom. Blew through it very quickly. So that was 24, 25 to 26 zone was our buy.
  • June 2025 — silver had been capped for a year with two highs at 35 bucks. A whole year wasted sideways. Everybody thought it was over. In June of 2025, silver came back up through 34, approaching those two highs. It went just above 35. And we said, "This time it's not going to stop. It's going to blow your head off." That was our second major buy — mark that down as $35. We exploded after that, another full leg up.
  • November of last year — six, seven months ago or so, silver was at $56 on the close that month. And it wasn't the momentum of silver that we put out the buy signal based on. It was the fact that silver then broke out versus gold. I mean, if you looked at the past 10 years of where was silver versus gold and plotted it as a chart, you blew through a ceiling. It was like 10 years wide. And therefore the spread said, "okay, we're gonna have another upward jolt in this ongoing process." And so we went from 56 bucks to 120 in the next two months.

Now, that 120 was phantom. I mean, you didn't spend a couple days up there. Really, your high is more like 100. I think your high weekly close, in fact, was 100, something like that. But since then, we've had this boring range.

So if you got long — not at our buy levels — if you averaged those three, the average price was $38, those three buy levels. If you got long at 100 or 110, you were a late-comer and you got punished, and I guess you should have been. It wasn't the place to be buying.

下一次爆发的信号

Now I think we're entering a point right now, just above where we are right now, that if we engage through those numbers that we've got (and they adjust weekly — we put out and update them every week), you're going to start a rally this time that won't pull back. But people won't know that at the start. They'll just think, "oh, it's just another dud rally." This time it's real.

And so where's the public going to wake up? It'll probably start to wake up on silver. If you look at a silver chart after that spike at 120, all the other rally highs since then have gone three times: you've gone up to 90 and failed. 90 area failed. 90 area failed. So 90 is a level that most price guys would look at and say, "well, if you get above that, maybe we're going up again." Okay? We're telling you: somewhere in the 60s, you better get on board again if you want to get on board, because the intermediate stuff is shifting back in line with the ongoing positive technicals.

But anyway, so it's going to be an interesting situation. But if we're correct on the upturn right now — that if it engages and triggers the intermediate factors back to positive after 5 and 1/2 months of negative — what's on the other side of that will be very dramatic, and likely it'll be not just price verticality of silver and gold but the miners especially. But it will be associated with headlines that turn people's heads, like financial-related. "Oops, I didn't think about that. What do you mean this bank has a problem? What do you mean this credit card company's got a problem?"

金融板块的隐秘信号

And these things are ongoing, but nobody's looking at them. For instance, last October we put out — we started focusing intently on the financial sector, which is very weak compared to the S&P. In fact, it's at multi-decade lows of value to the S&P. But we looked at the credit card companies and such as Visa and Mastercard — and they haven't collapsed since October, but they've steadily gone down while the S&P has gone up. Something's going on there. We know we have private credit problem, and they're telling you, and yet nobody's talking about them. So it's these kind of things that will come to the front, and I think when the next move up in the monetary metals occurs, you'll start to hear more of this stuff that nobody's paying attention to. That's where I think we are right now. We're on the edge of the next big boom.

白银的 300–500 美元目标

Alex: So I would like to then address your target as well, because you've mentioned a $500 an ounce objective — 300 to 500. It's the upper side of your range. And that comes partly from — if I understood you correctly — projecting the silver roughly ten-fold 50-year price range above the old $50 ceiling, the logarithmic charts. Now my question is: why should the height of the old range determine the scale of the new one?

Michael: Well, in our weekend report you saw we went back and showed, for example, base metals and gold going back to like 1980 — that's when silver was 50 bucks. Okay, silver went to 50 again in 2011, still confined in the box for some reason. But you look at like nickel, steel, aluminum, copper, lead, gold — you plot all these charts and there's this massive parabolic move where we're trading three to 10 times higher than we were in 1980.

How come silver — the baby monetary metal, but still thousands of years has been monetary metal (I think the Indians are shifting toward it now) — how come it's only five bucks above this 1980 high? And we put an arrow on these charts of all these metals and showed where they would be if they were matching silver right now. They would be all the way down to the bottom of their page practically. In other words, why is silver not done what all these metals have done since 1980 — trade at many multiples higher than that? Yes, in zigzags and so forth. But I mean, gold in 1980 was 800 bucks, 850. It was a peak of that bull market. Silver peaked at 50 then. Where's gold now? It's been trading 4 to 5,000 versus an old high at 850. Gee, if silver were doing that, where would it be? You do the math on it. You get the point.

与货币供应的对齐

The other thing is: if you look at an M2 money supply chart and look at its increase — where was money supply 1980, where's M2 now, what's the percent gain in the money supply, degradation in the money unit — silver would be $300 to $500 right now just to match the decay in the money supply. So silver is a lagged beast. Now, there's arguments why it is. It's not fundamental. It's manipulative. But obviously those who've been trying to restrain silver haven't succeeded, because they couldn't keep it in the box. Late last year we broke through the 50. And we think it's only begun.

After all, you broke through a half-century range, you surge for a couple months, that's the end of it? Think about it. Half a century range. You break out above it and — you know, when you look at the logarithmic ratio scale of that range, it's a 10-fold range from bottom to top. Five bucks to 50, five bucks to 50 for 50 years. When you go back and look at other metals using a ratio scale chart and then look where they are now in that, you've easily doubled the range. If silver doubled its range on a ratio scale, it'd be $500. Why? Ten-fold range above 50. 50 times 10 is 500.

So again, there's so many numbers that come up — whether it's against the money supply, matching other metals, the ratio range of silver's range prior and the breakout, if you simply double the range on a ratio scale — all keep coming up with numbers 3 to 500. Now, it's not a precise technical level, but it's the kind of thing you should expect. And quite often when these tantrums occur, they even overshoot reality, which — when you've had an error for 50 years in pricing that no other metals had, and when you want to make up for it — quite often you'll even over-make up for it. So don't be shocked by what you see on the other side of this.

That's our assessment. The key now is: do we come out of this intermediate process of decline with an upside signal? And I'm telling you right now that not far above today's highs on a weekly close, we'll start to engage those factors to the upside.

时间表：突破后的速度

Alex: And so what will be the timeline then of this upward move?

Michael: When you go back and look at some of those metals I mentioned, and look at where they had a range for decades as well like silver but they broke out of it — like at 2005 for copper, 2007 for lead — and gold has continued to make new highs on each bull market way above the 1980 high. It's after you've had that sharp break that everybody thinks is the end. When you take that out, what's on the other side is usually extremely violent, extremely vertical, and very fast.

Meaning that the process of the decline shook out so many people that you in effect puked out the weak holders of the market, and you've created massive doubt and non-participation. So only the strong and the appropriate entry-level longs are still with the market, realizing what's going on, the bigger picture. When you get through that and start to go up the next time — especially when it's associated with certain other asset categories perhaps doing the opposite, meaning flushing money out of the stock market, where does it go? Bonds? Monetary metals? Especially if monetary metals start to advance again, which I think they will — then the money flow becomes panic-like, bubble-like, where you literally go vertical very rapidly. You do something that people think is irrational.

Well, look at the semiconductors, what they just did over the last 6 months. Things like that. And I think you could see that, and I think you will see that. But it'll likely be associated not with war news — which, by the way, gold goes down on war news. Ukraine-Russia war March 2022 — okay, end of February is when it began. Silver, gold peaked in March, went down big. Gold dropped 20% during that war. And so people who say gold goes up because of global uncertainty — that's total baloney. It goes up because of monetary degradation and central banks pushing the pedal to the floor, and gold knows what's about to happen, and it doesn't wait for the headline.

Now, on the other side of this congestion zone, when silver and gold re-engage, you're going to start to get headlines that might be associated with reality — monetary related, financial crisis related. And that's when people will say, "oh, that's the reason." And I think it'll be an orgy to buy those markets, and therefore verticality in price.

对自身论点的反证？

Alex: And what would be, let's say, the current argument to your own thesis that would prove you wrong about the range or the timeline in that sense?

Michael: There's not much that would be contrary that I could think of that would counteract that. I don't have any negative levels below or anything like that. So I think we're simply sitting on a time bomb right now with the intermediate factors, and the factors that will come into play that the public is aware of won't be the things we're talking about now. I mean, not that we're talking about, but they're talking about. So that's where I think we are.

白银的工业需求与操纵

Alex: Would that be because silver is a much [industrial] metal? It's industrial metal. If it's a pie chart, you would give a certain percentage of what drives the demand of silver. You've also talked about market manipulation. How would you summarize that?

Michael: The silver thing is bizarre. I mean, it almost doesn't make sense that you'd have five to six years now of deficit of production to demand — industrial demand of silver. They can't mine enough to get it out to match the industrial demand. Five, six years. So the timing is a key issue. It's a technical issue, because those fundamentals have been there and yet silver sat on its butt for 50 years — the last five especially. It was only late last year that silver finally said, "oh, I'm going to get out of here." So that's kind of weird. So it's really a technical issue there.

But it is monetary metal. And when you compare silver — you want to say, "I want to buy cheap, not expensive." Well, when you measure silver versus gold, right now it's about one and a half. We divide silver price into gold the way we do — we don't do the ratio scale, but if you divide an ounce of silver into gold, it's like 1.5% of the price of gold. If you go back to the 1980 peak, silver was 6.5% of the price of gold — its relative value. In the 2011 peak, when silver also hit 50 bucks, it was 3.1% of the price of gold. And right now, it's 1.5%.

So, forget the net price. But when that spread breaks out, as it did last November — and the pullback has not negated that recently, by the way — if you take that spread up just to the 3.1%, let's get it back to the last 50 years of highs, which is 6.5% and 3.1%. If it even just went to 3.1%, you double the value of silver versus gold where it is now. So as gold goes up, silver's going to go up double as much just to get back to where it was in 2011. If it ever went back to the high of the last 50 years — which price has already taken out, but the spread would be at 6.5%, 12% — it's like a quadrupling of the price of silver versus where it is now to gold.

So our argument is — and by the way, when that spread advances favoring silver, it's always associated with an uptrend in both metals. Silver doesn't advance versus gold in a downtrending metal market. That spread, if it goes up favoring silver, means the net price of both will go up.

金银矿业股：最被低估的一环

The same goes with the miners, by the way, and for that same reason we think the miners are the better place to be in the complex now. I know people don't think that. They think miners are dogs, they're terrible. Actually, they've been beating gold for the last three years handily. If you do the actual measurements of where they were a couple years ago versus gold, they've gone up more.

But they're so low in price value to gold per ounce of gold. Divide GDX into gold, for example, and plot the chart — or XAU index to gold — that they could double or triple in value just to get up to what had been old low levels of value that prevailed for many decades: 1980s, 1990s, 2000, 2008.

Where, for instance, XAU index right now is just below 8% of the price of gold. Back then, it — average price of XAU was 25% of the price of gold. Meaning: the miners, despite the fact that they're killing it in terms of making money — they're making so much money per ounce right now versus their cost of production — that if price of miners went back to even a low-end level of where they'd been in the prior decades, the low end of that range had been 17.5%. By the way, we would more than double the miners just to go to the low end of their valuation to gold that prevailed for decades.

And we see the technicals for that engaging. And if that engages, that will be our last major buy signal — because when that spread moves favoring the miners, it always translates into gold and the miners advancing both net price, with miners gaining more.

We're at the edge of a massive breakout. We've shown, and you've seen it in the weekly reports, that when you break out above that, you're going to have the biggest monetary metals panic you've seen ever — as expressed by the miners. Suddenly a public realization that miners are dirt cheap per ounce to gold. And I think you'll have an orgy of buying.

Well, what does that mean? It means investors will be shifting from one sector of the stock market to this little tiny sector called gold and silver miners, and it will be like a wet-bar-of-soap phenomenon. That's what we think — technically, we're on the cusp of it.

矿业股的风险与轮动

Alex: But — and you maybe address this a bit — what about, let's say, the more risky side of just investing in silver miners for example? The fact that the sector still faces some cost inflation, political risk, operational failure and these kind of factors? When would you also say — I mean, this is one side, and then the other question is: when would you rotate back into then the physical silver?

Michael: Well, I think after you've had the orgy move upside — once you've had this volcanic move, which we think is coming both in relative value of miners to gold, meaning up in both, but miners rampaging more; same with silver versus gold, regaining old values (even don't even have to take out the 1980 high, 6.5%, but just getting halfway back there would be major for silver in terms of its price versus gold) — once you've had that volcanic surge, I don't think we're going to top and go back down into a bear trend.

You may top and go into a new reality is more likely what's going to happen. Because I think gold is going to — like you mentioned with China — I think gold is not going to be just a trading metal anymore. It's going to become monetary metal here, "oh, monetary metal there, oh, good idea, monetary," etc. And the crisis will help create that, the fundamental crisis will help create that institutionalization of gold.

But also, silver has always been money as well. People like to call it just an industrial metal. It is not. Yes, it does have those attributes, but it is a monetary metal, and it is vastly underpriced to gold.

So once we have that surge, my own personal investment strategy is this: I'm going to take some profits out of certain markets like miners — not now, but once you've had the surge — and silver, and put them into cash. The issue is: what's cash? You know what my answer is going to be? Gold. So I'm going to move my profits into cash, gold, bullion, or equivalent thereof, and relax — because I think a new reality is coming. This is not a normal gold bull market. This is something that will change things that have been institutionalized for the last hundred years. Fiat currencies, central banks will become laughing stock in terms of what they do and what they've done. So I think a lot of things will change, and I think gold knows that.

商品市场：宽基础配置

Alex: To conclude, Michael, my last question — and this is about also, you've just mentioned also at the beginning of this interview about the commodity Bloomberg index and commodities as a whole. So there's not just the monetary metals, but there's also all the other commodities. Which one has a special place in your portfolio heart at the moment?

Michael: I don't have one, frankly. In fact, I'm working right now on our monthly commodity report where we examine all the commodities, all the basic commodities within the Bloomberg — oil, natural gas, uranium (even that's not in — I don't think that's in Bloomberg) — base metals, grains, cocoa, coffee, sugar, stuff like that. And frankly, it's a pretty unified chorus, which I can't — I don't want to throw a dart and say "this is the one that's going to beat them all." Yeah, there will be — no doubt there will be some sub-category within Bloomberg that beats them all in terms of percent gain. But it looks like a wide chorus to me.

So maybe the best strategy — if you want to invest in commodities and I know a lot of people don't want to buy commodity ETFs where it's literally the raw commodity that constitutes the ETF, but they want to buy stocks related to the production of grains like fertilizer companies, related companies, base metal miners, various types of energy companies, etc. — maybe the thing to do is to do a widespread positioning.

石油：不追战争新闻

The oil was a problem for us because we said "buy it at 65 bucks" in January. And when they exploded on the war news, we said "uh-uh, this is no good. Don't buy this. These guys are going to get killed. Anybody who bought war news at 100 bucks is going to get killed." And sure enough, they got killed. They should have. You don't chase war news. It's always transient. There's reasons for oil to go up that are nothing to do with war. They have to do with its general price level, which is dirt cheap compared to its own history. It's dirt cheap compared to stock market valuations. It's dirt cheap to anything you want to measure it against.

So maybe commodities are going up simply because they're an undervalued asset category — a real-world asset category that has been neglected, underpriced to other competitive asset categories — is now going to wake up and go up. Because when that monetary excess occurs, when the Fed prints, prints, prints, that goes somewhere. And anytime the stock market is topped and headed down, when they print money, it doesn't help the stock market anymore. But quite often it always helps gold, by the way, historically. But this time it could be like the late 70s where you went into a global recession — stocks were a wasteland for like a decade, but commodities exploded.

And I think in general you can sort of blanket the commodity complex and say — now this is not going to be like gold and silver where there's verticality. I don't think it — it could be strong, but I don't think it's going to be like the monetary metals. But I think owning unlevered positions in stocks related to commodities, a broad basket, is going to be a great place to be over the next two to three years. And I think we're at a cheap enough level where you could call it — you're buying low, you're not buying high.

结语：唯一真实的货币

Alex: Michael, thanks a lot for your time, and — just like gold and silver — precious knowledge. For all the viewers and listeners, your newsletter is available on your website. You also share regularly tweets and insights on X. Is there anything else you would like to add or share to our viewers and listeners, Michael?

Michael: No. I just think — your program in particular will deal with this issue. But I think the issue that's about to hit the world is what we've been talking about, and it's going to change people's real lives, not just their portfolio positions. It's going to affect real people in real lives that aren't even involved in the market. It's going to also hurt the super wealthy, because I don't think they're aware of what they're sitting on either. Some of them are, but most of them aren't. So I think we're at a cusp where when it happens, a lot of it's going to happen this year, and it will be unpleasant for those who aren't ready for it. So the answer is: stack gold and silver. The only real money out there.

Alex: Yep. Yep. On this note, Michael, as I said earlier, thanks a lot. I'm very grateful for your time and knowledge.

Michael: Thanks, Alexi. Hey.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、被忽略的"张力反转"：黄金上涨的真正燃料不是恐惧，而是无聊
大多数人以为黄金因"战争"、"地缘冲突"上涨——Michael Oliver 却用一个反直觉的历史数据把这套叙事推翻：2022 年俄乌开战后，**黄金反而下跌了 20%**。真正让金价系统性上升的，不是恐慌新闻，而是一件**极度乏味**的事：M2 货币供应几乎每十年翻倍的抛物线曲线。
这里藏着一个令人意外的结构性洞察：**"黄金是新闻的敌人，是曲线的朋友"**。新闻属于事件驱动的高频层，而黄金真正对应的是货币系统底层的慢变量——像地壳运动一样几乎看不见，却决定山脉走向。这也是为什么每次黄金"出人意料"地在利好中下跌、在无消息时暴涨——它不是在回应现实，而是在回应**现实之下的会计恒等式**。
## 二、跨领域连接：白银的"50 年盒子" ≈ 语言学中的"未被言说词"
Oliver 反复强调白银被压制在 **1980–2024 的 5–50 美元区间**整整半个世纪。这让我想起语言学里的一个现象——**"缺席即信号"**：一种语言中如果某个概念长期没有词，通常不是因为不存在，而是**权力结构在压抑它的表达**。

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] Who Owns the Sea? – Sarah Paine
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、语言即地缘：藏在词根里的文明宿命

最令人意外的观察是——一个文明的海洋战略，早在它给自己起名字的那一刻就已经被"预装"了。

Paine 点出的对照极其锋利： - Mediterranean = Medi + terranean，"陆地之间的海"——海居于中心，陆地是海的环绕。 - 中国 = 中 + 国，"诸国之中的国"——陆居于中心，其他政治体是"我"的边缘。

这不只是词源趣味。这是一种认知拓扑学：地中海文明把世界想象成一个"以水为中枢的网络图"，而中华文明把世界想象成一个"以土为原点的同心圆"。前者天然会孕育出格劳秀斯的"海洋公有"，后者天然会主张 200 海里"近海即领土"。所以 UNCLOS 上的中美之争，本质上不是 1996 年才开始的——它在两千年前就写进了各自的地名里。

二、被忽视的隐藏主角：那个"西向收费站"

大多数人读大航海史，看到的是"欧洲人爱冒险""科技进步""香料诱惑"。但 Paine 把整个千年史压缩成一句冷酷的商业地理学：

谁控制了东西方贸易的西端"收费站"，谁就统治世界。

罗马、拜占庭、阿拉伯征服、十字军、奥斯曼——这些看似宗教、文明、政治动机各异的宏大事件，被她还原为一场绵延一千五百年的收费站抢夺战。而哥伦布的意义也随之被重构：他不是"发现新大陆的英雄"，而是一个绕过收费站的失败者——本来只想避开奥斯曼的过路费，结果撞见了金银，于是"欧洲人放弃了香料丝绸的高端餐具，改做重金属生意"。

跨领域连接：这个"绕过收费站"的动力学，和今天硅谷的 disruption 逻辑惊人一致——Uber 绕过出租车牌照，比特币绕过 SWIFT，Starlink 绕过海底光缆的地缘瓶颈。每一次技术革命的深层驱动，都是在绕过某个"收费站"。大航海时代的葡萄牙人和今天的马斯克，做的是同一件事。

三、脆弱者立法：为什么荷兰人发明了国际法？

Paine 抛出一个反直觉的因果链：格劳秀斯不是因为荷兰强大才写下《海洋自由论》，恰恰是因为荷兰脆弱。

"他们在欧洲的位置是脆弱的（vulnerable）。"

这里藏着一个普适规律——规则的制定者，往往不是最强者，而是最需要规则的中等强者。绝对强者用武力，绝对弱者靠依附，只有"贸易依赖度极高但军事纵深不够"的中间态，会疯狂地把自己的利益普适化、伦理化、法律化。

跨领域连接：这解释了为什么： - 瑞士（小国）发明红十字会； - 新加坡（小国）成为国际仲裁中心； - 开源社区（个体弱势）催生 GPL/MIT 许可证； - 甚至学术界的同行评审制度——都是"脆弱的中间者"把自己的生存需要，包装成了普世规则。

于是我们得到一个令人不安的推论：当今天美国从"海洋公有"的最大受益者滑向"科技脱钩"的实施者时，它正在从格劳秀斯变成 15 世纪的葡萄牙——不再是规则制定者，而是收费站的重建者。而中国对 12 vs 200 海里的坚持，某种意义上是在扮演一个"陆地版格劳秀斯"，试图把自己脆弱一面（近海防御）普适化为新规则。

海权的真正战场从来不在海面上，而在词典里、法条里、和人们对"什么是自然而然"的默认想象里。

📝 转录文本

引子：海洋作为最初的"网络"

In our own highly networked age, think about it, the seas of the original net network that potentially connected everyone to everything.

海洋帝国的起源：从雅典到罗马

All right, the genesis of this maritime world—I, uh, maybe it goes back further, but as far back as I go, is the Athenian empire: the seas hugging the shores of the Aegean, Ionian seas, and it's all about the trade that's accruing there that's paying for their empire.

The Romans likewise—a very, another rimland empire, very different from the consolidated empires like Russia and China.

词源里的世界观：Mediterranean vs. 中国

And think about the terminology: Mediterranean—meta, middle, terranean, land—so it's the sea in the middle of the lands. Whereas the terminology for China, Zhongguo, Zhong's uh, central kingdom. So, it's the kingdom among the kingdoms.

One term emphasizes the centrality of the sea, the other the centrality of the land. Different way of looking at things.

拜占庭与"收费站"之争

And here's the Byzantine empire, another rimland empire. And then for a while no one controls the rimlands anymore.

And in part what's going on is: there's always been a lot of money to be made on East-West trade. It used to go by camels, among other things, on the Silk Road. But whoever controlled the westward toll booth on that thing made loads of money—because that's where it splits, right? It's going to go on the southern or northern shores of the Mediterranean. A lot of fighting over that piece of territory.

For a while the Muslim conquests settled it. They controlled it.

欧洲的大陆整合与十字军

And then while those things are going on, there is continental consolidation going on in Europe, and both modern Germany and modern France trace their origins to the empire created by Charlemagne at the turn of the 8th and 9th centuries.

And then if you go to the end of the first millennium, you look at Europe: no one controls the rimlands, but you've got these large states that things have coalesced into. And then the crusades, in part, are a fight of these larger powers in Europe to try to come back and get that toll booth back again—which they fail. Instead, the Ottomans have it for a long time.

大航海：绕过收费站的意外发现

And this is when the Europeans have to get clever, 'cuz they want to trade with Asia. So, they—"how we're going to go by ship and we'll go the long way around"—and it's the Spanish and the Portuguese who try this, except they bump into the new world. New to them, not so much to other people.

And they also got, "Ooh, lot of gold and silver here." And so, they forget about the spices, silk, on the high-end dinnerware, and they're going to do heavy metal instead.

荷兰人与国际法的诞生

However, it's the Dutch who are the real followers of maritime empire, which is very much defined by their trading bases. Their problem is their location in Europe is vulnerable.

And it's not surprising that it is a member of the Dutch Republic, Hugo Grotius, who's the founding father of international law. Why? Because maritime empires want to be able to use the seas as commons, don't want people pirating their trade, and would like everyone to have rules so that everybody can trade in safety.

格劳秀斯的三段引文

So, here's Hugo Grotius in his Mare Liberum, Freedom of the Seas, writing that:

"Every nation is free to travel to every other nation and trade with it."

And then in his Law of War and Peace, he cites a Roman jurist—this is going way back:

"To all men belong the use of the sea."

And then he quotes a Byzantine compilation of Roman law, i.e. going way back in Western thinking:

"By natural law the following are common to everyone: the air, flowing water, the sea, and in consequence the seashore."

并非普世：中国与 UNCLOS 之争

Well, this is very much a product of Western civilization, but not necessarily shared by others.

For instance, in 1996, China ratified the UN Convention on the Law of the Seas (UNCLOS). And except—China says that freedom of navigation does not extend within 200 nautical miles of their coastline, except under UNCLOS the number is 12 nautical miles. And in the East and South China Seas, these numbers make a difference.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [Bits + Bips] Is China Secretly Funding the War on US AI Data Centers?
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个隐藏的悖论：最大受益者的CEO却是最强反对者

这段转录最耐人寻味的不是关于稳定币的技术分析，而是最后那句刺骨的观察——摩根大通本应是美元稳定币最大的天然拥趸，但坐在金字塔顶端的杰米·戴蒙却是华尔街最激烈的反对者之一。这不是简单的"老人跟不上时代"，而是一个组织病理学的经典标本：当CEO对某个前沿议题公开反对时，往下三层的员工就不敢把真实的战略机会送到他面前。这形成了一个自我强化的信息盲区——越是重要的颠覆性机会，越无法穿透高层的认知过滤网。

跨领域的意外连接：柯达时刻正在银行业重演

有趣的是，这个模式并不新鲜。1975年柯达工程师Steven Sasson发明了数字相机，但公司高层因为害怕蚕食胶片业务而将其雪藏；施乐PARC发明了GUI却让苹果和微软收割；诺基亚早在iPhone之前就有触屏原型机。戴蒙对稳定币的抵触，本质上是同一种"创新者困境"的金融版本——但更微妙：他不是害怕稳定币蚕食摩根大通，而是错误地把它归类为"加密投机"，而没看到它其实是欧洲美元体系的数字化重生。这里的跨领域启示是：颠覆性技术往往被顶层错误分类，因为它披着一件让人反感的外衣。

真正被忽视的系统性真相：结算层的暗战

大多数人讨论稳定币时聚焦于支付和跨境汇款，但转录中埋了一个几乎没人注意的炸弹——"从Citadel手中夺回股票市场份额，因为你改变了市场结构并能与他们结算"。这暗示了一场正在酝酿的、比支付革命更深远的战争：稳定币可能重新定义T+0结算，让银行绕过Citadel等做市商垄断的现有清算通道。换句话说，稳定币的终局不是取代Venmo或Visa，而是从Citadel这类高频交易巨头手中夺回过去二十年被剥离的市场结构主导权。这也解释了为什么Citadel的Ken Griffin对加密货币的态度长期暧昧——他嗅到的不是投机泡沫，而是自家护城河的地基在松动。

一个反直觉的推论

如果这个分析成立，那么美元稳定币的真正推手不会是硅谷，而是华尔街的"次级权贵"——那些想要绕过Citadel、绕过SWIFT、绕过现有清算所垄断的银行和交易所。这也解释了为什么摩根大通内部其实早就在做JPM Coin，只是被戴蒙的公开言论所遮蔽。顶层的反对，往往是底层革命最好的掩护。

📝 转录文本

JPMorgan: The Unlikeliest Champion of Stablecoins

There is no greater beneficiary in the entire financial system from U.S. dollar stablecoins than JPMorgan Chase. They are the biggest winner.

Why JPMorgan Wins on Every Dimension

Stablecoins are going to bring eurodollar deposits back. That is going to massively increase trading in Treasuries — in which JPM is a dominant participant.

It is going to massively increase usage and funding advantages in the repo market for U.S. banks, of which JP Morgan is both the largest trading party and largest participant.

And it's going to give the ability of U.S. banks to reach globally without themselves having to have a branch in every single location. And what is the largest U.S. bank without an extensive international branch network? JP Morgan.

The Natural Alignment

Right. And so every sign points to JP Morgan being naturally the biggest supporter of U.S. dollar stablecoins globally — just from those perspectives, before we even get into all the market implications of doing things like seizing equities market share back from Citadel because you've changed the market structure and can settle with them.

The Telling Contradiction

So the fact that the guy on top is opposing them tells me good information is not getting up there on frontier topics.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [Business of Sport] From Bankruptcy to Champions League in 9 Years | The Como Story
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

反常识观察一：他们把足球"降格"，反而让足球变得更好

Merwan最颠覆的举动，不是他做了多少商业化，而是他主动让足球在Como的商业版图中变小。他公开说："也许我们不该是一家足球俱乐部，而是一个体育旅游目的地。"这句话在意甲圈子里几乎是异端——但它是所有杠杆的原点。

一旦足球不再是唯一的收入引擎，它就获得了一种奢侈品：免于生存焦虑的决策自由。你可以选择攻势足球而不是Catenaccio，不是因为必须赢，而是因为要让来Como的英国游客愿意花250欧元买张票、并在离开时买一件跑步俱乐部T恤。Nottingham Forest去年跳进了反向的陷阱——他们把欧战当作救命稻草，然后被救命稻草反噬。Como从一开始就拒绝让任何一场比赛承担救命责任。

跨领域看，这个模式其实有名字：Amazon的AWS-Retail结构、Costco的会员费模型、研究型大学用本科生学费养博士。核心业务(足球/零售/教研)本身不需要赚钱，只要够好；周边现金流(旅游/云/会费)负责喂养野心。Como无意间加入了这个反直觉俱乐部——他们的对手在拼命让足球赚更多钱，Como让别的东西赚更多钱，然后回过头补贴足球。

反常识观察二：38%的秘密——他们卖的不是足球，是"身份居住权"

如果你打开 shop.comofootball.com，最惊人的数据不是1400万欧元营收，而是其中62%的商品跟球衣无关——是徒步系列、帆船系列、跑步俱乐部、赛车队。Como根本不做这些运动。

这不是merchandising，这是身份贩售。他们在做的事情跟Supreme、跟Brunello Cucinelli、跟Aspen Institute是同一件事：把一个地方/一个标志变成一种归属感的凭证。你不是Como球迷——你是"来过Como、爱Como、精神上住在Como湖边"的那种人。球队只是这个身份最便宜的入场券。

同一逻辑解释了他们故意把1927年老球场做旧、去跳蚤市场淘1700年代家具、拒绝装成SoFi Stadium廉价仿品的选择。当所有意甲球队都在追求"现代化"，Como选择了逆信号(counter-signaling)——patina即奢华。这是老钱区别新贵的手法，是爱马仕做旧包的语法，被平移进了体育场。跨领域连接：这也是Ryman-era 剑桥学院和京都老铺共用的护城河——旧不是缺陷，旧是无法被复制的资产。

反常识观察三：他把对手球队总裁的电话，变成了隐性卡特尔

当媒体报道Como在争抢一位英国球员时，Merwan做了一件在意大利足球圈几乎没人做的事：他打电话给对方主席，说"你要就拿去，我退出——但下次给我一个你的名单"。

表面看是绅士风度。深层看这是博弈论意义上的隐性卡特尔。足球文化把俱乐部看成零和竞争者，但从商业结构上，它们其实是互补的show partners——没有对手就没有联赛，没有联赛就没有转播收入。这跟Liberty Media接手F1后重新定义车队关系是同构的：一旦大家意识到自己在"共同创造一场秀"，博弈就从零和转向正和。

支撑这种反常操作的是他真正的组织创新——Senate治理模型。数据 → 行为分析 → 财务 → 战术契合 → 教练组 → Fabregas。这个流程不是足球传统的"总监凭感觉签人"，而是Andreessen Horowitz的投资委员会流程。他们用VC评估初创公司的方式评估球员，用初创公司的语言(seed vs growth)去跟UEFA谈判财政公平。当UEFA用过去三年财报衡量他们，Merwan回一句：你不能用growth stage的标准评估一家seed stage的公司。这是足球史上第一次有人把YC pitch deck搬进UEFA会议室。

隐藏的系统性真相：Como正在把俱乐部改造成"平台"

如果把上面三点连起来，会看到一个更大的模式：Como正在做的，本质上是把足球俱乐部从"资产"重新定义为"平台"。

  • 传统足球俱乐部：拥有资产(球员、球场、品牌)，试图让资产升值。
  • Como模型：运营一个平台，服务多个俱乐部(multi-club servicing 而非 multi-club ownership)。区块链票务平台卖给30家意甲、Root时尚协作打包给14家俱乐部、生产链租给"妈妈爸爸小店"、把媒体+旅行+电商打包卖给其他"tourism destination城市"如维罗纳……

这跟Shopify对电商的重构、Stripe对支付的重构、AWS对IT的重构是同一件事：当你不再拥有稀缺资源，而是拥有让别人使用稀缺资源的能力，估值逻辑就从"3-6倍营收"跳到"平台乘数"。这解释了为什么Merwan敢说他们值10亿——因为他不是拿Como跟其他意乙升级队比，而是跟Fanatics比。

最出人意料的系统性真相：足球史上最成功的商业创新，可能不来自英超豪门，而来自一支85,000人小城市的球队——因为只有他们别无选择地被迫放弃了"我们是一家足球俱乐部"这个身份认同。皇马、曼联、拜仁太成功，成功让他们无法想象"也许我们不该是一家足球俱乐部"。稀缺，逼出了想象力。丰饶，压死了它。

一个小彩蛋式的观察

注意Merwan两次微妙的语言选择： 1. 他自称"just an employee"，反复强调自己没有股权。 2. 他把公司决策形容为"family-owned business consensus"——everything's discussed together。

这跟他描述的Como Senate完全同构。他不是在管理一家俱乐部——他是在把印尼家族企业(Djarum Group风格)的决策文化嫁接到欧洲足球。这可能是这个故事里最被低估的杠杆：一种在意大利土壤里完全陌生的、来自东南亚华人家族商业智慧的"集体决策 + 长期主义 + 无finger-pointing"文化。日本100年计划、印尼家族senate、意大利足球俱乐部——一种从没被合成过的化合物。

📝 转录文本

Cold Open

So Champions League for you, it's going to open you up — prime time audience — to 500 million, let's say, people who watch Champions League globally. Yeah, it's almost like you get one shot, one opportunity, right? As Eminem once said.

I wasn't expecting the conversation to take that turn. Me neither.

How do we sustain ourselves? How do we try to survive? Maybe we shouldn't be a football club. Maybe we should be a sport tourism destination. This is Como. The population is only 85,000. Our capacity, if we can make it 15,000, we're already ecstatic. This is a place where when I came in, there was 1.9 million tourists a year. Last year was 5.6 million.

We were in the media that we were in competition with another Italian team for a British player. I was like, well, you know what? Why don't I just call the president of the other club and say, do you want him? If you want him, you go for it. We'll back out. But if you buy him, give me one of yours. And he was like, thanks for calling. I appreciate this. We'll let you know.

I met with UEFA. We talked about it. There's no precedence of where we are. But I told them, you know, don't hesitate to fine us. If we are in Europe, we want to become good members of the association. We want to become a model club. If you have to fine us, fine us. We don't want to be treated like this.

Welcome / A Business, Not Just a Team

Merwan, it is so great to have you on the show. Thank you so much for joining us in the studio.

Thanks for having me. It's great to have you in the studio.

For anyone that doesn't know — I think it'll resonate pretty strongly if they follow this show because we're banging on all the time about football clubs being more than just a product on the pitch. What you guys have done in a rather short space is actually build not just now a high-performing entity on the pitch, but a business around it.

Yep. Well, it was kind of like when we were in the second division, Serie B, when we realized the revenue coming from the league was so small. I don't say so small — it was five million. That's how much you get from the TV rights in the second division in Italy. And the average team was spending 11 to 15. So it's kind of like there's a significant gap.

If you are Palermo, where you have a stadium of 50,000 and you can fill it up with 35,000 people, maybe you can bridge that gap. Our stadium was 5,000 in the second division, you know, so there were a lot of gaps that we had to figure out.

The Pivot: "Maybe We Shouldn't Be a Football Club"

So when we tried to start exploring how to move forward — and then there was the year when Cesc Fàbregas came as a player, all that kind of stuff — it's like, okay, I think the goal is to get out of Serie B, second division, go to the first division because I think the opportunity will be bigger and easier there. But having said that, we never took that for granted. We tried to set up: how do we sustain ourselves, how do we try to survive in this limited environment?

That's when we came about — maybe we shouldn't be a football club. Maybe we should be a sport tourism destination. Because we saw how many Englishmen were always coming to Como and they were always drinking and everything like that. Well, why don't we push it? Why don't we promote it?

And it was by chance we met a bunch of guys from Isle of Wight. They're the Como chapter, Como fans chapter. And we're like, what are you guys doing here? Oh, we've been coming here for like 10 years. We came to a game to Milan to watch an AC Milan game, but AC Milan played away. And we found out the closest team to Milan that was playing was Como. So they came, they drank on the lake, and two minutes later they were in the stadium. And there were like 500 fans at the time. We were in the fourth division or two division — I don't know what it was, it was before my time. And they enjoyed coming. And ever since then, we have the Isle of Wight chapter until today.

The Content Strategy: "Humans of Como"

And now, as we pivoted and started promoting more about the tourism, about the community in Como, rather than about the football — because we were in the second division, there was no point talking about any of your players. Nobody would know unless you're in this 85,000 people community, which is our city.

That was it. So we said, okay, let's talk about how beautiful the place is. Let's talk about how beautiful the people are.

I was inspired by an Instagram account — I think it was Humans of New York — which was talk about everyday guys, about their stories, about their lives. So we said, for the social media team, I want journalist type. Not journalists — just journalist wannabes who wants to meet people, talk to people, interview people and tell their stories.

And I swear to God, we found so many good stories in Como. People who've been coming to the stadiums, three generations coming together. How proud they are when they're actually bringing their grandchildren for the first time. I met a guy who sold his car to help the team rent a team bus for the season because the team was bankrupt and the team couldn't travel away, and all these fans kind of like chipped in together to do all this stuff.

So those have become the heart of our content. And slowly, we got bigger and bigger and bigger and bigger as the team became more popular. It remains the same — it's never about football, it's always about a sports tourism destination.

From Bankruptcy to Building an Asset

But there was probably a bit of work at the beginning, right? Because the club has been bankrupt — am I correct in saying — three times in the last 20 years?

Yeah, more or less.

So there's an element to being able to do this, but then there's also an element of just stabilizing what needed to be done from a sports entity perspective to even build yourself into the position to start creating that value around a football product. You can never have one or the other, right? It still has to be a combination.

Yeah. At the end of the day, your biggest cost center is the football. So the football has to become an asset at some point. So when we realized that we wanted to be a sports tourism destination, then we realized the product — which is the football — has to be worthy of a tourism destination.

That's when we changed the mindset. At the time, we were playing very low block, very defensive, very Italian in a way — Catenaccio kind of way. And we said we want to be something different. And that's when we heard Cesc's ideas of how to play football, how to become more entertaining. We created this playbook called the Lake Como DNA kind of thing — playing football the Lake Como way. That's pretty much what's fundamental to it, and that's who we are today.

We try to play attractive football. Win or lose, you can't control, but you can try to be entertaining. It's almost like we want to be the LA Lakers of Italian football. That's the goal, so to speak. And as we do that, we identify opportunities. We didn't know that — well, why don't we get into that?

Performance vs. Business: Two Sides of the Same Coin

Can I ask you, when I listen to you, it's really interesting because it does differ to a lot of other leaders, execs in sport who very much orient around performance. It's about performance on the pitch. And yours is like — I think you said it was a "sports tourism destination." Style of play, win or lose, it's attractive and it's exciting. But it's not like we are here to win and performance above.

It's not my job. That's the sports team's job to win.

Yeah, but if you manage the budgets well, you allow for larger transfer budgets, you put a good team on the pitch, you orient performance more than I think you're giving yourself credit for.

Yes, both go hand in hand. But for me, the way we see it was like, okay — when we got promoted, the first thing we needed to do was realize that from a data perspective, the team wasn't good enough. So we know we need to invest. It's almost like a startup. You come in, you have to change your entire team. We can't change everybody at once. So we tried to do 11 players in the first window and another 11 in the second window, just trying to manage the cash flow.

Midway into the season, we realized we could find certainty a lot faster not to be relegated. If we invest a little bit more, in different kinds of quality players — younger players who can become investments. At the end of the day, the player trading model is still one of the key revenue sources for any football club.

We jumped from number 14 or 15 in the league to become number 10 — the highest the team's ever finished in like 20 years. In our second season, we saw again if we changed a few things, we could go even further. We made seven or eight more changes. Our target was sixth or seventh, and we finished fourth.

The overperformance is great — something we always be thankful for — but it's not something we were expecting, because that's unpredictable.

Getting Ready for Europe

And just to be clear to people, that means you're in the Champions League next year. So the minute you enter European football, it all changes from a business perspective — cost controls, everything.

I remember in January last season, somehow we had to play 13 or 14 games in the space of like a month. It was very tight — three games in a week, which was uncommon for a team not in Europe. I think we had the least rest day in the entire league for a team not competing in European competition — 2.67 days — because we were playing in the cup and the league, and our fixtures got congested because the other teams were playing in the European competition.

That's when Cesc said to me: this is a good test. If we go to Europe, we need to prepare for this — less training time, less recovery time.

So with that in mind, we know we're in for a very, very tough time this season. The squad has to be stronger, the staff has to be smarter, and at the same time we have to follow European regulations. In Serie A it doesn't matter whether you have homegrown players or not. Our team only had one Italian in the team last season. This season, that means we can only register 17 non-Italians in the Champions League. So we have to balance the team somewhat, and it comes with challenges.

Do you have too many calendar games? I'm not educated enough to answer that. One would say — I can hear arguments — there's too much. But at the same time, teams performing very, very well at the very top have to compete at the same schedule.

Fàbregas: From Player to Coach to Shareholder

When Fàbregas came in — he came in as a player. Did you guys always have a plan with him? Was there a look that it would evolve into him being a coach and manager?

No, not at all. It was a relationship that built. We were just talking a lot, and then I realized this is one smart guy, and he decided to retire because he was tired of playing the kind of football the team was playing. At the time I wasn't running the team — I was mostly just doing the business.

And I started asking questions more and more about what's wrong with the team — not what's wrong, but how can we become better? How can we become more competitive? One thing that really annoyed me was that there was never a measurement of why should this person be paid a certain amount of money, why the other one isn't? What is the control? What is the guarantee? What are the metrics?

That's when I met Billy Beane. I was very, very intrigued with what he has done, and we tried to apply the same analytics model into football based on Billy's software and program. And then we met Tony Bloom and everybody else. We realized — oh, there is a way to measure. Will it be accurate? Maybe not 100%, maybe not 80%, maybe not 60%. But it gives you guidelines. It gives you ways to think. It's the same as you're doing investments. You read all the metrics. At the end of the day, the execution still has to be perfect.

The "Senate": Decision-Making by Council

We try to be as educated in our guesses as much as possible. And then we try to create — for lack of a better word — a council, a Senate in terms of decision making. It's not dependent on one guy who knows everything about football. It's very diverse backgrounds. Just like our parent company back home. The group that I work for is a family-owned business, and everything's always in consensus. Everything's discussed together. Every decision is best made together, so that if it's wrong it's everybody's mistake; if it's good it's everybody's decision. You don't have this finger-pointing mentality within the group. It's helped us a lot.

Has that worked in football, in those decisions?

Yes. So far, so good.

For every player transfer, it's like voted on by a committee?

Yes. First we start with the data. The data is kind of like the filter of everything. Even when Cesc has players that he likes or that were recommended to him, the first thing he does is check the data. Then we have what we call the behavior analyst — we assess how they behave on the field. Then we go to the financial test. Then the fit test — whether they fit into our playing style, into what the roles the coach needs. Once it fits all that, then he goes to the coaching team, before he gets to Fàbregas.

I'm not being rude — are you able to move with the speed and agility that you need to with that decision-making structure?

So far so good. In December '23, when we tried to push to Serie A, we bought eight players using that structure — maybe more.

What was the most contentious buying decision?

There was one player that we thought we had to move because the data was just not very good. But he turned out today to be one of our best players because Cesc said, "No chance. This guy, I see something in him. We just have to work with him more."

Can Cesc push a deal through?

Within reason, yes. If it's like 30 million, that's too much — we can't gamble on 30 million. But if it's three, five million — yeah, why not?

There's also an element where you give autonomy to the coach if he's going, "The data shows him being a left back and actually I'm going to play him as a left wing back."

Absolutely. He always says: the team that he plays for is, let's say, a less dominant team. If we move him here, he could be a different player. Our data can translate that somewhat, but you have to give people like him with all that experience benefit of the doubt as well. The data is never blind. For us, the data is like 60-70% guidance. And then after that, it's up to the human how to interpret it.

That's why we have a Senate. To form the Senate, you have to have not just yes men — everybody has to have a strong opinion. But then you also have to have people who are wise enough to listen. That's Ocean Roberts for us. He's older than everybody else. He was Patrick Vieira's assistant coach at Crystal Palace. Technical director of Morocco, technical director of Wales, wealth of experience. He's a UEFA coach instructor — the guy who gave the coaching license to Arteta, to Thierry Henry. When he speaks, we all value his opinion.

Succession, Sustainability, and Losing Your Best People

You mentioned that you've benefited from the great work Jamestown Analytics, Tony Bloom, etc. have done. One thing Brighton and Brentford have done so well is that the people involved in those projects are important — but not the most important. If you lose one of them, the whole thing doesn't fall apart; the system and the structure are the strength.
