When you've got someone like Cesc who's been excellent and allowed you to do so much — how difficult is it to balance the control and influence, giving Cesc everything he needs, but also recognizing you've got one of the best young coaches in Europe and there's a high chance he'll leave?

It's the same as any — I came from a creative industry. When you have a good, strong creative director in an agency, usually these two guys will be the ones you bring to all the clients to do all the pitches. At some point, a bigger agency will pick him up or he'll become a movie director. It's the evolution of lifestyle. So we always talk with Cesc: at some point you will leave, and who will come to replace you? He's also a shareholder. He has interest in how to sustain the club beyond him. And actually beyond any of us.

Even myself — as I work there, I'm always consistently talking to my bosses. Anything happens to me, talk to this guy — he could be a candidate to replace me. Or talk to this guy.

Do you have an equity stake in it?

No. I'm an employee. I've been working with the family since 2018 internally inside the company, but we're working with them since 2004, 2003.

Football Manager, Real Life: How the Transfer Budget Gets Set

I spent most of my youth — sadly, I look back now and slightly ruined — playing Football Manager. And the first thing you get is a transfer budget. Who sets it?

It's usually started with what our goals are. What's the goal for the year? How much revenue can we generate? With this kind of revenue, how much are you comfortable to put into the team?

Revenue Breakdown: - Serie B TV rights: €5m - Serie A TV rights: €40–60m (depending on finish) - European competition: another €30–40m - Merchandising last year: €2.7m → targeting €15m this year → €30m next → €70m the year after - Ticket revenue: from €5–6m → €14–15m (only 12,000 seats)

Our goal is that the non-football revenue exceeds the football revenue within the next three years.

Not adding many seats — we just added experiences. Our stadium is so small. We only had about 100 hospitality initially two years ago. So we said, dude, the lake is on the stadium. You see all those villas behind the stadium? Can we rent those and turn them into hospitality before the game? People can just go have drinks and eat there and then walk to the stadium within three minutes. So we rented about four villas. We rented a boat before the game for drinks. Today we have about a thousand hospitality facilities. Tickets have gone up from €80 to €2,500 for the bigger, nicer areas — while maintaining that the tickets for the community stay at €20.

So last season was around €100 million. This season we're targeting €150 to €190 million.

Is Football Actually a Good Business?

So it is a good business? Because it was always a question of "is sports ownership good business?" and it's been TBD.

Fucking misery guts in the corner, mate. We've gone — but you gotta also see that we're only two years old. We're in a startup. I mean, how long before Uber turned a profit? The beauty of our business is that we don't have the revenue stream — yet. So you have to identify and create new revenue streams.

For example, our sponsorship last year was Uber. Uber didn't pay us a lot of money, but we're allowed to operate and manage Uber in northern Italy. So technically, in August, through a partnership, we will be launching Uber Maseratis for Como. We'll be launching the Uber boats that operate around the lake. And that revenue will be bigger than most any sponsorship deals.

That's your point. That's the development of the modern sponsorship package that a lot of organizations don't understand. Traditional sports entities go: I understand you've got inventory here. I'm going to put Uber here and give you a few of our hospitality seats. Isn't that great, Uber? But really what they want is better access and distribution across the region. They want an operating partner.

Our sponsorship with Uber was less than a million last year in cash upfront. Today, we're signing a deal for over 10 million for the same space. What are we going to become? Customer acquisition partners.

The Ticketing Platform, the E-Wallet, the 900 Touchpoints

There's only two ticketing platforms in Italy that operate in the football space. We'll be the third. We created our own — all last year's, blockchain-based. It's a ticketing, merchandising and F&B ecosystem. Anything you buy on this platform, you'll get 5% cashback.

We didn't have a finance permit to operate in Italy to store the cashback. But we wanted this e-wallet concept so the money can go there, and then the user can go to their next café to buy a cigarette if they want to, and the money rotates. So we needed a partner who can allow us to do that — which is what we just did. We will be rewarded each time someone converts, becoming a user of the platform, thus an acquisition customer for their product.

Because it's a ticketing platform — it doesn't sit just with us. We've offered this to other clubs. Our goal within the next year: six other clubs in Italy on our platform. Within two or three years: 30 clubs. The bank partner will have enjoyed new acquisitions, and we will have benefited from the new EDCs we helped them install.

And one of the unique things — when we first came to Como, we didn't know what we were doing. A lot of businesses over there were serving the tourists, and one of the biggest problems was managing inventory. Moms and pops shops. They have to sell to tourists — that means they have to buy and take risks. So why don't we say: we'll make those souvenirs for you. We'll make the merch for you. We'll give it to you on a consignment basis.

What have we got now? We have over 900 touchpoints — moms-and-pops shops, pharmacies, hotels that distribute these products. Do we make a lot of money? No, but we have the relationship. Now when we work with finance partners, these are touchpoints we've created for them to put EDCs, new things. For us, this model, we want to replicate it in as many towns in Italy as possible.

Player Trading as an Asset Class

When you think about the 150, 160 you said — what is the single biggest cost line?

Players. Probably 50–60%, maybe more.

Can you monetize the players? Of course, because the players that we buy are of high value. Just to give you an illustration:

  • Assane Diaw, 20-year-old left winger: bought for €12m over winter, offer of €60m in the summer.
  • Another one this year: bought for €18m+ bonuses, offer of €55m, but we understand his value is about €75m, so we hold.
  • A player bought for €2m in the winter, loaned to Ipswich last season: loan fees and bonuses of €2.6m, and we have an offer for €10m already.

The bookkeeping standards — the longer you keep the player, potentially the higher the profit, because you count off the book value as amortization reduces it.

From the player standpoint, we can see player trading is one way of monetizing them. But at the same time, for a player like Nico Paz — while we have him, what businesses can we create with him? Not just merchandising, but what are businesses relevant to him that we can create and build? Assane Diaw is from Senegal, he was in the World Cup — maybe we can create a product with him to target a certain market, certain demographics, certain geography.

Lake Como > Como Football: The Lifestyle Merchandise Strategy

We recognized very early — we're too small. Como is too small for a market. We're not Arsenal that's known all over the world. Lake Como is more famous than our Como football team. So when we started creating merchandise, we were creating more Lake Como merchandise than Como football merchandise.

When I look into our revenue — the €13.7, €14 million of merchandising last year — only 38% were jerseys. The rest — if you open our shop, shop.comofootball.com — you'll see we sell Como hiking collection, Como sailing collection, Como running club, Como racing team. We don't have any of those. They're just lifestyle products that sell extremely well.

Our chief brand officer, Rigi Villaseñor, owns a brand called Rhude. He was also the creative director of Bally at some point. Rhude is very well positioned premium urban lifestyle. His t-shirt retails about $200, $300. His hat is $285. His clothing is worn by Jay-Z, Paul Pogba, Dybala.

I said to him — Rhude, in the last game of our first season in Serie A, fourth jersey, can we put Rhude on it instead of a sponsor? Let's see how much we can sell. We sold it for €275 (usually €95). We sold 2,000 units. Everything was gone in less than seven days — sold to the US, sold here at Harrods. This year we did more — 3,000 units at €275, all money to charity (top-line revenue, not profit) because I wanted to see how much we can push with this brand.

The Match Shirt vs. the Lifestyle Brand

There's a big difference between doing that with a fourth jersey and dramatically shifting the product of the first-choice match shirt. That's where you have the balance between fans going "over-exploitation" versus you being a lifestyle brand creating premium.

When we were in Serie B, we made sure the new jersey lasted for two seasons. So fans don't have to buy a new one every year. Also on the fact that we knew we couldn't sell much anyway — we were a Serie B club. But we wanted to learn how to do the supply chain, learn to do everything. Even though we had Adidas as a partner, we told Adidas: don't give us money at the time. We want you to give us your products and allow us to customize them and do all the designs. That's why we created our own shoes.

Now with Rhude, we started approaching other clubs. What if we take it into a lifestyle product? Would you like to have Rhude and your club hoodies, t-shirts, leather jackets? We've managed to get 14 clubs signed up. The first will be launched July 27th — a big Premier League team. And it will be followed by — four teams in the Premier League, five teams in Italy, four teams in France, two in the Middle East, three in the MLS.

We've just added another one where we will actually manage all their e-commerce and manage their physical stores. So we're not interested in the multi-club ownership model, but we want to become the multi-club servicing model. We've gone through this process. We've made many, many, many mistakes. So all the smaller clubs, they don't have the resources to fuck up like we did. Take our learnings — at no cost. We'll cover all production costs. We'll just do revenue share with you.

Academy, Development, and the 10-Year Plan

Do you ever worry that there's so much time being spent on merch, building a premium brand, which takes away from the ability to invest in an academy?

When we started, we had no academy three years ago. Today we have 11 players in the Italian Youth National Team Program from zero within three years. And this is just the Italians — we also have Dutch, Portuguese, Latvian internationals.

Why do I talk about the non-football more than the football? Because the football is just a playbook that everybody can do. Everybody's been doing it — we're also doing it. We're working very, very fast.

Part of the Senate, we have: - Head of recruitment — the first hire after meeting Tony Bloom and Billy Beane. - Head of "beyond today" — someone to run the 10-year plan (similar to Japanese 100-year plans — I'd love to have a 100-year plan, so none of us can tell me whether I was wrong or not). - Head of development — coaches the coaches and creates individual programs. - Head of performance — injury recovery, etc. - First team coaches and a similar setup on the women's side.

We create two operations as two separate, almost like two separate companies with very clear KPIs. It's not wins and losses — because wins and losses, there's so many variable factors — but development and growth. How do we play? Did we create more chances than we didn't?

Cesc actually writes a game plan. He submits to the board 24 hours before every game. So the owners are able to watch the match and understand what to look for, rather than saying "God, that striker doesn't score" — his job wasn't to score, his job was to create this decoy. Are we playing a two-plus-three on the build up? Two-plus-four? Who's dropping to open the space? Who is running where?

After the game, Cesc writes a post-game report. Our data team creates a prediction of how the match would be, separately, independently of the coaching teams. At the end, we share all the data with each other. So the owners always know: ah, against Juve we have a 20% chance of winning, 40% chance of drawing. Our xG in the match was way higher than what we predicted. So it wasn't about winning or losing — it's about how we perform.

Italian Regulations and the Cash-Flow Business

But for you from an academy perspective, building the foundations is really important — because if you're going out to market to buy Italian players, that is a far more expensive endeavor than international players. Domestic players always have a premium. And you cannot pay in installment.

In Italian regulations, for some reason you're obliged to put a bank guarantee for however much you want to pay someone. Painful. So if you're buying a €40m player, fork up your cash flow for that €40m. Whereas if you're buying from Belgium or the Premier League, you can pay in six installments, five installments — whatever you can negotiate. So it's a cash flow management business as well.

What a bad rule.

The Stadium: Renovation, Rusticity, and Community

As you're growing and you've got European standards, how do you address the stadium?

We renovate. Right now we're already in a process of renovating to allow us to play in the Champions League by September. When we got promoted to Serie A, we had to meet the Serie A standard of 12,000 people from 7,000. Now we're doing it again to meet UEFA regulations.

The long-term goal is a nicer stadium — 1927, hasn't really been renovated much. We've filed our permission to build a new stadium. So far so good, fingers crossed. We can start building 2027.

How much does it cost to build a new stadium?

Ours will not be a lot — focused more on hospitality. About €64 million all in. Como's population is only 85,000. Our capacity, if we can make it 15,000, we're already ecstatic. What I want on that lakefront view is six restaurants, one rooftop bar, we're good. That's going to generate about €25 million per year — before the food court, before all the other hospitality on game day.

The first question we asked was: does the city want this? Do the people want it? What can we put in there that is of benefit to you? So one thing was a community health center — a high-performance sports organization can provide excellent health care to the community. Two: cheap rent. Como has become very, very expensive for people to open businesses in the city center. So on the East Stand, the whole East Building, for local businesses from Como — very competitive, low rate. We want to become a place where people feel that our presence is useful for them. The lake-facing side is the commercial side. The rest has to be beneficial for the community.

Stars, Celebrities, and the Como Effect

You've done really front-facing cool work with people like Chris Pine, and I think you've had Hugh Grant come in. You've built that kind of celebrity status around the club. How important is combining all of those worlds?

I think Rigi, our chief brand officer, has been quite instrumental in bringing in a lot of people — a lot of them are his colleagues or his fans because he dresses up those people. Also, it's the location. If you've been to Como, after four days you're going to ask what else is there to do aside from sitting around a lake. These guys usually go there when they are promoting a film. The concierge would recommend: if you have nothing else to do, you can go watch a game. And we would always get a call from the hotel — "so-and-so is here, would like to come to a game" — and we'd be happy to host.

That's become more and more the case now that we have a private members club — a house on the lake, "the club on the lake." NFL players coming, they want to hang out, throw a party, but they want to be private. Fine — become members of the private club. We just gave them free membership, and hopefully they come back.

Beyond Como: Multi-Club Servicing Across Italy

The big challenge you have investing in these types of assets is understanding what you can control to build value and what you can't. Italian football has its challenges — financial, historical, ownership, infrastructure. How much of what you're doing in Como is capped by the broader environment you sit in?

I think we can look insularly in Italy. If we can help every club in Italy grow, my market is bigger.

We own a website / media platform called Destination Culture — about football lifestyle, football travel, traveling to Italy, discover which football teams to visit, where to eat, where to go. It's in English. Ex-Daily Mail people running it — about 10 people strong right now. The next evolution is to marry it with our travel division.

My goal is to start offering the services to all the other Italian clubs which are also tourism destinations, like Verona. What a beautiful city — Romeo and Juliet. What if you can go there and experience the authentic Italian football culture with easyJet only £25 to fly in sometimes? Very affordable, lucrative business. And then on top of it, I can sell Verona jerseys and souvenirs, which we will be producing as well.

We own a shop called Curva — meaning the North Stand or South Stand where the tifosi, the hardcore fans, are. It's basically a fashion brand inspired by football culture. What do we sell the most? AC Milan stuff. Bologna. Ayers Roma items. We buy it from them: "Guys, you can't sell it? Give it to us. We'll sell it for you."

I always borrow the Disneyland analogy — I want to be like Disney. You have the core. But what if I can mitigate the risk of having our own football team by servicing 20 other football clubs?

The Competitor Phone Call

Do any other football clubs have an issue working with you because you are maybe a competitor?

No, no, no. Even today we were subject in the media that we were in competition with another Italian team for a British player. They were saying we might want to buy him, they might want to buy him. I said, "Well — why don't I just call the president of the other club and say, do you want him? If you want him, you go for it. We'll back out. Thank you. But if you buy him, give me one of yours." And he was like, "Thanks for calling. I appreciate this. We'll let you know by end of day. And if we're not moving through, these are the other targets we were going after — we're happy to share that with you."

This kind of win-win relationship within clubs being open — it helps all of us growing. I have a lunch next week with another chairman of another club to start managing their clothing brand together.

Adidas is one of our partners right now — amazing brand, but they will not pay us the same amount they pay Real Madrid or Liverpool. Of course they're bigger. So for the smaller ones like us — why don't we all make one of our own and split the revenue together? Some clubs say: why don't we make the kit? These are all conversations which we would not have had if we don't sit down together with the other clubs and say: what's your problems and how can we help you?

I'm sorry, maybe I'm incredibly naive — but if you call up your competitor in a league and say "if you want them, take them," if the person you sacrificed is the best one and will perform better on the pitch, are you not letting your team have suboptimal product?

We're looking at a target for us. Every target has a percentage. He's a top 10%. You want this top 10%? I'm not fucking auctioning with you. You can take that one. I'll take the other 10%. I know you have two 20%s. I can turn into 10%. So we are working based on numbers already.

We have a player named Mark Oliver Kempf — we bought him from Hertha Berlin for €1.5m. If low price is a sign of lack of quality, this guy will not be the best defender in terms of winning the highest aerial duels in Italy. But he was. So sometimes it's not about the money — it's about the quality of the players.

I'm not saying price denotes quality. I'm saying price denotes conviction. What you should ask when you're buying him is: fine, we paid 1.5, but would we have been willing to pay 6? Because if you'd been willing to pay 6, he's the key missing piece.

The supply is never that scarce. 25 players in a team, top five leagues, if I'm looking for a center back — each team has four, 20 teams in a league, quite a lot of options. Then you go to the youth teams, then to lesser-known teams. It's up to us to discover them and turn them around.

The US Playbook, the Sheep and the Shepherd

A lot of this plays to the US playbook that is often justified for some of the mad prices being paid — "there's so much untapped potential in some of these clubs and they don't optimize their revenues." Then they come in, own them for a few years, and I don't think they ever really get off the ground. They don't begin to exploit half of the things you guys are doing.

Does this become the classic shepherd-and-the-sheep thing? Football's generally pretty bad at being the shepherd, but clubs are very good at being the sheep. It just needs that one club to really show people the way.

Could be. Look at Newcastle doing that with the energy drink — they become collaborators in the sales so they enjoy revenue from the sales of the drink. More and more clubs will do that.

I was talking to a club in Holland at the time and they were saying, "Oh, you can do all this because you are Como. You have all these destinations." I asked them — what do you guys do? "In our place, we all make cheese. The people are cheese makers." I'm like, dude, I'll be making cheese. That's what I would do right away. And how can you actually use the football club? It's almost like a shop window to what you can create.

Right now, a lot of football clubs are just renting out that shop window — Prime for example sponsoring clubs to promote their products. Why not have your products yourself, and use the club as a shop window?

Champions League: One Shot, One Opportunity

So Champions League for you — it's going to open you up, prime time audience, to 500 million people who watch Champions League globally. There's a massive chance to really nail down some of those things.

It's almost like you got one shot, one opportunity, right? As Eminem once said.

I wasn't expecting the conversation to take that turn. Can we please have the dun dun dun? I don't know if you can add that without copyright.

But we know, we realize. And we don't take this for granted. We don't think that we belong in the Champions League — I mean, forever. This could be a one-year off. So we have to act like we don't belong. If we can sustain this presence, amazing. But we as a club need to act like we don't belong here. Don't overspend. Fight like hell. Try to prepare as much as we can. But trying to take this opportunity as best as we can.

So you don't change span to accommodate the — no. That's where you get in trouble.

Sponsorship: Front-of-Shirt Deep Dive

How much is front of shirt today? I can't say it. Last year it was €750,000. Because we were just promoted in Serie B — the contract ran from our second division.

Today it's north of €10 million. Plus all the revenue share, plus all the acquisition revenue.

And that's just front of shirt. You also have sleeve, backup shirt, training shirt, third shirt, training ground sponsorship. We're talking to a technology brand right now which I'm really excited about — with them, it'll open up a lot of windows because we're just big fans of this brand.

We become their partner for growth in terms of customer acquisition. We are compensated for each acquisition they gain — both on the EDC side (business-to-business) and the consumer side. We create collections together with them on a fashion standpoint. They also own a partnership with another sport which involves automotive, which is very very popular. We will do a lot of collaborations with that team.

Financial Position and UEFA

How long does it take to make the self-sufficiency model the reality? Owner financing?

How much did you lose last year? I don't remember. I try not to remember how much I lose. But I know how much I've spent of my owner's money — they remind me every day. We were joking about financial fair play regulations. I don't need UEFA to tell me financial fair play. I got my owners telling me financial fair play tomorrow, not even like next year.

We spent quite significant amount, but we do believe we will break even and start actually no longer requiring injections from the owners within next season. End of next season — so this season and next season.

Are you compliant at the moment with financial regulations?

No. We met with UEFA and talked about it. There's no precedence of where we are. In UEFA you look at the last three years — our last two years were not applicable. And then you put us in comparison to Juventus, comparison with Aston Villa. If you understand the startup level and then the growth level — you're comparing us in the investment level, seed investment, to those actually at the growth level.

Even they say they would have to evaluate how they look into FFP with us. But I told them: don't hesitate to fine us. If we are in Europe, we want to become good members of the association, a model club like everybody else. If you have to fine us, fine us. It becomes part of our obstacles that we have to overcome. It's a regular part of business. We can negotiate, we can restructure finances. But we don't want to be treated differently.

I was surprised at that. Not as surprised as the chairman of the other club was when he said, "You can take him."

Valuation

How much did the family buy the team for?

$800,000. Plus $150,000 in debt.

Why can you buy a team for $800,000? Because you've got a lot of investment that then needs to be made on top of that.

How much have you put in now?

Quite a lot. Hundreds of millions. Let's just say to recoup it, maybe another five years to recoup all the investments — but I think we can do it.

How much do you think Como is worth today?

We've rejected bids over the high hundreds for interest.

Which valuation methodology?

I would say right now, we are going closer towards a billion because we are not just a football club. We are a multi-club servicing model. We are the boutique version of Fanatics.

How much is Fanatics worth? Their revenue is more established. We jumped from €2m to €14m in one year, one brand, one club. Hospitality — we jumped from €7m to €14m. And I do believe we can bring value to so many other clubs.

Just to build on that — from a football perspective you need to put that into context, probably seven clubs in the Premier League are worth a billion.

How much is Disney worth? However many — 100 billion. There we go. That's a goal.

Not Coming From Football

Do you think your not coming from football materially helped you build a better business?

I don't know because I only know what I know. Our owners, the company that I work for, have started with one company to then today I don't know how many companies. I've sat in meetings across industries where they have grown businesses in the same style that we are operating today — always believing where we can be, and then trying to maximize opportunities by putting collective decisions on a lot of things.

We have now the biggest private bank in — I think Southeast Asia, but definitely in Indonesia. We have a tower business, e-commerce, the equivalent of Expedia. So many consumer products. Probably close to 100 brands operating under the group. So we are all brand people to begin with. When we see something like this, the first thing we see is that this is a brand. How do we grow it?

Ivan Gazidis said that when he compared Manchester United to Coca-Cola — as brands globally they're not too far off in recognition. The revenues, well, 1% is Man Utd. Real Madrid's merchandising revenue is like €200–220 million. Compare that to Fashionblend, which is in the billions.

So for us, we cannot benchmark ourselves against second division. Cannot benchmark ourselves against first division. Who do you want to become? I want to be Disney. Then the question — the answers change. We need a hotel division, a cruise division. Would you like to open a hotel? No, but we can manage one. We're in talks for three entertainment production right now — TV shows. We'll be paying for a lot of stuff.

Real Madrid — Adidas make the minimum guarantee of $40m, plus royalties, plus whatever they sell in the shop. It's a very good deal. And Adidas, I would have to say, is one of the best partners a club could have. Real Madrid actually turning down Nike/Adidas would mean they have to produce and distribute globally themselves — supply chain crazy. So these guys take a huge amount of legwork out of it. Your 15% is a licensing deal.

For us though — if we're not sitting right at the top where we're that global global brand, and we can create something here and then build value across all of those clubs, we're owning that. The way I want to do it: work together with all the clubs to grow together. Not one club benefit, but all clubs benefit. When we talk to other clubs, I don't say "I'll give you a 90-10 split." No — whatever we make, we split.

Rapid Fire

What would you like to do today, but the budget state does not allow? I don't think that way. I always work with what I have. The cost of innovation has come down quite a lot. Human capital is more important than money capital.

A failed investment? I'm really good at forgetting my failures. Bad idea, but I don't think it was a failure in the end — we tried to grow social media following by emulating the Oprah Winfrey giving-away-stuff-for-Christmas thing. Spent a couple hundred grand. Paid for a lady with cancer who couldn't travel upstairs — rebuilt her bedroom downstairs in Newcastle. Helped a community in Ghana with transportation, motorcycles and helmets. Financed a grandmother's first-ever travel — took her for two weeks in Lake Como. We didn't gain the followers, but we gained a lot of friends. One of them, Brandon in Glasgow, is now the founder of the Scottish Como chapter — the Como Tartan Army, now 3,000 strong.

Who has not come to a Como game that you would most like? George Clooney. Mr. Como. He's mostly there in the summer, and in the summer there's no games.

But you've started the Como Cup. Correct. July 28 to August 1st. Second edition this year. Last year: Ajax, Celtic and Al Ahly. This year: Lens, Villarreal, Crystal Palace, Fenerbahçe and Al Ula from Saudi.

Which fashion brand for a collab? No — we will just make our own. Our collaboration with Como is usually with Rhude because it's part of the brand we manage.

Champions League draw — who at home? Real Madrid at home. It would be me starting when we were in the fourth division with an audience of 200. Suddenly having Real Madrid in our stadium — that would be epic.

Away? Barcelona away — which we played and we lost 5-0.

Which other club in Serie A has the best hospitality? Depends how you define it. Best food — the southern clubs: Napoli, Lecce. Great food. If you come to our stadium, it's different. Italian infrastructure has problems — the stadiums are old, dated. When they renovate, they choose to become modern — in which, to me, you're losing the essence of being Italian. When you spend €60–70m trying to look like SoFi, you look like a wannabe.

Our stadium looks like a palace. We made it look rustic. We made it look as old as possible. In our suites, all the furniture is from the 1700s and 1800s. We go to flea markets and identify them. When you come to a Como vs. Napoli game, the food is from Naples. Como vs. Genoa, the food is from Genoa. Italy has that storytelling element of culture and cuisine — so we double down on that. At the club on the lake we have a two-Michelin-star chef cooking in the restaurant.

Final one — what surprised you most about what you've learned in this role? I'd like to say I'm just an employee. I can do whatever my boss tells me to do. If I don't know how to do it, I'll learn it — and it seems that I can learn quite a lot of things quite fast. We just bought a noodle brand in Indonesia — like the McDonald's of Indonesia. I said, "Boss, when do you want me to come over and take over that? Noodles is a lot easier than dealing with egos like this." Selling cigarettes, fine. Selling motorcycles — we own EV business in Indonesia. Whatever, I'll do it.

Merwan, thank you so much for joining us. This has been such a broad, wide-ranging discussion. Eminem clips. Thank you so much. You've been amazing. Thank you.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 反常识观察一：他们把足球"降格"，反而让足球变得更好
Merwan最颠覆的举动，不是他做了多少商业化，而是他**主动让足球在Como的商业版图中变小**。他公开说："也许我们不该是一家足球俱乐部，而是一个体育旅游目的地。"这句话在意甲圈子里几乎是异端——但它是所有杠杆的原点。
一旦足球不再是唯一的收入引擎，它就获得了一种奢侈品：**免于生存焦虑的决策自由**。你可以选择攻势足球而不是Catenaccio，不是因为必须赢，而是因为要让来Como的英国游客愿意花250欧元买张票、并在离开时买一件跑步俱乐部T恤。Nottingham Forest去年跳进了反向的陷阱——他们把欧战当作救命稻草，然后被救命稻草反噬。Como从一开始就拒绝让任何一场比赛承担救命责任。
跨领域看，这个模式其实有名字：**Amazon的AWS-Retail结构、Costco的会员费模型、研究型大学用本科生学费养博士**。核心业务(足球/零售/教研)本身不需要赚钱，只要够好；周边现金流(旅游/云/会费)负责喂养野心。Como无意间加入了这个反直觉俱乐部——他们的对手在拼命让足球赚更多钱，Como让别的东西赚

item.title: [Invest Like The Best] How to Raise a Few Billion Dollars
item.dc:creator: Invest Like The Best
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的洞察："信任"其实是"恐惧"的倒影，而不是"相信"的延伸

作者说了一句被大多数人忽略的话：相信 ≠ 信任。你相信飞行员是合格的，却依然恐惧飞行。募资失败最常见的死法，不是别人不相信你的逻辑，而是他们相信了却依然不敢。这意味着——做募资的人真正的对手不是"怀疑",而是"未被处理的恐惧"。

由此推导出一个令人意外的结论："厌恶风险 vs 偏爱风险" 是一个伪命题。作者一针见血——没有人真的"偏爱风险"，只有人擅长把风险合理化到一半，从而允许自己伸手去够那份奖励。赌场、创业、婚姻、加密货币——本质上都是同一台"合理化引擎"在运转。这也解释了为什么最聪明的投资人往往在事后复盘时说："当时我真的没觉得那么危险。"——他们不是勇敢，他们只是神经系统更善于自我催眠。

二、跨领域连接：募资 = 政治竞选 + 心理治疗 + 神秘主义

作者不经意间打通了三个看似无关的领域：

  • 政治学的"hard reelect number"（铁票基数）——你天然能拿到的钱决定了你的天花板：首轮融资几乎等于最终规模的 1/2 到 1/3。这不是营销学，这是选举数学。
  • 戏剧治疗的 Karpman Drama Triangle（受害者/迫害者/拯救者三角）——每一场募资会议本质上都是在诊断对方"扮演的角色"，然后决定你是要做"英雄解药"还是"共情治疗师"。
  • 神秘主义者/催眠师的技巧——"我不存在，我只作为一个客体存在于你的心里"。这是募资的最深层内功：不是推销自己，而是在对方的意识中住下来。

这三者的共通点被作者压缩成一个物理学式公式：说服 = 欲望 − 恐惧。而最惊人的部分是：信任是恐惧的疫苗。当信任足够高时，欲望甚至可以为零——这就是 Benchmark 周二晚上发邮件、周三上午封盘的原因：稀缺 × 极致信任 = 欲望不必存在。

三、隐藏的系统性真相："差异化"是一种"献祭"，而市场只奖励愿意流血的人

作者说了一句杀气极重的话："Great differentiation requires great sacrifice."（伟大的差异化必须以巨大的牺牲为代价。）

然后他举了一个残酷的例子——2019 年一大批 VC 公开承诺"永不投资武器"，2025 年这些人正在领投国防科技。他们没有失败在业绩上，他们失败在了"一致性"上——而一致性正是信任的原子结构。

这里有一个跨越到品牌学、宗教、乃至个人身份的更深真相：任何未经献祭的"Why"都会退化为营销话术，而市场终究会在长期定价出这种伪装。这也是为什么"why"不能是包装出来的——它必须能让你真正拒绝金钱。你拒绝的越多，你能吸引的越多。

四、意料之外的收束：募资者其实是"翻译官"

最出乎意料的观察藏在最后——作者把优秀的 IR/募资合伙人比作"国务卿"。而国务卿的核心能力不是理解总统在想什么，而是理解外国在想什么。这颠覆了 99% 从业者对自己的定位：

你不是在把自己的老板"卖"给市场，你是在把"市场的语言"翻译回老板的世界。

这解释了为什么许多前投行家、前 deal partner 转型募资都失败——他们太懂"总统"了，却完全不懂"中国"。真正的秘密是：募资人的稀缺性来自于他/她是唯一同时会说两种母语的人。

📝 转录文本

How to Raise a Few Billion Dollars

Invest Like The Best · 嘉宾访谈

开场：Money moves at the speed of trust

Money moves at the speed of trust. Most people know how to get people's attention and get interest moving in their direction. That last unlock of trust actually is the magic key.

The inner game of fundraising is really about putting the person in the room as the center of all conversation. Mentalists do this. They get inside your head. Hypnotists do this. Psychologists do this.

If they have no fear — in other words, is riskless — they will do it.

一、如果重命名这本书：Money Moves at the Speed of Trust

Patrick: We were chatting before and you said if you were to rename your book, you had an idea what you'd call it. How would you rename it?

Guest: As I wrote the book, The Tao of Fundraising, I had this idea that I wanted to put something philosophical out in the world, because fundraising isn't just an idea of persuasion. It's actually a way of life — that the actual interacting with people through the lens of them as the center of your conversation is a way of life. And that way of life requires a certain level of responsibility.

When you start to get good at understanding how people work and how people react — how they will react to me, or to you, or to other personas — you can actually use that for your own selfish needs, or you can use it for good. So I call it The Tao of Fundraising.

But in the end, I don't know how many people actually care about that philosophy. What most people want to know is: how do you get money? And so I think I probably would have just renamed it — Money moves at the speed of trust — because the entire book really is about that.

The entire book is about how money pools in areas, and people hold on to it, and resources. We can put a lot of words around what actually motivates people to move in a direction — and that's really important — and making sure that you say, "Hey, I want the money to move in this direction, not that direction."

The real trick is that most people know how to get people's attention and get interest moving in the direction. That last unlock of trust actually is the magic key.

二、Belief ≠ Trust：说服的最后一公里

So many people do such a good job of using logos or logic to actually get somebody to a "yes" — and they still say no. Because it's the difference between belief and trust.

I didn't write this in my book, and I wish it was the first chapter. Belief is like: I believe you. Yes, what you're saying makes sense. I believe you. Trust is very different.

People say, "Well, how can you believe something and not trust it?" You ever go skydiving? Or people who are terribly afraid of flying — do you believe that the pilot is qualified? Of course you do.

People just don't get to that last piece where they get people to believe that this is going to work. They believe it is the right thing. They just don't trust that you're going to actually fulfill what they need. And that's very complicated.

三、从零开始：Idea + Capital 的组合

Patrick: I want to go to the situation where I'm a person. I have an idea, something I want to do. It requires capital or resources of some kind. What should people understand about that starting state?

A good idea is important — a good product, a good whatever the thing you're trying to build or sell — but people maybe underestimate the role that capital can play in making that thing happen. And therefore this skill that you've learned a lot about is unbelievably valuable, but no one really knows how to do it beyond the idea. Just orient us around that combination of idea plus capital.

Friends & Family 的真相

Guest: First you have to look and say: okay, who are the people who trust you? This is why they call it friends and family.

What is friends and family? "I trust." I don't think it's totally true. Friends and family are people who will give you money and they're not afraid to lose it. Their tolerance for loss actually may be much, much worse than an institution. So that's kind of an urban myth — that "oh, go to friends and family, cuz they'll give you the money as charity."

I think the most expensive money is borrowing money from your friends, because you don't give the money back — your friendship is not the same anymore. But they trust you, and they want to see you do well. And their desire minus fear — we talked about persuasion — their fear of losing money is subordinate to their desire for you to do well.

They know that in order for you to do well, you need resources you don't have. So as an individual, first you need to find out who trusts you. Who are the people who desire your success more than they fear?

The Hard Reelect Number

Politicians call this the "hard reelect number." The hard reelect number is some base number where, no matter what you do, they still will vote for you.

So you first have to figure out naturally: who would give me money? And then from there you sort of multiply it. If I think my friends and family are going to give me, let's say, a million dollars, then maybe my goal is to raise $2 or $3 million — because from their trust I can leverage further. You're not going to raise $100 million off friends and family of a million, but you should do better than $500,000.

That tends to be my experience. Or if your first close is a billion dollars, you tend to tap out at 2 billion, because your first close almost always is your hard-elect number.

四、Persuasion = Desire − Fear

Patrick: Before we get into campaign mechanics — I'd love you to dig deeper into this idea of persuasion equals desire minus fear.

Guest: This is like a decade ago. I was talking to one of the masters of the universe. I said, "I think it's desire minus fear." And he said, "Hey, thanks Mr. Obvious. Everything's greed and fear."

I said: "That's actually not true." The simplicity of it isn't the wisdom of the phrase — it's the nature of desire. The nature of the positive side. It's the invitation to say that people can be inspired by something that is not just self-serving.

Greed is self-serving. The human condition has so many things it desires. The ego has so many things it desires. Otherwise, we would never give money away. Otherwise, we would never do things that are generous for people. Otherwise, we wouldn't care about the environment, care about our children.

LP 的 dirty secret

When you're talking to somebody and trying to persuade them — this is maybe one of the most important lessons people seldom get right when they first start fundraising in the alternatives world — an investing professional talks to LPs, and they are just so passionate about how much money they're going to make for them.

But the dirty secret to the relationship is that very few limited partners are actually compensated on the returns that the general partner makes. Very few are. Some are compensated on IRR — and it's hard, right? The alternatives business, you have to wait for 10 years to see if something's good or bad.

So there are a lot of people who simply don't make money if you make money. So look for the other motivation they have — because they're in that spot for some reason.

Fear is the antithesis of Trust

The fear piece is also really important. Fear might as well be another word for trust. It's the anti-trust. The way to inoculate yourself against fear is trust.

The more fearful somebody is, and the more trust you can develop, the less desire they need. If they just trust me absolutely, then everything else will pale in comparison. If they have no fear — in other words, it's riskless — they will do it.

关于"risk-loving"的祛魅

Many hedge fund professionals talk about "risk-loving" vs "risk-averse." I don't really think there's such a thing as risk-loving risk aversion. I think there are only people who actually perceive there's no risk.

I think no one actually really invests with a lot of risk. People convince themselves that the risk is far less than what it really is, in order to justify the risk they're taking so they can receive the reward.

Where do we see this? Gambling. The casino. It's part of the human condition that we rationalize away the risk. Somebody says, "I'm risk-loving." No, you're not. You're just really good at rationalizing away the risk. If you know your outcome is gigantic, you just have to rationalize the risk to half of what it really is, and you'll say yes.

五、成熟阶段的募资：Consensus 的艺术

Patrick: Let's talk about applying these ideas much later on. At General Catalyst you're on fund 8. You're established. You said in the book — the path of least resistance is often that money goes to stuff already in motion. What have you learned about doing this at that stage?

Consensus 是消除恐惧的核弹

Guest: We basically set out on a campaign of consensus. One of the ways to get rid of fear is consensus. Consensus, by the way, is the hardest and maybe one of the most powerful things to move entire markets — because consensus is a macro view. By definition, when you have a macro view, you've influenced the macro world. White propaganda in its best and worst forms creates a consensus.

There are a couple of ways to go about it:

  1. The classic diffusion curve — innovator, early adopter, early majority, late majority, laggard. Lots of wonderful books written about crossing the chasm. Once you cross that gap, consensus starts to happen. And the winner on the other side is winner-take-all.

  2. Big money tends to hide behind committees. If you've got a committee of 8 or 9 people who have to vote — by definition, that's a consensus decision. I have never seen a committee make a contrarian bet, unless the group is designed to make contrarian bets. That's why it's very hard to find good venture capital firms — they tend to be contrarian firms.

如何构建 Consensus

How do you build consensus? You build it with consistency. Find people who you can do things for that are actually part of the group you want consensus around — state pension plans, consultants, sovereign wealth. Do they care about co-investment? Fees? Access? Transparency? IP? Or just being entertained? Find it, give it to them, they come back to you, build.

Now you've got a sovereign wealth plan. Now you've got a pension plan. From there, people say, "Hey, you realize this state pension plan invested." Next round, you double it. Next round, you double it. And all of a sudden General Catalyst and others start to become consensus.

The courage to lose people

Here's the thing. You have to have the courage then to lose the people who originally invested with you — because you're contrarian. You have to have the courage to get past the innovator's dilemma.

If there's a set of high-net-worth family offices, endowments, or small fund-of-funds that say, "You're now too big for me" — you're going to lose them. And you have to have the courage to do that. The only way you're not going to succeed is if you actually want to have your cake and eat it too.

Now, if your performance is so dynamic, so differentiated, then you'll be able to run the table. There are firms who do that — you can't get into the fund because their track record is so absurdly strong that you trust they'll develop these returns again.

[广告插播：Ramp · Felix by Rogo · WorkOS]

六、Benchmark 的传奇与 Trust 的三要素

Patrick: I remember once one of the partners at Benchmark — I asked them how they fundraise, and they said, "Well, we send an email on a Tuesday night, and the fund is closed on Wednesday morning."

Guest: Right. So in that case let's talk about trust. There's consistency — they consistently perform exceptionally well. Consensus — they're one of the best funds in the world. And scarcity — you don't have to have a consultant tell you.

They don't have to do anything for you. There's no reciprocity but returns. And it's really about the consensus and the scarcity that allows them the privilege of keeping their funds small and their fundraising energy very, very low.

七、三大定律 · 之一：The Law of Differentiation

Patrick: You mentioned the word differentiation. Can you explain your law of differentiation?

Guest: If anybody wants to learn the three laws of physics most important in fundraising: Law of Differentiation, Law of Trade-offs, and Law of Pipeline.

Let's talk about the Law of Differentiation. This is the law:

(Track Record + Differentiation) ÷ Complexity of Story

  • Track record isn't just your returns — it's how you behave. If you're an elected official, your track record is your voting record, or the way you show up in the media. It's your consistency.
  • Differentiation — it can be anything. "I take contrarian bets." "I only do one or two things but I'm highly operationally intensive." "I access this part of the market no one else does." "My GP commit is abnormally large."

Institutional big money has a portfolio. You want assets that aren't autocorrelated. So you're trying to add something additive to your portfolio.

Complications 是信任的敌人

Complications are usually the enemy of trust. The more I have to explain — my daughter comes back late at night, I said, "You're supposed to be back at midnight, but it's 2 a.m., explain yourself." If there's a long story of this and that — I don't trust this. But if she's smart, she says: "Oh Dad, I just blew past it. I was having fun. Sorry." I trust her.

Complications get you for two reasons. First, they dilute trust. But the second is much more commercial: I've seen with my own eyes many times where people trust and want to do something, but they can't explain it to somebody else who is making the decision. You better give them a phrase they can repeat.

The OJ Simpson Lesson

One of the most famous examples: the OJ Simpson trial. "If the glove doesn't fit, you must acquit." One of the most famous lines in the history of the world.

Do you really think that if they didn't have that, those jurors would have been okay walking out of the courtroom finding him not guilty, and having to explain to the media why? No way. But he was smart enough to say: "Okay guys, you're going to have to defend yourself to everybody. Say: I had no choice. The glove didn't fit — I had to acquit."

Changed my life. When I saw that, I thought: wow, that's what persuasion looks like. If you have complications, make sure you give them that phrase that allows you to cut through those complications.

Why is your Why?

Patrick: Would you literally go through these three variables and try to improve each one systematically?

Guest: Religiously. Track record plus differentiation divided by complexity of thought.

The differentiation piece ties back to General Catalyst. There are two kinds of people who sustain: those who find their why — why are we this big? why do we exist? — and those who just say, "Now I've got the money, I'm big, and I'll continue to push forward." Those who do the latter actually get small again.

The why can't be branding — because every why has a cost. Every differentiation has a cost. Great differentiation requires great sacrifice.

If you're willing to say, "I am never going to invest in weapons," then you're going to miss out on a generational amount of investing happening right now. Take a look — in 2019, the vast majority of VC firms said, "We will never invest in weapons." It's the hottest area right now. The same people who said they would never invest in weapons are actually now leading the weapons charge. It's unbelievable.

Great differentiation requires great sacrifice. They will never be differentiated for what they say. They've lost their consistency. In the long run, people will remember: "Hey, you said you're never going to do this. Now you are doing it." That's you losing your why.

八、三大定律 · 之二：The Law of Trade-offs

Size, Speed, and Terms.

I cannot tell you how obvious it is, and how no one believes this: if you want to raise a fund or an investment, your trade-off is how much money you want to raise, how fast you want to raise it, and what kind of terms you're going to give.

Patrick: Like quality, cost, and speed in building a house — you get to pick two?

Guest: Yeah, you get to pick two. But here it gets back to money moves at the speed of trust. Size and terms trade off for each other. Speed actually IS trust.

  • Take my size, shrink it to real scarcity → people move faster.
  • No scarcity → geometrically slower.
  • Benchmark sends the email, closes the next day → scarce money moves fast.

The terms: lowering terms may make people move slightly faster — but not because the terms improved. It's because they think you won't have capacity for them, and they've reached a tipping point of "if I don't move, I'm going to miss out." Scarcity again.

State pension plans still have four months of diligence. People miss this. Very few people I know actually legitimately use scarcity. Benchmark does. Most people lie their way through scarcity — "Oh well, if we have room…" — and the investor 100% knows they're lying, and you immediately lose credibility.

I never let my candidates play that game. They see through it, and you lose trust — and if you lose trust, you lost the velocity of money.

九、三大定律 · 之三：The Law of Pipeline

You need to run a campaign where you have a pipeline, shove it through a conversion ratio, with a certain bite size.

The only thing you care about is your conversion ratio. Because if you know your conversion ratio is 20%, then you know it's just a matter of effort. Your bite size will be a certain amount plus or minus on a bell curve.

Then all you do is say: okay, cancel Christmas, cancel Easter, cancel Valentine's. I'm just going on the road, and I'm going to meet people.

Who figured this out? The largest asset managers in the world. They're just pushing product through a conversion ratio, and the levers are: better performance, better differentiation, reduced complexity of story. Now it all ties together.

Once you get past your hard-reelect number and start getting the market — one in 10 people saying yes — then it's just a matter of how much do you want it?

Pipeline × Conversion Ratio × Bite Size = the only math that matters for a fundraise.

十、The Karpman Drama Triangle：会议中的人性

Patrick: Describe this idea of the drama triangle and personas.

Guest: There is a psychological framework called the Karpman Drama Triangle. The idea is that we as people have a hard time accepting that we have agency in our lives. So therefore, we have a victim consciousness.

  • When something bad happens → we're the victim.
  • When you're a victim → you're looking for a villain.
  • And in a sales pitch → you're also looking for a hero.

If you already know this person is feeling victimized — if you can find out what that is — it is very easy to craft a story that alleviates that pain. And if you can do that, you become heroic.

For instance, somebody complains about fees. Rather than saying "hate the game, not the player" — which isn't so useful — you can talk about how you can mitigate fees. That allows them to say: "Okay, I've found a hero for my problem."

If you can't be a solution, then have deep empathy for the villain, and move into therapist mode. I have never met somebody who isn't better off by being empathetic. In fact, it's quite the opposite — they learn to trust you.

十一、大师案例：Oprah Winfrey

Patrick: Who are the great masters of building real trust?

Guest: Oprah Winfrey. I would call Oprah's game a promotion of goodness. Goodness is the combination of kindness plus conviction.

You can be kind but have no conviction. You can have conviction and not be kind. Whenever Oprah opened her mouth, there was a kindness — but also a conviction that she stood for something.

She used institutional trust-building exercises:

  • Reciprocity — she literally gave gifts. "You get a car! You get a car!"
  • Consensus — Oprah's Book Club. The mother of all consensus. She became more powerful than any bestseller list.
  • Authority — she recruited people with authority to talk about issues that otherwise wouldn't have a stage.
  • Liking — an African-American woman beloved in the Midwest. Incredibly empathetic. Always gave something inspirational.
  • Scarcity — she really didn't show up anywhere else but Oprah. You didn't see her in advertisements or on other talk shows. If you wanted to see Oprah, you had to tune in.

十二、最常见的错误：过度依赖逻辑

Patrick: What are the most common mistakes?

Guest: The most common mistake is super easy: people overindex on logic. It blows me away.

Logos, ethos, pathos — logic, values, emotions. Simon Sinek talks about the question why, which is your emotional and intuitive engine. The frontal lobe just puts meaning to the feelings.

Two ways to remember this:

  1. Belief vs Trust — you can win the beliefs ("yes, this plane won't crash"), but I'm still scared, money's not going to move. Biggest mistake: they stay with logic, don't address the person in the room.

  2. The word rationalization — literally means to create a condition. Civilization takes something brutish and makes it civilized. Organization takes something chaotic and makes it organized. Rationalization takes something that is not rational and forces it into becoming rational. It's the thing we make up in our head to explain why we feel the way we feel.

If you want to get to yes, it's desire minus fear. But desire and fear are both emotional states. You have to win the hearts and bodies of the people. The logic will follow — logic is an output of a successful sales pitch, not the input.

一个漂亮的映射

Patrick: Is it fair to say that in your differentiation equation: fear is complications, track record is rationalization, and differentiation is really the desire piece?

Guest: That's pretty good actually. I've spent 15–20 years talking about track record plus differentiation divided by complexity of thought. It's a trinity — like any other trinity. Size, speed, and terms. Logos, ethos, pathos. Yeah, it does fit.

十三、Secretary of State：什么样的人配做募资人

Patrick: Anything we've left on the table?

Guest: The tactical part is: what kind of person are you, and what kind of person do you want to represent?

Are you looking for a salesman, a service provider, or a Secretary of State?

The Secretary of State is one of the most powerful people in the cabinet — the one department where you don't have control over the constituents. You have control over Treasury, Energy, HHS, National Security — via budgets. But you do not have control over China. You do not have control over the UK. So you need a Secretary of State who can interact with them and represent you when you're not there.

That's what a great fundraiser is. When you can send that person in and say, "Ah, this person is here. I know you speak for the president." That's really hard to do.

The Secretary of State as identity

Ask: what is the first impression you want people to make when you're not in the room?

  • Nixon → Kissinger. Real politik.
  • Clinton → Madeleine Albright. Policy wonk of the highest order, UN ambassador, deepest international policy experience.
  • Obama → Hillary Clinton. "Change you can believe in." He had little foreign experience — so he hires his opponent to show he can cross the bridge, and gets one of the most experienced people ever to sit in that chair.

Who do I want you to see? And that person has to be of industry.

When someone says, "I'd be a great fundraiser because I'm an ex-investment banker, ex-deal partner — I understand the system," I say: that's exactly the wrong person. You want someone who understands the language of what's going on. If you don't understand the Middle East as Secretary of State, but you understand policy — that's not so helpful.

The intersection of who they are and what you are is where a great IR professional lives.

[广告插播：Vanta · Ridgeline]

十四、给"未来的国务卿"的建议

Patrick: For those who want to be the Secretary of State — what should they look for in a leader?

Guest: It depends on your ambition. The easy way to think about it: what kind of candidate do you want to support?

There are people who say, "I just want to be on a winning team." There are candidates who are strong but giving them huge sums won't do great in the world — but they're great candidates who get elected. And when they're elected, there are benefits to being Secretary of State.

The more powerful your candidate, the more powerful the Secretary of State.

One of the humilities we need to commit to is that we really are only as great as the people we represent. When I was at my peak as a fundraiser, I was still representing somebody else's greatness.

So ask: is it worth the sacrifice and ego deprivation for this candidate? At different stages of your career, life, and egoic development, you'll make different decisions.

If you can find both — a magnificent capital-attractor who is also someone you ethically and emotionally adore — that's like winning the lottery.

But unlike a bad marriage, this isn't binary. If you like traveling, if you like interacting with people, if you like the game of discovery — this is about as good a job as exists. You get to play the exploration of people every minute of every day.

And the ultimate dividend? Not the money you attracted — the fact that you actually became friends with the person. It cannot be any other way. If you're authentically developing trust, you're authentically creating a friendship.

十五、The Inner Game

Patrick: How would you describe your own version of the inner game?

Guest: The inner game of fundraising is really about putting the person in the room as the center of all conversation.

I actually don't exist — but for the fact that I'm in your mind at this moment. I am just an object in your mind, being processed by all the stuff that is Patrick O'Shaughnessy. Now that I'm living in your mind, what can I do to make myself interesting? Compelling? Someone you want to meet again?

When I look at you, I see such a deep curiosity. I see incredible patience — you're allowing me these long-form explanations. So when I'm talking to you, I really don't even exist over here as much as I exist inside your head.

That's the inner game of the highest level of persuasion. Mentalists do this. They get inside your head. Hypnotists do this. Psychologists do this. Anyone engaged in a mental discussion, if they're really good, isn't just saying "here's what I am." It's: "who are you, and how do I address you in a way that is satisfying to you?"

十六、Closing：最温柔的礼物

Patrick: What is the kindest thing anyone's ever done for you?

Guest: Thank you for asking — it opens up such a cornucopia of gratitude. There's no way I'd be where I am today without the charity of others.

The hardest part was finding one moment. When I thought about the nicest thing, I thought: it's actually the body of work of niceness. That has to be my wife.

One story: I'm 58 years old. Eight years ago I turned 50. I was born in 1967 — grew up loving 70s and 80s rock bands. My favorite is Styx.

On my 50th birthday, she got a band to play. I happen to play guitar and can play several Styx songs. They called me up on stage: "It's your 50th — why don't we have John come up and play one of his favorite Styx songs?"

They opened up the guitar case — and my wife had gotten me an autographed guitar from every band member of Styx. Inside were tickets to the next show and backstage passes to meet the band.

It's the nicest thing anyone's ever done for me. Not only rare, but she had to figure it out and have the resources. And — she also got my younger brother, who's 5 years younger and plays piano, to learn "Come Sail Away." So my brother gets on stage and starts playing. I'm laughing: "Oh my God, my brother's a very busy guy — sorry you had to learn this."

The whole thing was an expression of love. It was an identity of me. A celebration of me. You can never take that away.

Patrick: Beautiful. Wonderful place to close. Thanks so much for your time.

Guest: Thank you. Great to be here.

[广告结尾：Ramp · Vanta · WorkOS · Ridgeline · Rogo]

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的洞察：**"信任"其实是"恐惧"的倒影，而不是"相信"的延伸**
作者说了一句被大多数人忽略的话：*相信 ≠ 信任*。你相信飞行员是合格的，却依然恐惧飞行。募资失败最常见的死法，不是别人不相信你的逻辑，而是他们**相信了却依然不敢**。这意味着——**做募资的人真正的对手不是"怀疑",而是"未被处理的恐惧"**。
由此推导出一个令人意外的结论：**"厌恶风险 vs 偏爱风险" 是一个伪命题**。作者一针见血——没有人真的"偏爱风险"，只有人**擅长把风险合理化到一半**，从而允许自己伸手去够那份奖励。赌场、创业、婚姻、加密货币——本质上都是同一台"合理化引擎"在运转。这也解释了为什么最聪明的投资人往往在事后复盘时说："当时我真的没觉得那么危险。"——他们不是勇敢，他们只是**神经系统更善于自我催眠**。
## 二、跨领域连接：**募资 = 政治竞选 + 心理治疗 + 神秘主义**
作者不经意间打通了三个看似无关的领域：
- **政治学的"hard reelect number"**（铁票基数）——你天然能拿到的钱决定了你的天花板：**首轮融资几乎等于最终

item.title: [Sequoia Capital] Anthropic's Katelyn Lesse & Angela Jiang: Building an Ecosystem, not a Walled Garden
item.dc:creator: Sequoia Capital
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽视的哲学翻转：从"电力"隐喻到"代币不可互换"

Angela 用电力做类比，但真正锋利的洞察藏在她随口说的另一句话里——"tokens aren't fungible"（代币不是可互换的）。这句话是对她自己电力隐喻的隐秘反叛：电力最伟大的特质恰恰是可互换性（一度电就是一度电，无论用来烧咖啡还是启动手术台）。但 Anthropic 的下一层抽象——"策略层"——建立在完全相反的假设上：这个 token 用来"建议"，那个 token 用来"执行"，另一个 token 用来"做梦"。

这里出现了一个跨领域的惊人对应：这不像电网，倒像亚当·斯密的别针工厂——同一种劳动力，一旦被分工赋予不同角色，产出会指数级放大。更精妙的映射来自计算机科学的 Actor Model（Carl Hewitt, 1973）：分布式系统性能提升的关键从来不在"更多算力"，而在"更好的消息传递协议"。Anthropic 正在把这个五十年前就存在的洞察，重新走一遍，只是这次演员是 token。

二、冰川反转定律：AI 时代的 UI 会变得越来越"无聊"

Claude tag 引发的"这不就是个 Slack bot 吗"的困惑，是本次访谈中最反直觉的信号。传统软件的进化规律是：能力越强，界面越丰富（想想 Photoshop 三十年图标史）。而 Angela 揭示的是一个隐藏的反向规律——能力越强，可见界面越倾向于消失，复杂度全部沉到水面之下。

这里存在一个惊人的跨领域连接：这不是软件设计问题，这是具身认知（Embodied Cognition）问题。当一个"同事"真正胜任时，你不会为它设计一个仪表盘，你只会在 Slack 里 @ 它。最先进的 agent 界面，会退化成人类之间早已存在的界面——Slack、WhatsApp、邮件、短信。这意味着一个残酷的商业推论：任何试图为 AI 发明"新界面范式"的创业公司，都在逆着这条冰川流动。真正的赢家会是那些让 AI 悄悄住进旧界面里的人。

三、被访谈埋没的"第三杠杆"：为什么 Best-of-N 是当前最大的套利空间

访谈末尾埋了一颗被匆匆掠过的钻石。Angela 提到大家找 agent 性能时只会想两个杠杆——换更大的模型或让它跑更久——但真正被低估的第三杠杆是 Best-of-N（并行多路，选最优）。她说"论文一大堆但真做进生产极其难"。

这里有个物理学的漂亮对照：这正是蒙特卡洛方法在 AI 层面的重演。物理学家在 1940 年代发现，与其精确求解一个复杂积分，不如扔一万次骰子取平均。同样的思想在半个世纪后从洛斯阿拉莫斯漂到旧金山，只是骰子换成了 token。

但更深的洞察是："代币理性化"时代（token rationalization）的赢家不会是省 token 的人，而是学会"结构性挥霍"的人——知道什么时候该并行烧十份钱换一份最优答案。这与金融领域的凯利公式（Kelly Criterion）如出一辙——最优策略不是最小化风险，而是在正期望下的最大化下注。企业现在开始设"AI 预算封顶"，其实是在系统性地放弃这条 alpha 曲线。真正聪明的做法是 Caitlyn 暗示的："给它一个结果目标 + 一个预算，剩下的别管。"——这是把凯利公式外包给了模型本身。

四、隐藏的架构判断：为什么"删掉一部分脚手架"是本轮最反常识的建议

Angela 说的"随着模型变强，你应该主动删掉部分 harness"——这在正常软件工程里是异端。软件史是抽象层的堆叠史，从没有人建议你"删抽象层"。但在 AI 领域，脚手架是负债，不是资产：模型能力提升会"吃掉"你精心搭建的引导逻辑，留着反而妨碍它。

这让我想起生物学的"退化演化"（reductive evolution）——寄生虫失去消化系统，因为宿主提供了。你的 harness 正在被模型"寄生"，接受这个事实的团队会跑得比死守自建框架的团队快一个数量级。

📝 转录文本

开场：Anthropic Platform 是什么

Sequoia: Caitlyn and Angela, thank you so much for joining us today. Lauren and I are thrilled to have you here. You are responsible for building Anthropic's platform and so you are responsible for building what I think is one of the most important, if not the most important, developer platform in the world, and we are really excited to interview you today to understand more about what's ahead. So maybe just to get started, can you give us the context of what is Anthropic platform and where do you sit within Anthropic?

Caitlyn: Yeah. So platform is both our externally facing APIs — our developer platform that people build on top of when they want to build applications and systems that access Claude's intelligence — as well as internally, we run our product infrastructure and basically we're the layer that our apps build on top of internally as well.

两个北极星：内部速度 vs 外部广度

Sequoia: Awesome. What's your north star as a team?

Angela: It's a great question. We actually — because we have both internal and external — we actually kind of have like two north stars. Which is probably like, you know, you'd be like, "why? There should only be one north star." But…

Sequoia: Different planetary system.

Angela: Yes, exactly, they're separate solar systems, so it's fine.

On the internal side, what we really want to provide is literally as much leverage as possible for our internal teams to be able to ship AGI-pilled products. We want them to be able to move fast, be able to have a reliable, great platform to be able to build on top of. But that key bit about speed is really intentional for us, and we really really care about that internally.

Externally, we actually have a lot more complicated set of things. But one of the true norths that we have there is to be able to basically give any builder the tools to be able to work with Claude to build whatever they want to build. And so it's a bit of a broad statement, but as a result that boils itself down into being wherever that business is. We really care about bringing our platform really really close to that business. This is why we spend a lot of time with the hyperscalers integrating really closely — directly with them like AWS, Google, so on and so forth.

It is a lot of primitives that we end up creating. We want people to be able to express what they think their product should be. We want them to be able to almost do custom software in their own way. In this new world with AI, what used to be probably economically impossible — that last mile of custom software — now in theory should be very very achievable. We want to give them all the tools and all the capabilities to go and do that.

And so sometimes that comes in the form of primitives and APIs and higher-order abstractions. And sometimes that comes in the form of just standards. So for example, like skills and MCP — those are things that just like Claude needs them to be useful, and we can just give them out to the rest of the ecosystem, work with everyone to help you create those things and get the best out of Claude.

So externally, we really are oriented around just helping you just be able to build, but internally that orientation, while still existing, is probably more specified towards speed and being able to move really quickly.

内外一致的哲学：同一套 primitives

Sequoia: How do you decide what goes into the platform, what gets externalized, and what doesn't — to decide what products should be available?

Angela: Yeah, I mean we generally try to have a philosophy that we try to be consistent across the board. It's actually one of the reasons why we do internal and external. There's plenty of other platform businesses and constructs where you actually bifurcate these two things. For us, we kind of try to intentionally keep it equal. And then as a result, we try to hold this philosophy as much as we can — for any builder, internal or external, even though our internal builders might have some slightly different requirements (in the same way any user would have slightly different requirements). We want to have the same primitives that are available to everyone.

And one of the overarching theses for that is that we've just seen the capabilities of these models just grow at such exponential rates, and it's really hard to figure out a long-lasting form factor. I think two years ago we were all like "everything's chat," and now everyone's like "forget chat, just agents," and there's going to be another form factor, another form factor. We kind of imagine that constantly evolving. So the best way for us to enable that for everyone and also ourselves is to actually build a really robust platform that gives people those kinds of tools to figure out what those form factors are.

And I don't think we by any means feel like we're the only ones capable of figuring out that form factor — like not at all. In fact, the more democratization we can do on that and help people and allow people to experiment, I think the more those form factors will actually naturally come out of the market.

Caitlyn: Yeah. And I think within our team, we've had moments where we're experimenting even with just a packaging up of our primitives in a different sort of higher-order way. And we've thought about, okay cool, we've solved this exact type of problem with this product that we've built into the world. And so we can go and dogfood it for ourselves. But we'd never want to fall into this trap of "we're overindexed on the problem as it needs to be solved for an internal user." Because, exactly what Angela said, internal users have very specific requirements, external users have very specific requirements, and so if you overindex on one or the other, you fall into a trap. So a lot of the time what we'll do is dogfood something internally at the same time that we open up early access of some sort with external customers, so that we can get a range of feedback and bring those things back into the platform.

抽象的三层蛋糕：知识 → 执行 → 协调

Sequoia: I'd love to talk about the higher levels of abstraction that you discussed. So I guess at the base level this is just, you know, raw access to Claude Opus or whatever tokens. How do you think about the layer cake of abstractions above that?

Caitlyn: Yeah. If you look back — so when I joined Anthropic around a year ago, the platform was basically just the messages API. It was a messages API. We had come out with standards like MCP, we obviously have developer tooling around our SDKs and our docs and our console and things like this. But for the most part it was a stateless API.

And what's interesting to Angela's point on form factors evolving over time is: we found a lot of our customers solving the same problems over and over again that we also were solving over and over again — around, as the models got better at running for longer and working with more context at a given time, you want to build agents that can succeed in a kind of long-running context, and even a remote context that doesn't necessarily have a human in the loop.

And so we found that we could piece together our primitives and stand up all the same infrastructure that we're finding ourselves standing up internally to power our own products, and arrive at some higher-order abstractions that let you do more agentic work out of the box.

The problems that we're solving for you are: infrastructure being kind of a hard thing to deal with — like how do you figure out spawning sandboxes that are going to have the right governance and security, and spin them up and spin them down when you need to; or the storage around transcript sessions so that you can resume a session if you stop it and pick it back up later. So that infrastructure is a big thing that we wanted to be able to provide more of out of the box, and we do more of that today.

And then the second thing being harnesses and harness engineering. There's a lot of thought and energy going into: how do I do my prompt caching, and how do I manage my context window, as well as how do I actually just get more intelligence out of the model, and how do I manage my costs and things like that.

So we've kind of packaged up our primitives a bit more in tune with the problems that we found ourselves solving, to provide more of these things out of the box for people so that they can — if they're building systems for themselves internally, if they're building products — they can just be more focused on the problems that they want to be solving, and if they want to offload some aspects of those problems to us, they can. And that's kind of the ethos.

谁用 primitives，谁用 managed agents

Sequoia: And are your customers generally choosing to opt from the grab bag of stuff that you offer, or are they — how often are they opting into just the managed agents offering, "I guess just take care of it all for me"?

Angela: It varies by the user group. For, I would say, really AI-native startups — the ones who are tinkering and experimenting at a really low layer — they're just going to go for the primitives.

And then for everyone else — and these are kind of classic like more like enterprises, or areas where the purpose of the startup or the philosophy behind the startup isn't necessarily to optimize on some kind of hill-climbing piece; it's more like stringing together a bunch of workflows and providing unique user value to that user — for those people, it's just kind of not their core competency. It's not where they want to focus their time and resources, and they reach much more for these kind of higher-order packaged offerings.

三层结构详解：Knowledge、Execution、Coordination

Sequoia: What are some examples of the primitives you've released at different layers in the last few months? We've seen a few of them. Would love to hear.

Angela: Yeah. I think maybe one framing I would give for some of the constructs that Caitlyn was talking about is — and this is a bit of an oversimplification — but effectively there's approximately like three layers of this cake.

Knowledge layer

At the very bottom is just kind of like knowledge. And so at this layer, in many ways, it's knowledge about the model, it's knowledge about the things that the model needs, and it's just like the ability to know how to actually do something with Claude — maybe the way I'd phrase that.

And so there, the primitives that we have spent more and more time on have been actually things of the past — because we still evolve them, but they tend to be a little bit more baked. For example, there's very specific shapes and parameters we put on the messages API, and it's more like trying to expressly showcase Claude the model's actual design: the way it thinks, the way it respects certain parameters, the way it will do tool calls, all of those different pieces.

And then we started standardizing tools. And then we started standardizing bits and pieces of context that you could put in at different moments in time, which is concretely like skills and memory. So those are the kind of knowledge-layer type of abstractions that we've put out over the past year plus a bit.

Execution layer

The next layer of abstraction that we've actually started to spend more and more of our time on is like: once you kind of know stuff, you then need to execute. And so at the execution layer, that level of abstraction is the part that Caitlyn was talking about — around we're doing these higher-order pieces. But what are we putting higher-order there? It really is because you're now getting Claude to execute work. It's not just to know something, right? I can give it a question, give me an answer — you can string a lot of that stuff together. But now if you need to execute — like do work, give me the output, edit files in a bunch of different systems — that becomes a lot more complicated and requires infrastructure to handle.

And so that layer is basically, I would say, a low-level harness plus managed infrastructure as the set of abstractions. Today, our high-level product for that is called Claude Managed Agents. And so that's like a piece, but we started to wrap more and more pieces in that.

Coordination layer

I think there's going to be a layer on top of that. We have some inklings of it, we started to build towards, but the last layer of abstraction on top of this is probably the coordination layer. So you have knowledge, and you have execution, you have coordination.

And at the coordination layer, we've started to expose some of these in ways that aren't very obvious, but we're beginning to think of these things called like strategies, where basically it's almost like a meta-harness, right? The true low-level harness is designed for execution. But the next one is about: okay, if tokens aren't really fungible and you need to give them different jobs — like maybe this token is advising versus this token is executing, this token is dreaming versus this token's executing, so on and so forth — you want to start composing these kind of orchestrated strategies that go together. And they should sit on top of all these things, because at the end of the day you still need to execute, and the execution still needs to know what to do. So everything, in theory, should kind of ladder together.

And so I think you know if you were to look at our roadmap and maybe project forward a little bit where you kind of expect us to go, we'll move more and more from the knowledge layer to the execution layer, and from the execution layer to the coordination layer, in terms of the abstractions that you can see us put out.

生态视角：不是围墙花园

Sequoia: That's a really cool. How do you think this all comes together into a broader ecosystem beyond just the things that you guys are building? How do you help support people building products on top of it, and how do you help them get the most out of all these pieces?

Angela: Yeah, I think this is like super top of mind for us. We really want to find a way to support as many people in doing this as we can. I think we're still learning — a lot of the industry has evolved. We've seen a lot of different pieces get spun up and spun down.

And I think the operative part for Caitlyn and I has been in the category of making sure at least at the base layer that we provide as many primitives across the board as possible.
