BRDC：850 位成员的私人俱乐部

So, where to start? I think perhaps —

Do you want to really start? I think the first thing, just based on how we're going to develop this, is to really look at who actually is responsible for putting on Silverstone itself.

Indeed. That's right. So Silverstone is owned and operated by something called the British Racing Drivers Club, the BRDC. Were you aware of this body until I brought it up with you?

I was.

You were? Yes. Okay. But only — I was only relatively recently — because I've been spammed by Jake Humphrey's social media accounts as he has just recently been made a member of the BRDC, which he was absolutely ecstatic about because it's very meaningful within petrolhead world.

There are 850 members. And to become a member you have to have achieved something of excellence within motorsport. This isn't something which is: "Okay, well, I'm rich, I can just pay to get in." This is an invitation thing. You're proposed and seconded and accepted. But your proposal and second have to explain: "This is why this person has achieved something significant in motorsport." And you have to be either British or from the Commonwealth. Right, now they made a little exception to let Eddie Jordan in — the late Eddie Jordan — where they said, "Okay, well, if you were born in Ireland before it officially became fully separated, if you're in the Irish Free State or something like that." Basically it was, "We want Eddie in and we'll create a sort of exemption there for him because we like him."

从 1928 到 Silverstone

But it's 850 members. So, it was founded in 1928. So, we're coming up to the centenary of the BRDC. And it was founded by a man called Dr. Dudley Benjafield. And he was one of what was called the Bentley Boys, and these were the early motor racing drivers. Back when just cars themselves were a relatively recent innovation, they were racing some of the early faster types of cars, including obviously Bentleys, hence why they're known as the Bentley Boys.

And the BRDC was based at Brooklands, which was a track in Surrey. And it was an up-and-coming sport. And during the Second World War, actually a lot of BRDC members died — a lot of them involved in flying. So you have Battle of Britain people who died etc. So it was still a relatively young organization as we come out the back of the Second World War, and this is when we start the move towards Silverstone.

So they took over the lease of Silverstone in 1952, the BRDC did, as a motor racing track. And then in 1966 — right, so this is within some people's, not mine before you mention it, you cheeky sod. Do you know my age? — this is within living memory for some people who might be listening to this show. So the BRDC opened a subsidiary which is called Silverstone Circuit Limited, which was specifically for the development of the British Grand Prix.

Bernie Ecclestone 时代：Hosting Fee 的诞生

Right. So that's where we're going back to. So over the following 20 years the British Grand Prix develops and grows as do other Grands Prix around the world, but in slightly chaotic fashion. The whole thing wasn't sort of properly put together until of course famously Bernie Ecclestone, who had been both a driver himself and then a team boss, came along and said to the other team owners, "We need to organize this properly. We need to sell the rights properly. We need to do this whole thing in a much more structured way."

And of course by this point the British Grand Prix is one of the established Grands Prix — one of the legacy, historic Grands Prix which Bernie's new F1 circuit — effectively circus — inherits. Silverstone as already being a major event. This is the early — late 1970s, early 1980s. So it's already a major sporting event.

And Bernie's business model then starts to develop during the 1980s. And one of the ways in which Bernie starts to make money — where previously they would say, "Oh, well, the British Racing Drivers Club is having a Grand Prix and that's great. Let's all go and race there and happy days" — Bernie then starts saying to circuits, "If you want your Grand Prix to be in my circus — if I'm going to bring all of these teams to race at your circuit — you need to pay me money. You need to pay Formula 1 management money." It's what's called a hosting fee, right?

So that starts off quite small, but as Bernie then starts to access Grands Prix in different parts of the world where they're very happy to pay very large sums of money — particularly the push into the Middle East that started to happen 20, 25 years ago — some of the historic circuits start to drop out because they can't afford to pay these hosting fees. Okay?

2015：Silverstone 濒临破产

And eventually the hosting fees started to get so big that Silverstone started to struggle to pay them as well. So this is around about 10 to 15 years ago — they've gone from 5 million to 10 million to 15 million a year. Right? And by this point, what Bernie's done within Formula 1 is he's basically said, "Right, I keep pretty much all the commercial revenues for Formula 1 management. Obviously we've taken the TV rights revenues." So as a host of the race, you don't get any TV rights revenue. You don't get any commercial revenue. The one thing you do get is ticketing. Congratulations.

Right. So, you're starting to be confronted with quite a stark thing, which is: "We pay Formula 1 management £15 million a year. We then need to get £15 million worth of ticketing just to cover our costs."

So, the chairman John Grant — I was just reading this. He very effortlessly put it: "The costs are more than the revenues."

Yes.

Great. I mean, that's the kind of —

So what happened was by 2015 they're on the verge of bankruptcy. They were being bankrupted by Bernie Ecclestone. And Bernie Ecclestone's view was, "Well, guys, if you don't want to have a Grand Prix, don't have a Grand Prix." Right?

Really interesting.

Yeah. He used to — terrible circuit anyway. Terrible facilities. He was always slagging it off. He was always slagging off the BRDC, right? I think was there an element of sort of chip on shoulder about the whole thing? That there was this rather sort of grand club which included lots of dukes and sirs, and there was Bernie the Southeast London car salesman who wasn't really part of that whole set. Was there a bit of that going on? Paying off old scores from what he saw as — maybe he felt slighted in the 1970s by not being invited to be a member back then?

All of maybe there was a bit of all that going on. But the bottom line is is that by 2015, which is just before Liberty took over, they're tied into this contract with Bernie by which basically the amount they have to pay to host the race increases like the equivalent triple lock — ahead of inflation every single year.

Well, it was on 5% escalators, weren't they?

Exactly. So each year it went up 5%.

Yeah. And this is during a period when inflation was very very low. But it's also interesting here because it's during a period where Formula 1 itself is struggling more than it has done in a very long time.

And that was important on both sides. Right? So on Bernie's side, the fact that Formula 1 was struggling in general meant that the hosting fees had become an absolutely critical part of the entire financial ecosystem. Extracting money from tracks was becoming being the main revenue line, right? Because they were going to tracks in the Middle East. Bernie was going, "Right, do you want to have a Grand Prix?" "Well, it's 40 million, right? Do you want to have a Grand Prix? It's 45 million." And then to Silverstone saying, "Well, look, if you're not even willing to pay me 15, 20 million, then I'll just go and take another one somewhere else. I'm not particularly interested in you."

Which of course was disastrous for the sport — and you lost some historic Grands Prix like the one in South Africa and the one in Argentina during this 20, 25 year period — because Bernie knew that they wouldn't pay the hosting fees and he'd rather take it somewhere else. But of course those were the places where you get massive crowds in big countries like South Africa and Argentina. Whereas some of the places where Bernie then took Formula 1 to was a little bit more marginal.

Break Clause 与 Liberty Media 的接手

So John Grant, in effect, he activated the break clause, didn't he, for the BRDC.

Correct. So he basically said —

Exactly right. "We're going to — fine, we're out."

We're out. So just before Bernie sold — or CVC and Bernie sold — to Liberty Media, which is 2017, 2018 — just before then, Silverstone, the British Racing Drivers Club, had activated a break clause in the contract and they basically said, "We can't afford this." Right? So we were looking at the end of the British Grand Prix at that point. And that was much discussed in the media at the time.

And then Liberty come along and Liberty take a totally different view of the industry and the sport and say, "No, the British Grand Prix is a crucial bit of IP which lends into the value of the entire thing. Having a race where there are 200,000 people there and everyone's going absolutely mad — this is like the core of fandom. And we're trying to build fandom." So they then went and cut a better deal with the BRDC, which meant the BRDC could then see, "Right, we are now going to carry on hosting this Grand Prix for a significant period of time, but we have much greater certainty over it — potentially affordable."

Stuart Pringle 与 20 年稳定治理

But at the same time, the BRDC just said, "Okay, well, we also do need to get our own house in order. We need to become a more diversified business so that we can be a proper business rather than a club that holds a Grand Prix. We actually need to be a motor racing business as a whole."

And there's been a guy there, sort of unsung hero of sports business, called Stuart Pringle, who's been the CEO — or I think originally was the secretary of the British Racing Drivers Club, but since then the title's been changed to CEO — since 2006. So we're talking about a 20-year regime stability there. The first 10 of which was extremely difficult when they were having to spend money on repairing facilities and when the hosting fees were going up and up and when Formula 1 as a whole was going down and down, leading to the verge of bankruptcy in 2015.

10 年后：1.1 亿英镑营收，5 亿英镑估值

Lo and behold, 10 years later, through both the efforts of Silverstone themselves but also of course the efforts of Liberty Media in terms of the way in which they run Formula 1, Silverstone now has an annual turnover of 110 million pounds — 50% of which is from the British Grand Prix. So you can work it out. Okay, it's about 50 to 60 million they get from the British Grand Prix and 50% from the rest of the year from the stuff that they do during the rest of the year.

And during that period they have invested heavily in a heritage center, conference center, hotel, increased capacity, hospitality — all this type of stuff. And Liberty have got a much more progressive model in terms of working with tracks, where these days the tracks do get to keep past the hospitality, right? So it's not the old days of the paddock club where that was all kept in-house. These days tracks are incentivized to drive hospitality and to actually sort of become part of the whole mix.

The bottom line is they're now making pre-tax profit of 14 million quid and they've got cash reserves of almost 50 million quid. I mean, it's the most astonishing sports business turnaround.

10 years. In less than 10 years you've gone from basically going, "We can't afford this," to now — just this week gone by, hence why we're talking about it — the biggest event in Formula 1 history.

Yes. That is actually most importantly financially valuable, profitable and massively valuable. So if you think — let's just have a think about this. Right? Just as a business, as a sports industry business, annual turnover of 110 million quid with a profit margin of getting on for 10% — no, more than 10%, getting on for 15%. Right? So you're looking at probably a business which the BRDC, if it were a private company and was now to sell itself — Christ, I don't know, what would it be worth? You're probably talking about certainly the thick end of 500 million.

Right? So you've gone from something on the verge of bankruptcy to something that's worth 500-odd million in the space of 10 years. So it's been a great —

BRDC 的会员构成：一个反 DEI 的组织

Just to give people quickly a bit of a flavor of how idiosyncratic this organization is: so the president is David Coulthard, the former Formula 1 driver. And the vice presidents are Martin Brundle, former Formula 1 driver, media pundit; Ron Dennis, the former McLaren boss; Damon Hill, the former Formula 1 world champion; Nigel Mansell, the former Formula 1 world champion; Jackie Stewart; Derek Warwick, the former Formula 1... So basically —

Their diversity is very strong.

So basically —

The Lancaster cricket board test — they're not passing that.

They're not. But they're actually doing a great job.

They're actually doing a great job. They might not be ticking too many DEI boxes here, but the BRDC are doing a great job.

And for all that, sometimes on this show we do pick apart things that aren't working. I think sometimes we have to take a step back and say that it wasn't Silverstone's fault that this race was a mess. That was Formula 1's problem. In terms of the event as a whole, obviously stonking weather as well which helped, this was an absolutely epic sports business event.

与其他 GP 主办模式的对比

Yeah. And most interestingly as I was going through it as well, it was looking at this compared to the other hosting organizations around. And this is — so this is the only private members club hosting. I think actually I'm going to say now because I think the Dutch Grand Prix was also similar, but they're losing their race. So it will now be the only independent. The rest of the models generally are funded by state. So then thereabouts, Abu Dhabi, Bahrain — actually Australia part as well for the Melbourne Grand Prix. And really for those fees — I'm not quite sure how it works in Miami.

So I do. Yeah.

So but the fees attached on the state naturally — Azerbaijan reportedly are paying $57 million a year. Qatar and Saudi $55 million — 10-year agreements locked in with Qatar. So that's 550 million. Abu Dhabi 42, Australia, Singapore 35–40. You then have kind of a mixture of promoter and state, which is the Monaco and Monza type models, and they pay about 15 to 20 million.

And interestingly they both recently doubled in the new extensions. So prices going up.

Then you have Silverstone, a private members club. Then you have Miami and Vegas — F1 itself pays the hosting fee. And it's not hard to understand why the value for Formula 1 —

Breaking the US market.

Breaking the US market and having Vegas. So those are generally the different ways in which Formula 1 makes its hosting fees. And it totals — you mentioned it was a big part. I mean, it's $700 million a year in hosting fees.

In hosting fees for everyone. That's the amount of money that the holding company of Formula 1 rakes in from places paying them for the privilege of having a Grand Prix.

It's a huge business. It's about 20 to 25% of Formula 1's revenue.

Which is extraordinarily high.

It is. Yeah. It's a key part of the pie.

巴林大奖赛的战略意义

And you can sort of understand it. Like — I advised the Bahrain Grand Prix for many many years. Christ, 8, 9 years. And I would often ask, "Is this worth it?" Right? Absolutely it's worth it. Right? This is one week of the year when the attention of a good chunk of the world is on our little island kingdom. We struggle for attention. We struggle to drive a tourism ministry even though we've got beautiful places for people to come to. How do we drive attention on that? Other countries might spend vast amounts of money on marketing tourism, but we find that by hosting a Grand Prix — with all of the imagery that goes around that and the eyeballs that go onto that imagery — is actually what helps drive our entire economy.

It also means that they can then have a motorsports economy in Bahrain because they've got a Formula 1 track there. So you can test cars, test products. It enables them to be a center for motorsport in the Middle East. So as an investment over a 20-year period, it's been extremely successful. So, this has actually been for most of these deals, it's been a happy thing for the BRDC.

It was a little bit different — they were there first and they were a club. Not — they're not trying to pay dividends and make big profits and all this type of stuff. But they've effectively been forced into the modern age, into the 21st century, by the economics of hosting a Grand Prix. And they've done it.

The F1 also have the flexibility to make the calls as and where they want to on which countries and which host cities will pay more for. So that's a balance of heritage versus — under Liberty they've been much more cognizant of that, where under Bernie it was getting very mercantile. It was whoever pays the most.

It's the luxury of success. When you're successful you can work out how to make those numbers work for you. When you're struggling for cash, making decisions — Liberty did this right from the start, didn't they? So they did it before they made a big success out of F1. They made decisions like, "We need to keep the British Grand Prix." So it's a bit of chicken and egg involved here. But I think you have to give Liberty a lot of credit for saying, "We're not losing the British Grand Prix."

Brilliant. Right. Brilliant. Fantastic.

结尾预告：Elliot Anderson 转会分析

Good stuff, Charlie. Now, if you want to stay with us, we're going to go and do a very nice segment on the absolutely crazy transfer of Elliot Anderson to Manchester City from Nottingham Forest. Because there's a little bit more than meets the eye to some of this, as there is with the broader footballing ecosystem when you start getting into the details of some of these transfers. Come and join us on Substack if you want to keep going.

But otherwise, thank you. I really enjoyed that this week. It was good fun.

Yeah, thanks Chaz.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 三个隐藏的系统性真相
### 1. 挪威模式的反直觉：**"延迟专业化"是一种高级形式的风险管理**
表面上看，挪威把足球职业化推迟到 15-16 岁是"人文关怀"，但这其实是一种**期权定价思维**在体育人才培养中的应用。
传统精英俱乐部体系（英格兰、苏格兰、南美）本质上是**在 9 岁做投资决策**——他们必须在信息极度不完整时"押注"哪个孩子会成才。这就像风险投资在 Pre-Seed 阶段就要 All-in，注定错误率极高。而挪威系统实际上是把决策点推迟到"信号-噪声比"最高的时刻（青春期发育之后），这时候**每一份投资的边际回报率呈指数级上升**。
哈兰德是最好的证据——他早期身材瘦小，在任何 9 岁筛选制的国家都会被淘汰。挪威系统"保留了他的期权"，直到他自己行使了它。这跟金融里的"美式期权 vs 欧式期权"是同一逻辑：**保留决策权到最后一刻，永远比提前锁定更有价值**。
### 2. 一个跨领域的意外连接：**特朗普事件与 Silverstone 的 BRDC 揭示了同一个"治理悖论"**
这两个故事看似毫不相关，但在系统层面上讲的是同一件事——**"程

item.title: [All-In Podcast] Open Source Wins, AGI Is Here, and Scorsese’s AI Toolkit with CEOs of Cerebras & Black Forest Labs
item.dc:creator: All-In Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反常识的观察：这场"AI 大建设"不是在追未来，而是在追"昨天的欠账"

Andrew Feldman 说了一句极具颠覆性的话："They're trying to capture yesterday's demand."

我们习惯把基础设施投资理解为"预测未来 → 提前布局"，但 AI 数据中心的现状恰好相反——订单在芯片出厂之前就已经排队，250 亿美元的 backlog 不是赌明天，而是在偿还昨天已经发生但没被满足的需求。这与历史上所有"泡沫"的心理结构截然不同：泡沫是"如果我建，他们会来"；而现在是"他们已经在门口敲门，我建不出来"。

这里藏着一个更深的系统性真相：人类第一次遇到了一种"消费速度快于生产速度"的通用生产力商品。电力、钢铁、石油都曾经历过短缺，但从未有一种商品的边际效用像 token 这样——用得越多，用户变得越聪明，从而产生更多需要 token 的问题。这是一个自我加速的需求飞轮，不是需求预测问题，而是需求本身在递归自我制造。

二、跨领域连接：Costco 悖论 × 精子鞭毛 × 石匠世家——同一条学习曲线

Feldman 用 Costco 类比 token 消费的成熟过程（从"每条通道都走一遍"到"直奔烤鸡"），这看似只是消费心理学。但把它和后半段 Robin Rombach 谈的"石匠家族传承"、以及 Andrew 提到的"果蝇代际学习"放在一起看，会发现一个惊人的同构：

  • Costco 用户：初期无差别遍历 → 后期策略性收敛
  • 石匠世家：三代人在同一座建筑上叠加认知
  • 果蝇遗传学：一天两代，让千代压缩成人类可观察的时间
  • AI 递归推理：在 24 小时内完成人类几周的思考

这四者的共同底层结构是："代际压缩"作为智能进化的通用引擎。人类文明过去两千年最大的瓶颈从来不是缺乏聪明人，而是范式随人死亡——Jason 引用心理学教授那句"paradigms don't die, people do"是本次对话最锋利的一刀。Freud、Skinner、Jung 的理论都要等他们的学生老去才能被替换。

而现在，AI 把"代际"压缩到了小时级。这意味着范式更迭的速度不再受制于人类寿命。这才是"超级智能"最不起眼但最颠覆性的定义——不是它更聪明，而是它可以在一天内经历十代认知革命。

三、隐藏系统性真相："意图理解"的出现，正在悄悄摧毁一个百年产业——中层管理

对话中一个几乎被忽略的细节：Jason 说 AI 现在会反过来问他"你有需求文档吗？你的目标是什么？"

如果你把这句话放到组织行为学里看，会发现这正是中层管理者百年来存在的唯一理由——把高层的模糊意图翻译成下层可执行的规格。彼得·德鲁克整个管理学体系的基石就是"目标管理 (MBO)"，本质是人肉的意图澄清器。

Reasoning 模型的出现意味着：意图澄清这个功能，第一次可以由非人来完成，且比人更耐心、更结构化。这不是"AI 取代白领"这种粗糙叙事，而是更精准的：AI 正在从组织架构中抽出"意图翻译层"。

跨领域连接：这和 Robin 讲的"图像是比语言更高带宽的沟通媒介"完美咬合。Scorsese 用 AI 把脑中画面"外化"，本质上和 CEO 用 AI 把战略"外化"是同一件事——都是在绕过语言这个 lossy compression 层，让意图直接落地为可执行的产物（图像/代码/决策）。

四、一个让你意外的推论：开源不是"民主化 AI"，而是"AI 主权化"

大多数人把开源解读为"让更多人用得起 AI"，但这次对话透露了完全不同的信号：Feldman 提到 UAE 的 G42、GSK 定制模型、欧洲的主权担忧——开源真正在做的事情，不是普及，而是让国家/大企业能够拒绝依附。

这有个非常反直觉的意涵：开源的最大受益者不是散户开发者，而是"不想依赖美国 AI 巨头"的主权实体。中国的 GLM、Kimi、Qwen 之所以能快速崛起，本质上是地缘政治为开源模型提供了刚需。

再往深挖：如果 AGI 已经到来（Feldman 和 Jason 都同意这一点），那么"谁掌握模型 = 谁掌握认知基础设施"。这就把开源从软件哲学问题，升级成了国防问题。这也是为什么 Anthropic 部署前政府要"红队测试"——它已经不是软件发布，而是认知级武器的军控。

AHA 时刻的浓缩：这场对话表面在谈芯片、GPU、生成模型，深层却在讲同一件事——人类文明第一次遇到"代际压缩速度超过生物代际的智能载体"。所有的建设狂热、开源博弈、主权焦虑、意图理解，都是这个底层现实的不同投影。

📝 转录文本

开场：超级智能竞赛与 Cerebras 的回归

We are in the race for super intelligence, and uh Andrew Feldman is back — uh and obviously CEO and founder of Cerebras, doing inference chips, pioneered the space, had a successful IPO.

We've talked about this a couple of times. We got to see each other in January at Davos. IPO happens. Uh, the boys and I got to sit with you recently.

That was fun.

At liquidity.

That was really, that was really fun. Had a great discussion with the boys.

I wanted to deep dive with you about a couple of topics.

第一话题：史无前例的 AI 基础设施大建设

The first one is the buildout of AI. We've never seen a buildout like this since, you know, the Great Wall of China —

right? Who knows?

The pyramids. I mean, it feels like the amount of capital, time, and intelligent people on the planet dedicating themselves to the buildout of something. Um, I I can't think of anything in our lifetimes — with perhaps uh, you know, before our lifetimes — the war effort.

Right.

This is a mobilization on a scale that we read about, we hear about, but you're actually doing it. You have customers who are building data centers and you're a key piece of that.

（广告插播：AppLovin）

All app started with an $8 domain and no VC funding and became one of the largest ad platforms in the world. Now that same engine powers Appleven ads for e-commerce. Your ads run inside mobile games, reaching over a billion people with full screen, distraction-free attention. The platform finds buyers and optimizes for profit. You set the target, it does the rest. One cookware brand went from 4 million to $16 million, turned profitable, and is on pace for 80 million this year. Visit apploven.com/allin to launch your first campaign today.

2026 年的 Cerebras 与得州数据中心

Maybe you could just enlighten us in 2026. What is Cerebras doing and what is happening with this buildout out in Texas? These are some gigantic, gigantic efforts. The size and scope of what is being built — the physical size and scope. Usually when we talk about software or we talk about hardware, we're talking about chips or boxes, and they don't have the same sort of physical enormity, right?

Right. And what we're talking about now are data centers that are, in the next several years, going to use more power than the previous 50 years on Earth took.

Wow.

Right. We're talking about individual buildings the size of football fields that have more power coming into them than mid-size cities. And they're being built across the US. They're being built in Canada. They're being built throughout the Nordics. They're being built here in Paris and throughout France, in Europe, in the Middle East, in nations that sort of weren't front and center in anybody's mind previously. You know, Kazakhstan, Tajikistan are building out. Georgia building out data centers of size. Armenia. Everybody's sort of focused.

Every country —

um and every state obviously in America feels they need to participate in this.

客户与需求：追赶"昨天的欠账"

And the people who are buying the capacity — the OpenAIs, Anthropics, SpaceX, xAI, uh the Googles — they are insatiable right now.

Yeah.

And they're building how many years out when you talk to them? They were ordering chips from Cerebras before you were finished with the chips. They're putting orders in ahead of time.

The irony is, unlike many sort of exciting times in technology, they're trying to capture yesterday's demand, right? The demand is way outstripping our ability to build data centers and to fill them with hardware, all right? And so, you know, we have a $25 billion backlog —

$25 billion backlog.

and we are not alone in that. That OpenAI, uh Anthropic, you go through this list of Google wants more data centers, Microsoft wants more data centers, AWS wants more data centers, right? All of these players are not chasing sort of "if you build it, they will come." They're chasing — the demand is booked.

Right. How do we keep them from leaving?

Right. And that's extremely unusual.

Token Maxing：价值真实存在还是实验过剩？

It's very unusual. And now we have people who are, you know, we have a term for a "token maxing."

Yeah.

And there's a great debate: is this actually creating value? I'm curious where you stand. You know, is it even possible that this much demand could be created if value did not exist? There is clearly massive value happening.

Yeah.

But there's also massive experimentation.

Oh, for sure. You know, I liken this to when we first started with uh AWS and it was so good to get around your own IT organization,

right?

that you told every engineer, "Yeah, go ahead, put it on your credit card, kite and sign up."

Yeah.

And a lot of it was really useful, and some of it was like, "God, I wish we didn't do that."

Yeah.

And so for sure there's experimentation, but it doesn't mean that the net value isn't enormous. It means some of it is going to go nowhere.

Costco 类比：从遍历到策略性消费

And you know, it was the same. I remember when Costco opened up in in the Palo Alto area in 1988. And people used to shop Costco like they shop Safeway. They go down every aisle.

Yes.

And that's a horrible way to shop Costco because you end up with four things you didn't need and each was $22.

Right. And as people got more sort of accustomed to it, you go to the back, you get the chicken —

18 cupcakes for the kids' birthday party, bang, you are strategic. And it's exactly the same.

I think at first people opened up and said, "Everybody, as much tokens as you want." And in enterprises, there's no open loop. We don't give sort of any resource unconstrained to people. And now we're jumping on saying, "Whoa, all right. These guys should have as much as they need. They're enormously productive over here. We can use maybe an open-source model, maybe a cheaper model over here." And now we're sort of running like a business and we're really seeing a certain type of person emerge who knows how to deploy this technology.

系统性思维与"意图理解"的跃迁

Systems thinking.

Yeah,

which developers kind of have innately. CEOs tend to be great strategists and understand systems. Uh, but the intelligence is getting so much better every step along the way that I'm watching individuals — typically startup founders, but also venture capitalists and associates who work at my venture firm. They start playing with the tool, and then the tool starts playing with them. They start to go, "Oh, I haven't clearly defined what my goal is. I don't understand what a system is. I've never heard about making a requirements document." And the software's like, "Do you have a requirements document? What's your goal?" The AI starts telling people, "You're token maxing, and you need to get a little more focused here."

One of my colleagues 20 years ago, a really smart computer scientist, said, "Computers really dumb. They do exactly what you tell them."

Yeah.

And at first, prompting was like that, right? You modified your prompt a little bit and it changed the answer

dramatically.

Dramatically. And increasingly it's understanding what your intent was.

Right, right. And if you have a chance to play with Fable or 5.6 from OpenAI, increasingly you don't have to get the prompt just right. You don't have to be a prompt whisperer. Instead, you ask it and it says, "Well, here are some things, and by the way, maybe you wanted the chart to go two ways. You wanted a line and a bar." And it's like, "Well, that's exactly what I wanted. I didn't ask for it, but that is better."

And so it's understanding intent, and that's a huge leap, which — if we were sitting here two years ago, the idea —

we would never have been able to predict in a short 24 months that it would go from being a great summarizer / researcher of web results,

right,

to actually understanding your intent and then providing a solution and abstracting it all from you.

That's right. That's right.

Hermes Agent 与推理模型的觉醒

Which is a very weird thing. I don't know if you've played with the Hermes agent yet.

Yeah.

Have you played with it yet?

I mean, I asked it just this morning. And I was given a secret Bit Tensor project that has the new um ZAI's uh model 5.2 —

and they gave me —

GLM 5.2. Yeah.

GLM 5.2. So somebody in that Bit Tensor — I think you understand Bit Tensor, you've heard of it — the distributed crypto project. And so they have all this extra capacity. I was told — a whisperer told me — probably some capacity in China that has free energy. Okay, fine. So they gave me unlimited capacity. So I started having to do some really crazy jobs where I was saying, like, "Every hour I want you to tell me what the trends in the world are that nobody else has identified yet. And you can do whatever you want to do that. But my goal is to be the smartest trend hunter in the world."

And I watched what it was doing in the background, and it started debating itself

on where it should find the things.

It said, "Well, we should probably go to Hacker News and Reddit." And then I was like, "Yeah, but there's also social media and trends tend to manifest on Instagram."

That's a reasoning model. You were watching a reasoning model work out.

Yeah. Isn't that interesting? I mean, that's amazing. And it was collapsed. So, as a civilian,

right,

who doesn't hit the uncollapsed moment, and if you were using ChatGPT 3.5 or you were using 4.8, whatever it was, and you haven't used this new level of reasoning and inference and unlimited compute essentially,

right?

It opened my eyes just this morning of what a world

of unlimited tokens might look like,

right? Because unlimited tokens, I believe, means unlimited reasoning.

It does.

What does that mean?

Yeah, it's uh — I mean, if you run these for 25 or 48 hours, you get amazing things now. And what what if by using Cerebras we were 15 times faster, and then you ran it for 24 hours,

right? And you got weeks or months worth of thinking.

Yeah. And it is uh, it is extraordinary. And I think one of the things is people like Ilya and Sam in the early days were saying this was coming.

Right.

Right. And I think when you look back you say to yourself, "Holy crap." Those guys saw it.

Yeah. They could see around the corner.

That's right.

And the rest of us could see like, "What? I'm not sure." It's — when we had Sam on All-In at one point, uh, he said, "Oh, you know, I'd love to come on at some point." I said, "Sure, come on." And he was talking about it. He said —

you know, I said, "What's next?" He said, "Reasoning." I said, "Unpack that. What does it mean?" Well,

understanding what your intent was — just as you're saying — and then figuring out a strategy, and then maybe talking to other agents and other threads about, like, "Is this the right thing to do?" and vetting each other's work. And I'm like, wow, we have come a long way from "guess the next word."

Right, right, fill the sentence in, you know, summarize this PDF.

Now Cerebras is at the center of this because this reasoning is inference.

This reasoning is inference, and it's computationally intensive.

Right.

Right. And so fast compute makes this sort of work fast and sort of tractable. It doesn't — by taking a huge amount of time to get a good answer. And so it's exactly the fact that this reasoning consumes a huge amount of tokens internally that allows a blisteringly fast machine like ours.

那块半亿美元的芯片

And I brought one, because I I'm never far without you know — when one costs half a billion to make, you bring it everywhere with you. Uh, we were tossing this back and forth at Davos.

Uh, what's the model number of this one?

This was in the first eight or 10. Um

so this has a special place.

This has a special place. I mean, my wife says it's like I'm a kid with a dirt bike for his 8th birthday. It was in his bedroom at night. I carry him with me.

I mean, when you have um, you know, your next party at the house, I highly recommend just a little or something — or I —

I think it would be like a great fit. It would be a great bit if you had some.

That's right.

Cerebras 版的摩尔定律

Um, but what we're looking at here is the ability to do that reasoning at scale. And what is Moore's Law for inference and for Cerebras? Do you have something internally you discuss as "we're going to double this every X time period"?

So all chips prior to us in the processor world followed Moore's Law.

Got it.

And we broke —

doubling every 18 months.

Doubling about every 18 months.

Got it.

Um and we crushed it with this chip

and we've carved out a whole new trajectory. And uh my view is in the next 18 months we'll be way over 2x.

Interesting.

And so, uh, I think that early in an architecture, you have room to do much better than what was traditionally Moore's Law. Now, if you've got a 20-year-old architecture like the GPU, it's much harder,

right?

You have to rely on things like smaller geometry,

right? Going to the next fab node.

motivation.
